### Huck — 2016-09-28T18:29:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202025>

I asked my doc about using MCT oil even though I am restricting saturated fats. MCT oil has 14 g of saturated fat per tbsp. She recommended against it.

But I think it should be OK. MCT oil is medium chain fatty acid and is digested differently than long chain. I just got this info from Now who makes the MCT oil I use.

> Our MCT oil is mainly C8 and C10.
> The specifications are as follows:
>
> C8: 65-80%
> C10: 23-33%
> C12: 3% (max)
> C14: 1% (max)​

​

So at most, long chain fatty acids are only 4%, while the other 96% are medium chain. Coconut oil is different, so I will have to contact the manufacturer for specs on their product.

### Huck — 2016-09-28T20:10:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202031>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=202028)
>
> You have to figure what is your problem.
>
> Histamine ---- do not drink alcohol.
>
> pyroluria------ have a drink
>
> ////

Again, it's not food that is causing the histamine problem. Histamine is being created in response to foreign objects in the blood that the liver can't keep up with. Every day I ate a high fat diet the histamine bucket was getting a little fuller, until after 2 to 3 weeks, it overflowed. One 1 occasion eating shrimp was enough to make it overflow and on another occasion it was cod. But I have eaten cod and shrimp for years with no "allergic" type of reaction, so there is no reason to think that I have a problem with histamine in foods - except when my body has already filled up the histamine bucket.

Think of it this way. The liver is a self-cleaning filter, but it has a given capacity. I eat a meal high in saturated fats. Some is digested properly and some isn't. The undigested fat, toxins, bad bacteria, then make it into the lymph system just like the digested fat. Lipid metabolism takes over and stores the "crap" in fat cells. Then the body needs energy so it starts breaking the fat cells down and putting it into the blood stream, releasing the "crap" stored in the fat cell.

The liver gets hit with all this "crap" and it doesn't take long before the liver is overwhelmed and allowing the "crap" to get through. So what should happen now? What is the body's next line of defense?

I believe it is to make histamine to attack the foreign bodies in the blood. Let's say at the next meal, I eat either no or a small amount of saturated fat. The liver gets caught up and the body stops making histamine.

But what happens if I have bacon and 6 eggs for breakfast, oysters for lunch, and a 16 oz steak for dinner, and I repeat this for 2 or 3 weeks? My guess is there is a built-in process that stops the body from overflowing the histamine bucket to fight foreign bodies. So even though the liver can't keep up, at some point the histamine bucket doesn't fill any higher - until I eat a food high in histamine - and that's when it spills over and I have a histamine reaction - eyes swell. Histamine created by body and histamine released from food are apparently 2 different process.

But there is something else happening, coincident with the histamine, that causes my blood pressure and heart rate to spike. It seems like my body goes into flight or fight mode. This is the part I am trying to understand. What process kicks off around dusk that tries to clean up the mess left over by histamine? The body has to have a 2nd line of defense. What is it?

Note: Remember I don't have a scientific background and don't always use the right words. Or I may misunderstand exactly how something works. I am relying on common sense and logic. Kind of like, if I had designed this, what is the simplest way to make it work. Very similar to writing a computer program.

Plus I was a nervous wreck.

So for now, it just means eating a lot of broccoli, tumeric, blueberries, etc, taking the appropriate supplements, and getting plenty of exercise. I am scheduled for a follow-up on blood pressure in 3 weeks, so I will retest then. If it still high, I will consider mold along with any other good ideas are out there. My gut is telling me this is BPH from all the inflammation, so I'll try to shrink it down some. I have a donut cushion I never used. I guess I could put an ice pack in the middle of the hole and then sit on it!

>
> What is your prolactin level?
>
> /////////

I don't think it was ever tested, but I'm not concerned about it now. If my body follows the same pattern as last time, my hormones should normalize over the next few months. By reducing saturated fat, I am greatly reducing stress and inflammation and allowing the body the opportunity to heal. For now my focus is on eating healthy and walking. I will have new labs run in January.

No amount of supplementation improved my steroid hormone levels. In fact, supplementing testosterone and dhea caused urinary and prostate problems. Progesterone cream helped some, so I may start that again. I hope that by reducing saturated fats, lipid metabolism will improve and along with it, the conversion of cholesterol to steroid hormones.

### Huck — 2016-09-30T02:29:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202099>

Something strange happened that raises a serious question.

I decided to do a water fast today. My bp stayed under 125/75 until some time between 1 pm (last time I checked) and 5 pm.
1 pm 124/75 and hr was 72.
5 pm 165/100 and hr was 109.

I also felt chilled, something I don't remember ever experiencing before. Room temp was about 78.

I was due for .1 clonidine, so I took that plus .1 I normally take at bedtime. It takes 45 minutes to an hour for clonidine to work, if it does. I also ate a baked potato. I think a plain potato helps bring down bp a little. I was just trying to stop bp from going higher.

40 minutes after taking clonidine, BP was slightly lower but hr was still over 100.
65 minutes after taking clonidine, BP was down to 145/80, but hr was still over 100.
80 minutes after taking clonidine, BP was down to 97/62 and hr was 85.

So in a short period, BP went up from 124/75 to 165/100 and hr went from 72 to 109

Same type of thing coming down.

In 1 hr and 20 minutes, BP dropped from 165/100 to 97/62 and hr went down from 109 to 85.​

I have no way of knowing if the reading at 5 pm was while BP was going up or coming down. If it was going up, I wouldn't expect the meds to bring it down that far and that quickly, so I'm thinking it either was at its peak or on the way back down.

But that shows there is something beyond food involved. Whatever kicks off late afternoon/early evening, does so whether I eat or fast.

Something I am remembering from the past that might be related. I often feel hot in early evening, like the house has warmed up a couple of degrees. When I check the thermometer, the temperature is what it was supposed to be. I especially notice it in winter when I feel very warm for about 2 hours right around dusk. Same time of day as the bp spikes.

​

### Huck — 2016-09-30T18:51:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202133>

It looks like my hunch was on the right track. This image (from [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circadian_rhythm#Effect_of_light.E2.80.93dark_cycle)) shows highest blood pressure at 18:30 and highest body temperature at 19:00. Adjusting for daylight savings time and time of year, that would put it closer to 5:15 pm than 6:30 pm. I have no idea if highest bp and highest temperature are related, but my best (unscientific) guess is they are.

> [Obesity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Obesity) and [diabetes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diabetes) are associated with lifestyle and genetic factors. Among those factors, **disruption of the circadian clockwork and/or misalignment of the circadian timing system with the external environment** (e.g., light-dark cycle) **might play a role in the development of metabolic disorders.**[\[57\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circadian_rhythm#cite_note-Zelinski2014-57)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202137>

Had another spike - what I'm calling a mini spike.

At 8 am 119/81 hr 68 (took morning med)
at 12 noon 134/91 hr 89 (I knew this was trouble because hr and diastolic go up before systolic) Took noon med
At 12:40 pm 162/93 hr 111
At 1 pm 137/85 hr 99
At 1:10 pm 114/76 hr 89 (HR still a little high, but it always comes down last) So start to finish, about 1 1/2 hours.

I wonder if I had taken the med an hour earlier, if the spike might have been avoided? One dose of Clonidine is usually good for 3 - 6 hours and it was only about 4 hours between doses today. I guess I need to go back to taking more readings. Maybe shoot for every 2 hours.

### Huck — 2016-10-03T15:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202230>

Had another mini spike yesterday evening.

4 pm 102/69 hr 89
6:05 pm 140/110 hr 100 (took .1 mg Clonidine which works in 45 minutes to an hour, was ineffective against bug spikes)
6:10 pm 161/114 hr 108
6:20 pm 158/110 hr 123
6:30 pm 149/112 hr 123
6:45 pm 149/113 hr 115
7:00 pm 135/103 hr 105
7:15 pm 120/86 hr 93

The last 3 spikes were much shorter in duration and systolic only spiked into the 160's. Diastolic and heart rate both peak a little lower. I can usually tell when a spike is starting by a rapid increase in heart rate. I am trying to walk 6 miles a day, so sometime it is hard to tell if heart rate is higher because of exercise or a spike starting.

Got several labs back. Only surprise I have seen so far is histamine.
Histamine Blood 623 range 180-1800 nmol/L

Blood draw was about 2 1/2 hours after spike started to decline, so it is possible histamine had been higher. I don't know what else would cause a 1 time reaction to both shrimp and cod.

### Huck — 2016-10-04T01:21:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202242>

Since I called the shorter bp spikes mini, I guess I just had a micro spike. It only lasted about 15 minutes. BP increased slightly, but I could see my heart rate go up point by point from 80's up to 100 in a matter of seconds. It stayed at 100 for a few minutes and then started coming back down - much slower than going up.

A couple things I did differently today were to take a clonidine about 3 pm and eat a potato about 4 pm. Potatoes are high in potassium and vitamin c. I don't know if this shut down the spike, but I wouldn't have tried it if I didn't think it might work. I also took some magnesium about 3 pm.

I will try to stick to this for a few days and see what happens.

### Huck — 2016-10-04T18:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202273>

I had at least 2 more of the micro spikes last evening. BP only went up a few points, but I watched my heart rate go up from the 80's to 100 in about 10 seconds. It stayed there for a couple of minutes and then came back down.

I recently bought a pulse oximeter that you put on your finger and it measures blood oxidation saturation level and pulse. I know if pulse stays in the 70's or low 80's, bp is likely OK. When I see a pulse rate above 85, I know it might be the beginning of a spike. This way I don't have to measure BP as often to catch the spikes.

### Huck — 2016-10-06T16:57:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202387>

I continue to have these micro spikes. heart rate jumps up to 100, diastolic jumps up to 90, and systolic only goes up a little - 2 - 10 points. They seem to only last for a few minutes. I woke up the other night with my heart beating real fast, so I think they occur while asleep as well.

### Huck — 2016-10-06T17:06:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202388>

Something I learned yesterday, that Jack probably has covered and I didn't get, is that when fasting, your organs shrink. They do this by killing cells. When eating again, new healthy cells are created as the organ grows back to original size.

Dr Rhonda Patrick interviews experts on topics she is interested in. She is very enthusiastic about being as healthy as possible in old age. What makes her videos so good is she acts as an ombudsman for the scientifically challenged. She asks questions in such a way that people with a some scientific knowledge can understand what she is asking. She also paraphrases the information given by the expert in language that is easy to understand. And finally, after the interview, she edits the video to add definitions, references, research papers, etc.

Rhonda interviews Valter Longo, Ph.D. on Fasting=Mimicking Diet & Fasting for Longevity, Cancer & Multiple Sclerosis.

[Fasting, Circadian Rhythms, and Time-Restricted Feeding in Healthy Lifespan](http://www.cell.com/cell-metabolism/pdf/S1550-4131\(16\)30250-9.pdf)

### Huck — 2016-10-12T21:00:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-202695>

In about 3 weeks, PSA dropped from 31 to 10. Still too high, but this kind of drop indicates it was most likely inflammation and a bacteria infection. I think when I was eating a high fat diet, bacteria was getting into the blood stream.

I also did a free PSA test which indicates about a 23% probability of cancer. This is exactly the same as 1 1/2 years ago. Percent free has been roughly the same since 2012.

I see my doc next week and am going to ask her if she is willing to do a PHI lab for me. It is a another prostate cancer screening test that is "3X more specific for prostate cancer (PCa) detection than PSA testing alone". [https://www.accureference.com/phi/](https://www.accureference.com/phi/)

### Huck — 2016-10-21T00:38:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-203136>

Another piece to the puzzle.

I had an appt with my chiropractor today for foot pain. He didn't have any patients waiting for him, so I told him about my blood pressure problem. As soon as I mentioned the SNPs indicating problems digesting and metabolizing fat, he knew exactly what I was talking about. It turns out he is treating a couple of people who have the same SNPs, plus he has them as well. He told me the supplements he recommends, so I know he knows what he is talking about.

Then I explained that I thought my problem was the result of undigested fat getting into the blood stream. He said it is, and tell your doctor you have _**Portal Hypertension**_. Now I've seen 2 cardiologists, my regular doc, doctors in ER, and several other doctors and nurse practitioners, and not one had a clue. Now here's a chiropractor that diagnoses the problem in about 2 minutes. From [WebMD](http://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/digestive-diseases-portal#1)

> Portal [hypertension](http://www.webmd.com/hypertension-high-blood-pressure/default.htm) is an increase in the [blood pressure](http://www.webmd.com/hypertension-high-blood-pressure/guide/diastolic-and-systolic-blood-pressure-know-your-numbers) within a system of veins called the portal venous system. Veins coming from the [stomach](http://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/picture-of-the-stomach), intestine, [spleen](http://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/picture-of-the-spleen), and [pancreas](http://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/picture-of-the-pancreas) merge into the portal vein, which then branches into smaller vessels and travels through the [liver](http://www.webmd.com/digestive-disorders/picture-of-the-liver). If the vessels in the [liver](http://www.webmd.com/hepatitis/rmq-know-your-liver) are blocked due to liver damage, [blood](http://www.webmd.com/heart/anatomy-picture-of-blood) cannot flow properly through the liver. As a result, high pressure in the portal system develops.

My chiro said that undigested food is blocking the smaller blood vessels in the liver which causes my BP to go up. It seems to make sense to me because since I cut down on the amount of fat I eat, I haven't had a major spike in BP.

### Huck — 2016-10-21T16:56:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-203153>

I went for a long walk this morning and out of the blue, another piece of the puzzle came to me.

When your sink is clogged up, you have 3 main options. You can dump some acid down the drain, you can snake it out, or you can use air pressure (either air compressor or plunger). I think the same thing applies to clogged blood vessels in the liver. I could take betaine hcl, digestive enzymes, and ox bile to try to prevent a clog, doctors like to go in and snake out the blood vessels, or the body could use pressure.

So the first thing the body tries is increasing the heart rate. See if pumping more blood through the vessels will break the clog loose. If that doesn't do it, it starts raising diastolic pressure. If that doesn't work, it starts raising systolic pressure.

That would explain the micro, mini, and regular spikes I have seen. If just raising heart rate and diastolic pressure is enough, it may only take a few minutes to clear out the clogs. If that's not enough, then it also raises systolic pressure. I think it does this at specific levels. So first, I get a blood pressure of about 165/95 with a heart rate of 100. If that works, the spike might be only an hour in duration. What is interesting is that BP goes from high to normal in a matter of minutes. I have seen both of these type of spikes since reducing saturated fat in my diet. But when I was on a high fat diet, the clogs were more serious. It took raising bp to 220/120 with a heart rate of 135 over 3 - 6 hours to clear the clogs. It also meant that after several hours of extremely high BP, it would drop to about 160/90 for another hour or 2 before finally going back to normal.

Now this part I haven't quite figured out. I always get really bad heartburn and burping when I get either the mini or the full blown spike. I think what is happening is the undigested fat which was clogging the blood vessels in the liver is being dumped somewhere that results in acid indigestion. If anyone has an idea on where the undigested fat might be dumped, please post it here.

I'm also thinking that what the doctors are doing is completely wrong. They give me several different meds to try to bring down my blood pressure. I think that defeats the purpose and also explains why more meds is so ineffective. Until the body is able to clear out the clogged blood vessels, my BP is going to stay high, and it will return to normal either with or without the additional meds.

Since Thursday, I have been cycling through mini spikes every few hours. They only last about an hour, but they are wearing me out. Also been getting dizzy when I stand up and have had a bad headache the last couple of days. ### Huck — 2016-11-03T13:32:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-203557>

Finally had my appt with hypertension specialist. It was a bust. Like every other doctor, he says I am under-medicated. I patiently explained that my BP is within normal range at least 90% of the time, so how could I be under-medicated?

He was completely rote. No matter how many times I explained that BP returns to normal after a spike both with and without any additional meds, he continued to insist I needed more meds.

I came close to just walking out until he said there were some labs he could run. I decided to do the labs and see if there is any new information that might help. He is a kidney specialist and that's where he is looking. I think the problem is more likely in the liver.

I had a mini spike while I was at the dr office. BP jumped up to 180/110 with a hr of 120. I had my meter with me and confirmed this reading on my meter. When I got home about an hour later, BP was 125/85 and that was without taking any additional meds. The spike was about 3 hours in length from start to finish - normal to high and back to normal. (This wasn't white coat hypertension).

### Huck — 2016-11-28T20:22:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-204439>

My BP has been near perfect for the last couple of weeks. I thought I finally had it licked.

This morning, I had to go to doctor's office for blood work. Checked my BP before I left the house and it was the highest it has been in at least 2 months - 175/95. Recently it has been running under 120/75.

After the blood draw, I asked the nurse to check my BP. First reading was 155/90. That told me it was already on the way down. She checked it again within 3 minutes. It was down to 135/85. Then she had me stand up and checked it again - 120/80.

(ETA: After I got back home, BP continued to come down)

So in about 5 minutes, BP dropped from 155/90 to 120/80. I explained to the nurse that this was the exact problem I was seeing the doctor for. My BP goes from normal to over 200/100 in an hour or 2, stays there for a few hours, and then drops back to normal over 2 or 3 hours. More recently, instead of these major spikes, I have smaller ones that go up to about 175/100, then drop right back down to normal in a matter of minutes. The entire spike cycle lasts about an hour.

I actually tested to see if taking extra meds during the spike helped and it didn't seem to make any difference in peak or length of spike. The one effect it did have was to drive my BP too low after the spike was over, so I usually don't take extra meds.

I didn't think I had white coat syndrome, but 2 of my last 3 trips to doctor's office resulted in a BP spike and the other trip resulted in urinary retention. The retention problem was at 8 am when I was having a kidney/bladder ultrasound and had to have a full bladder, so I had to drive for over an hour starting at 6 am with a full bladder and then hold it for about 45 minutes after I got to imaging center, so about 2 hours with full bladder first thing in morning.

Maybe the best thing I can do is just quit going to the doctor!

ETA: I think the BP problem is possibly a liver problem, but I can't get any doctor to actually look there. I did a little research and it looks like Choline and Inositol may help, so I started taking them about 2 weeks ago. Except for this morning, my BP has been very stable and usually under 120/80 since I started taking them.

One other thing I noticed is my heart rate is lower. Most readings have been in 60's or low 70's, with some readings in the 50's. I think what is happening is the choline is enhancing the BP meds.

### Huck — 2016-11-29T02:42:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-204456>

I'm taking Solgar Phosphatidyl Choline and is made from soy lecithin. My doctor recommended it.

It was also the highest rated PC on Amazon, a little higher than BodyBio. If it stops working, I will think about switching.

Like I said, this by itself didn't seem to make much, if any, difference. When I did some research, I came across this.

> (NewsTarget) Choline is an important nutrient for so many bodily functions, but it is particularly helpful in **keeping the liver healthy**. Without this B vitamin, **fats can become trapped inside the liver**. There, **those fats can block the metabolism, resulting in a build-up of fat that can rise to dangerous levels. This accumulation of fat can cause kidney disorders, jaundice, high cholesterol, and atherosclerosis.** People with a deficiency of choline may find themselves with memory problems, as well. [Choline Restores Liver health](http://www.naturalnews.com/031108_choline_liver_health.html)

This fit with my theory that fat was blocking blood vessels in my liver and choline might be the answer. When I started looking at supplements, I found that choline was usually packaged with inositol. So I researched Inositol and this is an example of what I found.

> **Inositol helps move fat out of the liver, and may help prevent liver disorders including those involving high levels of cholesterol**. Serotonin and acetylcholine (neurotransmitters) rely on inositol and is linked to reduction in depression and panic attacks. Often the two supplements are used with prescription drugs to treat schizophrenia and other mental health disease.
>
> Studies on brain waves show a similar effect as Librium or Valium. Since it has a calming effect it **can lower blood pressure**.
>
> Choline is essential for the production of acetylcholine which helps nerve cells communicate. It can positively effect memory and mood. Patients with Alzheimer’s often supplement with choline in combination with other drugs for positive effect. **Like inositol, choline helps with liver function and elevated cholesterol levels.** [Choline and Inositol](http://www.brainphysics.com/articles/mental-health/choline-and-inositol)

So my doc thought phosphatidyl Choline would help remove fat from my liver, but it seems the combination of choline and Inositol is more effective. Plus as an added benefit, it is also supposed to reduce LDL and lower blood pressure. Inositol seems to act like a milder form of niacin.

I'm getting new labs done in about 5 weeks, so I should know then if this is working.

Jan, if you were having severe blood pressure spikes at your age, would your 1st priority be your overall health or stopping the spikes?

I am going on gut logic. I don't have the scientific background to understand most scientific/medical articles, so I pretend it is my job to design whatever it is I am trying to understand. In this case, I looked up the functions the liver performs, and then I designed a liver to perform them. Then I looked to see where there might be problems and how the liver could repair itself. Then I looked for what I could do to help the liver (as I designed it). If fat is gumming up the liver, how do I clean it out and prevent it from happening again? That's what led me to choline and inositol.

I'm hoping to take a long trip in January, but it is dependent on keeping my bp under control. For the previous 2 years, my worst spikes started in late December and lasted until late March. The only things I have found that help are minimizing saturated fat in diet, taking digestive enzymes and ox bile to digest fats, and taking supplements that help liver function. For the last 2+ years, doctors have been of absolutely no help, so experimenting seems my only choice.

My trip is to go house hunting in the gulf south. I plan on staying in campgrounds on or near the gulf and eating lots of seafood. I bought a small trailer 3 years ago for just this reason, but the BP problem caused me to cancel my trip the last 2 winters.

If things work out as I hope, I will find the area I want to move to and then come back and put my house up for sale. With luck, I will move to subtropical Louisiana sometime next year.

### Huck — 2016-12-10T23:40:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-205118>

I had to stop the Choline/Inositol. My heart rate dropped about 10 points which was a problem because my BP med already slows my hr. I started having very vivid dreams, one after another, all night long. I also woke up in the middle of the night, on several occasions, shaking and feeling "nervous", and I was waking up around 3 am and couldn't fall back to sleep.

It took about 2 days for the effect to wear off. I was taking a very low dose of both (250 mg/250 mg), so I have no idea why this happened. The only thing I can think of is it might have been amplifying the effect of my bp med.

I posted about him a month ago on a different thread, but it looks like that thread disappeared. Same thread where I posted video by Dr Rhonda Patrick.

I have been using fasting for weight loss for the last 6 months. I also use it whenever my BP starts creeping back up. It only takes a day or 2 at the most to drop BP back to low normal. As long as I limit saturated fat, sugar, artificial sugars, and flour, BP stays in range; but too much pumpkin pie or pecan pie sends it through the roof. Fasting is the only thing I have found that brings it down quickly - something drugs won't do.

I am about 30 hours into a fast right now. Plan on fasting until New Year's day. Dr Fung says most of his patients prefer every other day (eod) fasting, but I find 2 or 4 day fasts a couple of times a month to be the easiest for me.

I think his 1st book, The Obesity Code, had better information than his fasting book, but there is very little information in his books that is not on his website. The problem with the website is finding all the related material and associated videos. For you, the website is probably the best way. For others who have less knowledge, his books are probably an easier approach.

Dr Fung recommends a low carb, high fat diet and fasting. He really doesn't get into any medical conditions for which a high fat diet might not be the best solution.

I don't have answers to your questions. I'm having blood work done next week, so hopefully that will tell me if the changes I made are helping. Unfortunately, when the cold weather came back in mid-November, my hunger went through the roof and I have eaten too much over the last 6 weeks. I have only managed a 2 day fast in early December and now my current fast.

I'm taking supplements to lower cholesterol and I think that the supplements and lower saturated fat in diet should help. I'm also having a [PHI lab](http://prostatehealthindex.us/2014/02/19/the-benefits-of-phi/) (prostate - covered by medicare). I've been taking pygeum to see if it would help reduce BPH/prostatitus.

> **The benefits of _phi_**
> The Prostate Health Index (_phi_) is a new combination of 3 blood tests that results in a score, or “_phi_ score.” This score gives you more accurate information on what an elevated PSA level might mean and the probability of finding cancer on biopsy.\*
>
> **_phi_ is**
>
> -   3x more specific for prostate cancer (PCa) detection than PSA testing alone1
> -   The only multi-analyte PCa blood test with FDA approval2
> -   The only multi-analyte blood test recommended in the National Comprehensive Network (NCCN) Guidelines for PCa Early Dectection

I sometimes have bulletproof coffee if hunger starts to get out of control. Another trick that for some reason works for me, is a baked potato. It almost always kills my hunger for the rest of the day and usually brings my bp down. It might be the combination of magnesium and potassium that does it. I often have breakfast and then a baked potato about 2 pm and that satisfies me for the rest of the day.

Theoretical musings...
I have not read much of dr Fung's writings yet,
but it looks to me that longer fasts may overall be easier,
unless one thinks of eating (enough) once a day most of the time.
The idea is about time that it takes to empty liver of sugar.
I think he says 2 days, but it looks to me that it is more like 4 days.
After 4 days of fasting, hunger is supposed to subside and there is plenty of energy.
So the big deal is mostly about getting thru the first few days.
If that is correct, then one would like to go thru this projects less often.
OTOH,
if one lingers over not eating enough but eating enough just to raise insulin,
he may end up with all bad stuff only and no benefits:
hunger
low energy
high insulin
high body fat

water with sugar substitute will raise insulin.
Body fat can be used for energy **only when insulin is low.**

### Huck — 2016-12-31T00:10:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-205936>

There are some other studies that show if calories are reduced enough, you still get most of the benefits of fasting. I think it was 300-500 calories a day. There is also something called fast mimicking diet, but it is fairly complicated in what you have to eat.

Dr Fung says go with whatever works for you. Longer fasts are better and have quicker results, but the important thing is to go with a fasting plan that you are comfortable with. One of the nice features is you can enjoy holidaysand have a piece of birthday cake as long as you use moderation and follow up with fasting.

### Huck — 2017-01-05T16:45:08+0200

I have had back problems for about 40 years. My ilium gets cocked so that 1 side is higher than the other. It makes one leg about 1" shorter than the other. It takes a good chiropractor about a minute to pop it back into place. It appears any inflammation caused by diet will aggravate my sacroiliac.

I had really bad foot pain a few months back. X-ray was negative. I thought I would try my chiropractor. He adjusted my sacroiliac and pain was gone in about a day.

So today I was thinking, I wonder if my back could also be causing my prostatitis? I get discomfort in the back of my legs just below my butt. I thought this was likely from an inflamed prostate that had increased in size. I still think that, but I'm wondering if the inflammation might be caused by my sacroiliac being out of alignment.

I'm going to ask my doctor about it today, but I think I will also try an experiment. When I'm having the discomfort I will schedule a chiropractic adjustment and see if the discomfort lessens or even goes away.

### JanSz — 2017-01-05T17:02:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-206161>

Also orthotic shoes may help.
\---
I had my problems with metatarsal bones at one time.
Spend $500, got shoe inserts, problem went away.
I mean pain problem.
Those inserts would not fit my dancing shoes.
So I found something for $19 that worked, actually worked even better.
It was similar to the picture below, but much better, solid plastic, last forever.
That lasted couple years, and one time I got stuck with another chiropractor.
Actually as a chiropractor (spine) he was not that great, but when he noted my inserts he went for my feet, shook them up, voila, I do not need any inserts.
Latter I asked my steady chiropractor, why he works only on my spine, how about feet.
Well, because I newer asked.
So my best chiro, have his limitations.
But since then, once a week he also works on my feet, problem or no problem.

I'm 6' and currently about 235. Cold and cloudy weather makes me extremely hungry and I keep bouncing between 230 and 240. I'm hoping that this Spring I can get the rest of the weight off. I had a net loss of about 40 lbs for 2016. 220 gets me to overweight instead of obese, so that is my top range for goal weight. 200 is probably a good weight for me, so I plan on getting to 200 - 220 by next summer.

My back problem started after I had lost a lot of weight. I reached down to pick up my gym bag and my back spasmed. Since then I don't see any correlation between weight and back pain. It can happen at any weight. If anything, it is related to foods. Anything that causes inflammation is likely to cause back pain. That's why I'm thinking food might be causing the inflammation that causes my back problem which in turn causes the prostate to become inflamed.

I used to wear orthopedic shoes when I taught a lot and now wear walking shoes all the time. Anything with a heal seems to cause a problem.

### Huck — 2017-01-20T17:32:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-207148>

Just watched a video that gave me an idea. Can cravings be stopped by other means than food? I think the answer is a big yes. Exercise can help. Keeping busy can help. A stronger emotion can help.

I'm thinking that thinking can stop cravings. Just have to break the association between cravings and food. Maybe learn to substitute walking for food - get a craving, walk a mile. Rewire the brain.

Often we know things, but never make the association between what we know and what we actually do. I'm going to see how many associations I can define that I want to change. Then use meditation and NLP techniques to change them.

I made an important diet decision last week and did great for a few days. Felt much better, very motivated, relaxed, improved energy. Then my Amazon order came and I had to try everything at once. Need to get back to more fruits and vegetables and less nuts and seeds. I don't know the upper limit for saturated fats I can handle, but I have read 15 g/day is a good target.

I understand that saturated fat in coconut is handled differently by the body that is saturated fat in meat, but I still don't know if that means I can handle it any better. I guess my best bet is to just use it sparingly for cooking.

### Huck — 2017-01-25T18:22:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-207341>

Been watching a lot of videos and it seems to me a lot of very knowledgeable people are becoming vegans. At least for the 1st few years, they seem to lose weight and become much healthier. Even the ones that go on a high starch diet see good results.

So I'm thinking that early man must have been on several different diets depending on season and location. In this regard, Paleo or low carb are probably not much better than vegan or high carb. Paleo takes all the food types early man ate throughout the year, and says you can eat them on a daily basis.

In northern hemisphere, one likely didn't eat much starch in late spring, but did in mid-summer into fall and even winter. Greens and berries in Spring and Summer, vegetables and fruits in Summer and Fall, and then foods that stored well in the winter. A small amount of fish and meat when available.

So if we eat what is in season, we eat a low carb, near vegan diet for a few months, followed by a higher starch diet of mainly fruits and vegetables, and then we add nuts and seeds and fats. That is supplemented with whatever fish, seafood, and meat is available.

Are we doing damage by eating high protein and high fat on a daily basis? By alternating diets, wouldn't we be correcting imbalances caused by too much of some category of food? Does our digestive system change based on the seasons? Don't we need fruits and vegetables to make digestive enzymes? As the seasons change, don't the in-season fruits and vegetables create different enzymes?

I'm going to try a high starch, mostly vegetarian diet for 2 or 3 months, and then move to a lower starch diet over the summer and see what happens. A small amount of fish or seafood 1 or 2 times a week. Maybe some meat. I will also do some intermittent fasting and 2 and 3 day fasts.

I plan on running labs in April so that should tell me how the change in diet is working. I also want to see if reducing protein will get rid of excess skin.

In Polish language there is a word that describes lean period.
Przednówek
[https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Przednówek](https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Przedn%C3%B3wek)

it is time between end of last years food
till
time when new food is available
we are talking about multiple lean months
specially when last year's food got rotten or eaten by vermin and this year is dry
and you happens to live at high elevation where the summer is shorter and winter longer.
....

For some reason, I couldn't get the link to work.

> The link you followed may have expired, or the page may only be visible to an audience you're not in.

I do know that cancer feeds on glucose; but as best I know, I have prostatitis and bph, but not cancer.

The biggest problem I have is that eating saturated fat (I haven't figured out the threshold amount) causes my bp to spike to stroke levels. The only thing I have found that keeps it down is to greatly restrict saturated fat and to eat a potato every day. I'm not talking about a drop of 20 systolic points, but 100 or more. When I eat too much saturated fat, BP can spike to at least 220/130 with hr jumping to 135. By restricting saturated fat and eating a potato, BP stays within 10 points of 120/80 all day (with reduced meds).

I realize this is a mitochondria and redox problem, but I have been unsuccessful with a ketogenic diet or any diet high in saturated fat. I'm hoping that a diet high in starch will help me lose 20 - 30 lbs and also increase production of digestive enzymes. What I will be trying is very close to the Mediterranean diet. I will still consume healthy fats (fish and olive oil). My labs should indicate whether this was a good or bad choice for me and I will adjust accordingly.

The only time I have seem a substantial improvement in my labs was when I thought I had cancer and greatly increased non-starchy vegetables and reduced red meat. Even testosterone and dhea showed great improvement and I was able to stop my bp meds. For whatever reason, I have been unable to stick to that type of diet again - maybe because I have not tried it during the right season for it.

So my plan is to go high starch for 2 to 3 months and lose at least 20 lbs, then to reduce starch and increase non-starch vegetables in Spring into Summer. Based on how I do, I will decide what works best for me for fall and winter.

wow, that sucks - weirdly, the Costco Connection had this whole article on beets which contain betaine - I think that's different than betaine HCL and they act on the NOS system - basically enlarging/dilating your veins so your blood pressure drops - maybe if you're going to do a carb, make it beets instead of potato?

### Huck — 2017-01-28T03:08:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-207463>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=207461)
>
> wow, that sucks - weirdly, the Costco Connection had this whole article on beets which contain betaine - I think that's different than betaine HCL and they act on the NOS system - basically enlarging/dilating your veins so your blood pressure drops - maybe if you're going to do a carb, make it beets instead of potato?
>
> [http://www.drdavidwilliams.com/beet-high-blood-pressure/](http://www.drdavidwilliams.com/beet-high-blood-pressure/)

Betaine hcl is to supplement stomach acid (hcl - hydrochloric acid).

I'm not sure why potatoes work, but they do. It might be the combination of magnesium and potassium, plus I love potatoes and hate beets. Potatoes don't help if I am eating a high fat diet. I did recently try a beet supplement, but it didn't help.

I've only been doing my carb diet for about 4 days and already had to cut back on BP meds. I was getting very lightheaded from BP going too low. Heart rate is also too low now, so hopefully will be able to cut back even more on meds sometime next week.

I wonder if it is the combination of starch and fats that make so many of us fat? People that eat fat and cut out starches lose weight and so do people that cut out fat and eat starches. That's why I'm thinking it makes sense for me to try alternating between these 2 diet plans.

I either have to stay on a very low fat diet or rebuild my digestive system to the point my liver isn't being overwhelmed with undigested fats. That's why I am doing a high starch diet now. Hopefully the vegetables and especially the fiber will rebuild my microbiome.

The article is based on the conventional understanding of blood pressure, which really doesn't make sense. BP meds, at best, mask the cause, resulting in more and more meds over time. Just as doctors wrongly prescribe statins for cholesterol, they prescribe diuretics and other meds for high BP, without ever identifying the cause.

### JanSz — 2017-01-28T03:32:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-207464>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=207461)
>
> wow, that sucks - weirdly, the Costco Connection had this whol**e article on beets which contain betaine - I think that's different than betaine HCL** and they act on the NOS system - basically enlarging/dilating your veins so your blood pressure drops - maybe if you're going to do a carb, make it beets instead of potato?
>
> [http://www.drdavidwilliams.com/beet-high-blood-pressure/](http://www.drdavidwilliams.com/beet-high-blood-pressure/)

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trimethylglycine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trimethylglycine)
**Trimethylglycine** (**TMG**) is an [amino acid derivative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Amino_acid_derivatives) that occurs in plants. Trimethylglycine was the first [betaine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betaine) discovered; originally it was simply called **betaine** because, in the 19th century, it was discovered in [sugar beets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sugar_beet).[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trimethylglycine#cite_note-Ullmann-2) Since then, many other betaines have been discovered, and the more specific name _glycine betaine_ distinguishes this one.
\========================================
On the blue chart lower right
there is **TMG=betaine**

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Biological function

TMG is an important cofactor in methylation, a process that occurs in every cell of mammals to synthesize and donate methyl groups (CH3) for other processes in the body. These processes include the synthesis of neurotransmitters such as dopamine and serotonin. Methylation is also required for the biosynthesis of melatonin and the electron transport chain constituent coenzyme Q10.

The major step in the methylation cycle is the remethylation of homocysteine, a compound which is naturally generated during deamination of the essential amino acid methionine. Despite its natural formation, homocysteine has been linked to inflammation, depression, specific forms of dementia, and various types of vascular disease. The remethylation process that detoxifies homocysteine and converts it back to methionine can occur via either of two pathways. The major pathway involves the enzyme methionine synthase, which requires vitamin B12 as a cofactor, and also depends indirectly on folate and other B vitamins. The minor pathway involves betaine-homocysteine methyltransferase and requires TMG as a cofactor. Betaine is thus involved in the synthesis of many biologically important molecules, and may be even more important in situations where the major pathway for the regeneration of methionine from homocysteine has been compromised by genetic polymorphisms such as mutations in the BHMT gene.

> **Trimethylglycine** (**TMG**) is an [amino acid derivative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category:Amino_acid_derivatives) that occurs in plants. Trimethylglycine was the first [betaine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Betaine) discovered; originally it was simply called **betaine** because, in the 19th century, it was discovered in [sugar beets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sugar_beet).[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trimethylglycine#cite_note-Ullmann-2) Since then, many other betaines have been discovered, and the more specific name _glycine betaine_ distinguishes this one.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturated\_fat#Examples\_of\_saturated\_fatty\_acids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saturated_fat#Examples_of_saturated_fatty_acids)

**Examples of saturated fatty acids**
Main article: [List of saturated fatty acids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_saturated_fatty_acids)
Some common examples of fatty acids:

-   [Butyric acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butyric_acid) with 4 carbon atoms (contained in [butter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Butter))
-   [Lauric acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lauric_acid) with 12 carbon atoms (contained in [coconut oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coconut_oil), [palm kernel oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palm_kernel_oil), and [breast milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breast_milk))
-   [Myristic acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myristic_acid) with 14 carbon atoms (contained in cow's [milk](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milk) and dairy products)
-   [Palmitic acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palmitic_acid) with 16 carbon atoms (contained in [palm oil](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palm_oil) and [meat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meat))
-   [Stearic acid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stearic_acid) with 18 carbon atoms (also contained in [meat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meat) and [cocoa butter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cocoa_butter))

### Huck — 2017-01-30T21:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-207592>

> [Penny said:](/goto/post?id=207584)
>
> Exactly Forgot about the beta-glucoronidase and estrogen link Janz - thanks Huck, maybe you can't fat burn and thus you are reverting to carbs - like so many of us with crap-chondria...

Since moving to a high starch diet about 5 days ago, I have been completely satisfied with no cravings and have been losing weight. I'm sleeping better and can tell inflammation is reduced. In just a few days, I have had to cut 1 bp med by half and the other about 15%, because BP dropped too low.

I found 1 reference that indicated fat interferes with carbohydrate metabolism. In my case, I can either eat high carb or high fat; but if I eat both, I gain a great deal of weight. I've tried a high fat low carb diet for the last 15 years and only managed to lose about 50 lbs before hitting a plateau. I spent 14 years unsuccessfully trying to break through the plateau with little success.. When I reduced fat, especially saturated fat, I finally broke through and lost an additional 35 lb. The challenge now is to see if I can lose the remaining 30 lbs over the next few months.

I think hflc diets were the exception in human history, not the norm. Just look at the Kitavan. History is full of societies that ate a high carb diet and were extremely healthy. Meat was eaten infrequently and in small portions. If we look at the Mediterranean diet, same thing. I'm guessing some of us have a genetic makeup that favors a high fat diet and others a high carb diet. Then there are those fortunate ones that can eat both! I guess if we can ever reach the optimized state, we might be able to eat both.

I started doing research on saturated fats yesterday, but haven't had much time to spend on it yet. I did find a statement from Nathan Pritikin (Pritikin Diet) that claimed fats interfered with carbohydrate metabolism. It wasn't footnoted and I wasn't able to find a source to confirm it.

I do have my genetic analysis that indicates I have trouble digesting and metabolizing fats (especially saturated). I also know that taking supplements to help with digesting fats didn't make any difference. I also know from experimentation that even coconut oil (medium chain fatty acid) caused my BP to spike. That threw a wrench in things because mct is digested differently than other saturated fats.

I doubt if checking the blood for fatty acids would tell me anything important. I believe the fats are not being digested and metabolized, but dumped directly into the blood stream. I doubt if they are identifiable as fatty acids.

### Huck — 2017-02-05T21:58:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-208022>

So far, so good. It's only been about 10 days and I have been able to stop 1 BP med completely and reduce the other by 33%. I checked FBG the last 3 mornings and it ranged from high 60's to mid 70's, which is much lower than I was expecting as I am eating a high carb diet.

I had sleep problems but hopefully that is over. Last night was very restful. My energy level was also much better today. Also noticed my eyesight is better today.

Most days I don't eat meat or fish, so I take fish oil and sublingual B12 on those days. Other days, I eat a small portion of seafood or turkey. I have lost a few pounds, but not quite as much as I expected.

I'm leary of trying nicotine. In the past, I had a love affair with the nicotine patch. The 1st time I tried it, I felt better than I had felt in at least 20 years. The effect diminished quickly over time, but it reminded me of taking a diet pill to stay awake all night to cram for an exam. Same effect on me. I only took the diet pill 1 time because I was afraid I would quickly grow dependent on it. I felt normal!

I will give low dose aspirin a try. I'm also heading out soon on a month long trip, camping (trailer) at as many beaches as possible as I head for Louisiana. I plan on doing lots of walking and grounding as I follow the sun.

### Huck — 2017-02-27T16:30:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-209371>

I was going along fine until about 2 weeks ago. BP suddenly spiked again. I would get it down for a day and then it would spike again.

I had already postponed my trip South once because I cut chunk off my thumb, and it's hard to do things like put the key in the ignition with your thumb all wrapped up. I waited a week and took off. Thumb is still painful and wrapped, but at least I can function. BP was still spiking, but part of the reason for the trip was to see if I noticed health improvements when I got near the I-10 corridor.

Last Saturday was absolutely the worst BP readings of the trip and I was about 40 miles N of I-10. I had tried to add whole wheat bread back into diet and I think that added to the problem. My Sat morning BP was 182/119 with a HR of 101. (This 2 week episode has been different. HR stays high, Diastolic stays high, and Systolic seems to respond to meds.)

I thought I might be dehydrated because urine always seemed to be dark. I drank more water, cranberry juice, and ate a huge amount of fruits and vegetables. I started seeing improvements on Sunday. I arrived at my new campsite about 50 miles south of I-10 in the middle of a swamp and went for a walk and then grounded for an hour.

This morning (Monday), BP was 110/78 with a HR of 72. That was before my meds. About an hour after taking meds, BP was 95/72.

About 5 days ago, something else happened that may be significant. I suddenly felt like I had cuts inside my mouth and had a few spots on my lips that got hard and sensitive. Probably coincidence, but I had used a natural mosquito repellent for the first time and got some on my lips and possibly on some food. I thought that might have been what caused the mouth problems.

The mouth is much better this morning and BP is too. Coincidence? I did take an antibiotic yesterday, because it seemed like I had an infection I couldn't shake. In addition to the mouth problem, I had a sore spot on the back of the hand with the sliced thumb. The soreness on the hand is finally gone and the thumb is much less painful. I had tried increasing vitamin C, but that didn't seem to help.

No mosquitoes so far. I think it was in GA where the bugs were bad.

I'll only be here for a couple of weeks total. On Wednesday, I head to a small island just off the coast of LA for a few days. Then back here to look at a few houses just to get an idea of what's available. I need to get back home to get my house ready to sell.

The campground I am in is called Palmetto Island and there are Palmetto plants everywhere. Their branches fan out like big hands, so I high five them when I go for a hike. I wonder if there is a benefit (for men) just breathing the air around so many Palmettos?

### Huck — 2017-03-12T16:20:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-210188>

Back home after wonderful trip. My eyes were opened to so many things - wish I understood them all!

I woke up with my heart racing on at least 2 nights. This has happened a few times at home as well. I thought about what was different on these 2 nights on the trip. Bingo - I was using my space heater those 2 nights. The a/c power comes into the trailer right under where my head is when I sleep. From then on, I removed the shore power cable before I went to bed and reversed my sleeping position so that my feet were over the power. Never had the racing heart problem again and sleep improved. (If I would just listen to Jack!!!)

My body also seemed to react better to food. My body started getting hot at night while I slept, something others have posted about but I never experienced. If I ate too much or ate something I couldn't digest well, it was eliminated the next morning.

BP readings were all over the place. I had extremely high readings and very normal readings. I think the power problem also contributed to high BP, but can't confirm it. On a few occasions, I think the high BP was a result of eating too much saturated fat (accidentally because I didn't check fat content of some things I ate).

On trip home, I packed several baggies with celery, grapes, and carrots, and snacked on them all day while driving. I also had apples and bananas. I tried to eat 9 servings of fruits and vegetables every day. I checked BP the 1st morning after I returned home and BP was normal before taking any meds. BP stayed low all day and I had to reduce meds from what I was taking on trip. Same thing this morning, except morning BP was even lower. The only change I have made at home was to put all electrical I use in bedroom on a power strip. When I go to bed, I turn it off.

I also lost 5+ lbs during my 21 day trip!

I am going to continue to try to eat 9 servings of fruit and vegetables every day. I've been eating an oat cereal with a banana every morning, and I find it filling and satisfying. I'm also taking fish oil and a methyl-B12 lozenge every day, and eating oysters every day or 2. I'm also going to try making my own sourdough bread and see if it is the high saturated fat and other ingredients in store bought bread that causes a problem.

> you used only 2 Potassium tablets and drink spring water as need?
> if yes
> you may be low in potassium
>
> /
>
> .

Potassium by itself hasn't resulted in lowered BP. I'm pretty sure that it was from lowered blood viscosity. I tried the same thing today and it helps for a few hours.

That's also why grounding helps. Next on my list is to see if donating blood helps and for how long.

### Huck — 2017-03-18T12:48:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-210578>

[http://www.mirror.co.uk/science/proof-modern-life-really-kill-10047382](http://www.mirror.co.uk/science/proof-modern-life-really-kill-10047382)

> A new study estimates that an 80-year-old from the Tsimane has the same vascular age as an American in their mid-fifties.
>
> Heart rate, [blood pressure](http://www.mirror.co.uk/all-about/blood-pressure) , cholesterol, and blood glucose were also much lower, probably as a result of the tribe's lifestyle, according to the researchers.
>
> The indigenous Tsimane people, who live in the Bolivian Amazon, have the lowest reported levels of vascular ageing for any population.
>
> Hardening of the arteries, known as coronary atherosclerosis, which leads to coronary heart disease and angina is FIVE TIMES less common than in the US, according to the research published in The Lancet.

> Their diet is largely carbohydrate-based (72 per cent) and includes non-processed carbs which are high in fibre such as rice, plantain, corn, nuts and fruits.
>
> Protein constitutes 14 per cent of their diet and comes from animal meat. The diet is very low in fat with fat compromising only 14 per cent of the diet - equivalent to an estimated 38 grams of fat each day, including 11g saturated fat and no trans fats.

\========================================

I do not like much when in discussions on health the word "carbohydrate" is used.
Usually that clouds the issue.
Indeed, one of the most important distinctions is processed vs non-processed.
Even if adjusted for processed vs non-processed, there are other distinctions.
Recently I learned that one of the distinctions in wheat is in the type of fiber.
The outer shell of grain.
Ancient grain had it soft
current is hard, abrasive, it can scratch and injure intestinal wall.
Not to mention that current wheat grass can grow and be green long beyond its harvesting time.
Shortly before planned harvest it is soaked in Roundup, dies, dries up, ready to be cut down.
Did I heard Roundup--->glyphosate
Wheat is just one item in headlines.
Monsanto is rather busy, what they did to the other food plants?

///

### Inger — 2017-03-19T11:20:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-210650>

What is your source of saturated fat?
If you eat low quality fat from cattle fed grains, hormones, antibiotics.. roundup.. who knows what.. it will all accumulate in the fat... and in you!

Might be your body is reacting to that... not to the fat per se.

You might want to try out real, healthy fat from cattle sans any added chemicals. It is not easy to find but you can find it if you look for it.

It needs to be 100% grass fed and grass finished, no grains.

I would test it, do only high quality saturated fats and see what happens.
The same with eggs.
If you are not healthy, I think it is important to look for clean fat sources, as your body´s Redox is low anyways and it has a hard time to get rid of the chemicals.

I could also try raw fat (the good quality one) instead of cooked .
Might make a difference

### Huck — 2017-05-07T20:19:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-213610>

I have had a dull headache just about every day for the last couple of months. I didn't have it on the trip, and some days I am fine, but most days have the headache.

A few months ago I stopped using the electrical outlet behind the bed and moved all the power cords to a wall outlet several feet from the bed. In addition to the headache, I have woken up several nights where it feels like my body is vibrating. I looked it up on Internet and found many others having the same problem. Many described it as feeling like they have their cell phone on vibrate. Doctors don't seem to have a clue what is causing it.

So last night, I put my finger heart rate tester on the night stand, so I could check my heart rate the next time it happened. I had a mild episode about 4 am this morning. Heart rate was slightly elevated (71). I rechecked 5 minutes later and it was 61. The one difference I was aware of was my position on the bed.

I got my trimeter and starting checking around the bed. Everything seemed OK, until I measured directly over the electrical outlet. An inch or 2 to either side and there was hardly a spike, but right over the outlet magnetic 0-100 and 0-3 were all the way to the right. Electric was high as well.

Several other outlets in the house were also bad, but others seemed OK. I turned off breakers to identify which breakers turned off the "bad" outlets. What is interesting is that after turning the breaker off and on, the magnetic level is much lower.

Anyone know what might cause that to happen? I'm guessing a bad ground. I'll test them for grounding if I can find my tester.

The way I sleep, I end up with my head right in line with the electrical outlet and about 6" from the wall. I can turn off the breaker for now because we are having a cool spell, but my house uses 2 window air conditioners so the breaker needs to be on during the summer. I thought about moving to the other bedroom, but same exact problem. One outlet is bad and it is right where the only place for a bed is.

If you live in single family home, you may be able to figure out your ground.
In my house for the ground I got about 1/4" round rod barely in the ground and barely attached.
That ground was still good enough to make few small GFCI work for 50+ years.
Problems started when I was trying to make 100A GFCI work with my spa.
Look here:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-5#post-201902](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/page-5#post-201902)
for ideas of what to buy and how to sink it in ground.

### Huck — 2017-05-08T01:21:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-213624>

I found what was causing the problem.

I rechecked the outlet and magnetic had dropped to very low level. I thought back to this morning and the only difference was I had the light on.

I was using a full spectrum light bulb and that's what caused the problem. It was plugged into a different electrical outlet, but on the same circuit. I replaced it with an incandescent bulb and it measures OK now.

### Huck — 2017-06-07T16:21:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-215565>

Some links to prostate cancer studies courtesy of [FoundMyFitness](https://www.foundmyfitness.com/news) ([Dr Rhonda Patrick](https://www.foundmyfitness.com/)).

**[Starving prostate cancer with what you eat: Apple peels, red grapes, turmeric](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2017/06/170606112750.htm)**

> New research from The University of Texas at Austin identifies several natural compounds found in food, including turmeric, apple peels and red grapes, as key ingredients that could thwart the growth of prostate cancer, the most common cancer afflicting U.S.
> men.

**[Study shows 60mg of sulforaphane slows doubling time of PSA in cancer patients by 86%.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zz4YVJ4aRfg&t=00h06m06s)**

> Dr Patrick discusses 2 studies showing benefits of sulforaphane in treating prostate cancer.[](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2017/06/170606112750.htm)

**[Low Cholesterol Leads To Lower PSA, Lower Prostate Cancer Risk, Study Suggests](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2008/05/080515072654.htm)**
This appears to be a study to show benefit of statins on PSA

> Data collected from a study of 1,214 men prescribed cholesterol-lowering drugs (statins) between 1990 and 2006 at the Durham Veteran Affairs Medical Center in North Carolina shows that PSA levels were reduced after starting statin medications and that this decline was proportional to the decline in LDL cholesterol.

**[Lifestyle And Diet May Stop Or Reverse Prostate Cancer Progression (2005)](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2005/08/050811084426.htm)**

> Men with early stage prostate cancer who make intensive changes in diet and lifestyle may stop or perhaps even reverse the progression of their illness, according to a new study. The research is the first randomized, controlled trial showing that lifestyle changes may affect the progression of any type of cancer. Study findings are published in the September issue of the _Journal of Urology_.

**[Heart disease may be a risk factor for prostate cancer (2012)](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/02/120208132717.htm)**

> If confirmed that heart disease is a risk factor for prostate cancer, the malignancy might be combated in part by lifestyle changes such as weight loss, exercise and a healthy diet, which are known to prevent heart disease.

**[Treating gum disease reduces prostate symptoms, researchers find](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2015/05/150505102437.htm)**

> Treating gum disease reduced symptoms of prostate inflammation, called prostatitis, report researchers from Case Western Reserve University School of Dental Medicine and the Departments of Urology and Pathology at University Hospitals Case Medical Center.

### Huck — 2017-06-30T01:58:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-217531>

Accidentally came across this video. Skip over the parts where they ask for a pledge for Louisiana Public TV (it's several years old). The Atchafalaya River is the original route of the Mississippi River in Louisiana. Now it is the largest swamp in US and the major spillway for the Mississippi.

It's about a young couple living on a houseboat in the Atchafalaya swamp back in the 70's. Living off the fish and other foods from the swamp, plus the food they grew in their garden, plus fresh meat dropped off by passing hunters.

ETA: The reason I posted this was because it reminds us that the urge to get away from overcrowding and live a simple life eating fresh fish and seafood, game, and fruits and vegetables is innate in us.

We hate flying so many vacations are a drive into New Orleans, since my son and I are foodies.

Every time we drive past the Southern tip of Atchafalaya National Wildlife Refuge I feel very drawn to the area. A flooding river and swamps surrounded the first house I grew up in. I loved it.

### Huck — 2017-06-30T15:04:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-217576>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=217564)
>
> what about selling your house and just travelling in your RV? Lots of people do that here in OZ.....

I'm looking at 2 options. That's one of them. The other is to move to the gulf coast (US). I'm thinking of combining them - Travel until I tire of it and then move to the gulf.

I have some big (for me) repairs to do to the house before I can sell it. They were in the house when I bought it, but I missed them and so did my "inspector". I'm slowly knocking them out, but it means spending lots of time on youtube researching how to fix various problems. I just replaced the bathtub/shower faucet diverter and fixed the drain linkage. Last year I replaced the wall that was rotted out and replaced the tile. Next up is to replace part of the bathroom subfloor which has gotten spongy.

By the time I cut the grass, trim the trees and shrubs, weed the garden, weed my landscaped hill, etc, etc, etc, I'm too tired to get as much of the repairs done as I would like. If I do the repairs first, the weeds overrun everything making it much more difficult to get that job done. I tried pushing myself to do more and after 4 days, I crashed for 2 days, so that strategy didn't work. I think my best bet is keep up the yard and do as much home repairs as I have energy for.

I'm doing the repairs myself partly to reduce costs, but also because of the satisfaction I get from learning how to do it myself. At my age, learning knew skills is good.

### Huck — 2017-07-07T03:09:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-218133>

I'm in the process of reglazing 24 glass panes. Eventually I figured out that a heat gun made it a lot easier to scrape out the caulk that had been used previously. This is one tedious, labor intensive job.

Today I bought a grease gun and learned how to use it to grease the wheels on lawn tractor. One wheel had fallen off when I was cutting the grass. I think it was because the wheel got so hot from friction that the ring that holds the wheel on popped off. That was quite a surprise to be zipping right along and then suddenly have the axle buried 6 inches in the ground.

That was quite a surprise to be zipping right along and then suddenly have the axle buried 6 inches in the ground.

LOL, been there done that. Even referred to it in Jan's butyric acid thread. Butyric acid enemas remind me of my grease gun!
