### Huck — 2019-02-06T15:19:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-262398>

This post should have been posted before previous post.

I had a cardiac cath about 3 weeks ago. I was diagnosed with severe aortic stenosis, so I thought this was the last procedure that was needed before having TAVR (Transcather Aortic valve Replacement).

About an hour after the cath, my cardiologist and one that did the cath came into recovery room and told me I had blockage in blah blah blah. They seemed to lapse into a foreign language at that point. The only word I understood was 70%. I've been trying ever since to get my cardiologist to tell me exactly what's blocked and percent of blockage, and getting nowhere.

Then they said I needed either stents and a new aortic valve by catheter through the leg or bypass surgery and open heart surgery to replace the valve. They gave me (and my sister who was with me) the impression this was urgent. Then they sent me for an echocardiogram.

The cath procedure caused my bladder to spasm, so I had the urge to pee but couldn't. My BP and heart rate also jumped up to very high levels and I had trouble with the technical information they were telling me. As my cardiologist was leaving the room, he said he was sending the surgeon in to talk to me. A little later the surgeon came in and didn't really have any idea why he was talking to me. He had talked to my cardiologist and new my diagnosis, but no surgery was planned at that point, so he really had nothing to add.

I have the echo and learn that my aortic valve area (ava) is 1.1 cm2. This was interesting because what had caused the diagnosis of severe aortic stenosis 4 months ago was an ava of .77. Now either my ava is getting larger, which is extremely unlikely, or the the interpretation of previous echo was way off - .77 indicates severe aortic stenosis and 1.1 moderate. The cardiac cath also showed an ava of 1.1.

Three days later I get a message saying they had discussed my case and decided it wasn't urgent and to come back in 5 months. I've yet to get an explanation of why it changed from urgent to not urgent, what arteries are blocked and the severity, and what do they recommend for reducing plaque in arteries. I'm already on my 5th cardiologist, so I have my doubts changing again will make much difference.

So that's why I had the inflammation lab I referenced in above post. The only direction from cardiologist was take a baby aspirin and 40 mg Crestor. Crestor is used to reduce inflammation and my lab shows I already have a low level of inflammation. I see my integrative doc tomorrow and she can help me develop a strategy to reduce arterial plaque.

What is it with cardiologists that they can't communicate and don't seem to have a clue on anything but statins and surgery.

Since I'm venting, from what I am reading, the carotid intima–media thickness (CIMT) echo lab seems to be the best at identifying arterial plaque and percent of blockage. No radiation, so can repeat every few months to see if strategy is working. I called imaging center in my city and they didn't know what it was, same for an imaging center in larger city, and searching web found closest clinic 5 hours away. It's almost like they don't want us to get better!

### Sue-UK — 2019-02-08T12:23:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-262578>

> [Anne V said:](/goto/post?id=262533)
>
> **Silica** is responsible for the depositing of minerals into the bones, especially calcium. ... Even when calcium is insufficient, the **body** can turn **silica** into calcium that the bones need. 4. Healthier skin – Overseas, DE is used extensively as a health and beauty product for hair, skin, nails, bones, and joints.
> silicon can conduct electricity. silica cannot.
> help from there please [@Sue-UK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9658/) would it means that silica helps calcium going into the bones as opposed to aorta?

I think a big issue is aluminium - and although there are various ways to accumulate it, (including nnemf) I would suspect a pilot's added exposure through airport pollution would be significant. The Chris Exley studies show silicon (in the form of orthosilicic acid in water) removing aluminium. But you could easily read it the other way round. Aluminium depletes us of silicon. So I would suspect that over a period of time, if silicon intake was low, even if aluminium intake was also considered low by conventional wisdom, you could eventually go beyond not laying down bone properly or not so good looking hair skin and nails to losing or not replacing silicon in places that are supposed to be silicon rich. For my trying to hack DD's situation, the reference to animal studies showing that the aorta**,** trachea and **tendons** were found to be 4-5 times richer in silicon than the liver, heart and **muscles** put the tendons and muscles in bold text … Add in living like a mole in nnemf hellholes while she did a degree and then did a masters, and the shit hit the fan …

Chris Exley's view IIRC is that silicon supplements or DE aren't the way to go. I would suspect someone could do it with epi paleo (oysters, mussels, survivors soups etc) but I don't know any figures for silicon from those sources. There's silicon in plant products, but then there's bioavailability questions, grain being best sources, deuterium etc. I think silicon rich mineral water is a low hanging fruit, because the mineral content is measured, and it doesn't need an optimally working digestion to get the silicon into a bioavailable form- the orthosilicic acid is already there, and just follows the water through.

I think the silicon transmuting into calcium "recipe" needs UV. And as for nano bacteria creating a biofilm  UV is nature's antibiotic.  As for conduction and semi conduction … I'm still trying to get a picture in my head from time 21.

### Huck — 2019-02-08T20:03:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-262618>

I had heart palpitations (heart racing and pounding) last night which woke me up. The 1st 2 times, it was mild and within a minute or 2, heart stopped pounding and returned to normal. The last time, it lasted for at least 5 minutes and heart rate was over 100. After a half hour, I still didn't feel right and decided to go to ER.

I have had this happen in the past and it was caused by EMF. Both cases were running a space heater, several times in my bedroom before I found the cause and once in my travel trailer. I checked for EMF last night and didn't find a problem.

When I got to ER, HR was about 130 and BP was elevated. They did EKG, chest x-ray, blood tests, and examined me. Then started a drip with BP med. After 3 hours they reran the blood tests and did another EKG. All the blood tests, EKGs, chest x-ray, etc were negative. After laying in bed for 6 hours, they let me go.

Outside of moving to a different bedroom tonight, I have no other ideas.

### Huck — 2019-02-09T14:46:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-262669>

Moved to other bedroom - no palpitations last night. I checked it with trifield meter and magnetic and electric are both 0.

Found some articles that are of interest based on a search for mitochondria heart disease.

[**Mitochondrial Quality Control in Cardiac Diseases**](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fphys.2016.00479/full)
**Summary and Perspectives**
Mitochondria are essential organelles for the maintenance of myocardial homeostasis. They play critical role in bioenergetics, redox balance, ion homeostasis, and cell death. The importance of functional mitochondrial to the heart has been highlighted by the fact that situations that lead to mitochondrial dysfunction are often associated with cardiac diseases. Many of these diseases manifest later in life, where mitochondria seems to be less functional.

Therefore, different levels of mechanisms of surveillance and quality control capable of detecting and fixing defects that affect mitochondrial performance are critical for the maintenance of long-lived cells with high energy demand such as cardiomyocytes. The central role of mitochondrial quality control in the health of myocardium has been recently reported. As described above, the machinery regulating mitochondrial quality control including mitochondrial redox balance, aldehyde metabolism, proteostasis, dynamics, and clearance are potential novel therapeutic targets for cardiac diseases. However, future research focusing on the critical molecular events involved in mitochondrial quality control is needed to develop better pharmacological interventions.

[**Role of Mitochondria in Heart Disease Should Not Be Underestimated**](https://mitochondrialdiseasenews.com/2016/11/14/mitochondria-role-in-heart-disease-should-not-be-underestimated/)
This is a review of above article

> [**Mitochondrial Quality Control in Cardiac Diseases**](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fphys.2016.00479/full)
> **Summary and Perspectives**
> Mitochondria are essential organelles for the maintenance of myocardial homeostasis. They play critical role in bioenergetics, redox balance, ion homeostasis, and cell death. The importance of functional mitochondrial to the heart has been highlighted by the fact that situations that lead to mitochondrial dysfunction are often associated with cardiac diseases. Many of these diseases manifest later in life, where mitochondria seems to be less functional.
>
> Therefore, different levels of mechanisms of surveillance and quality control capable of detecting and fixing defects that affect mitochondrial performance are critical for the maintenance of long-lived cells with high energy demand such as cardiomyocytes. The central role of mitochondrial quality control in the health of myocardium has been recently reported. As described above, the machinery regulating mitochondrial quality control including mitochondrial redox balance, aldehyde metabolism, proteostasis, dynamics, and clearance are potential novel therapeutic targets for cardiac diseases. > This is a review of above article

I was interested to read that cardiomyocytes don't undergo fusion and fission like other cells - instead they form more of an interconnected network, and 'kiss' to exchange contents. Non adjacent mitochondria form nanotubular tunnels or nanotunnels, to exchange contents at a distance. Apparently the contents of a single mitochondrion can disperse over a whole cardiomyocyte in the space of ten hours.

### Huck — 2019-02-16T15:24:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-263319>

I've been sleeping in spare bedroom for a week and have not had a single occurrence of heart palpitations (that I know of). I have also had 5 days in a row of feeling really good and my slight heart discomfort is mostly gone. I spent 2 hours working in the garden yesterday and no hd symptoms at all.

I bought this house a little over 10 years ago. The wife, of the widower I bought it from, died from complications of Alzheimer's about 5 years earlier. The husband then died of complications from Alzheimer's about a year ago. Coupled with the heart problems I am having, I think something in the master bedroom may be a cause.

I don't know why it works, but it sure seems to.

About 10 days ago, I realized that for the previous 3 nights I had gotten up just 1 time on 2 nights and not at all on the 3rd night to go to the bathroom. This pattern has continued every night since then. Prior to that, I got up anywhere from 2 to 6 times a night.

What changed? I think it must be that I had started taking 1000 mg of MSM a few days previous to the change in night time urination. I've noticed a couple of other improvements since starting msm, so I think it might be reducing hidden inflammation.

I can't be sure that MSM is causing the improvement, but maybe someone who is also having this problem might give it a try and let me know if it works for them.

### Huck — 2019-07-11T21:55:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-274087>

> [Anne V said:](/goto/post?id=274078)
>
> the asthma was caused by an extremly stressful phone call. it happened half way through . i had no asthma for 15 years.

Don't let people get to you. Always put yourself first.

No symptoms. I feel good. My latest ejection fraction was 60%-65%, so breathing hasn't been a problem. Haven't had a problem with blood pressure in over 6 months, in fact I cut back on the 1 BP med I am still taking.

I was early for my doc appt yesterday, so I went to a nearby park. I walked down a very steep hill, around the picnic area, and then started back. Although it wasn't a huge hill, it was much steeper than what I am used to. I started wondering if I could make it up the hill OK. I was breathing a little harder when I got to the top, but no harder than I would have been 4 or 5 years ago.

Think about my theory. As the number of particles in the blood increases, there are more opportunities for particles to collide with artery walls and cause damage. Whether it's ldl, or triglycerides, or glucose, or toxins, or lps or white blood cells, they would start bouncing around in the arteries. Close to the heart they are under a lot of pressure so that would increase the damage. So anything that decreases triglycerides, cholesterol, glucose, inflammation, etc to normal levels should reduce the opportunity for damage. Grounding and getting plenty of sun would lower viscocity and make blood vessels more flexible, also helping to prevent damage. But if one has a leaky gut, that has to be fixed first.

Think about the ball machine they use for bingo or the lottery. All the balls colliding with each other and the sides of the container. That's what I think may be going on in our arteries. Once the artery wall is damaged, like any wound it first scabs over and eventually new cells grow over the damaged area. Some of the particles in the bloodstream are either used for repair or accidentally stuck in the repair. I can imagine that ldl, which is a small particle, might get lodged in a hole created by particles hitting the cell walls and forcing a small opening.

### Huck — 2019-07-11T22:11:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-274091>

> [Anne V said:](/goto/post?id=274083)
>
> can you live the life you want without an op?
> is it just fear that makes u wish to have it done,
> if i get it right u are ok now are you?
> so would u project what could happen
> and want to control (stop it) now
> before all these terrible things happens to you

Although I am doing everything I can to try to reverse as, there are no confirmed cases of anyone reversing severe aortic stenosis. I also have 80% blockage in OM2, which I am prepared to live with, but doctors are insisting on a stent. They haven't convinced me of the need, so I am still discussing the stent with them.

As I am 70, and the odds are about 100% that I will have to have my valve replaced, I want it done asap so I can move on with the rest of my life. If tavr was covered by Medicare, it would be different, but since I have to rely on being accepted into a tavr trial, I need to be in contact with a facility that participates. I am being seen by a participating major university that is about 1 1/2 hours from my house. I don't want to move until I have had the valve replaced as there is no participating facility close to where I plan on moving.

What it comes down to is if I stay here, I can likely have TAVR, but if I move I very well might have to have open heart surgery. I will do almost anything to avoid open heart surgery.

Can I live without an op? I don't think anyone with severe as can live without having the valve replaced. I think if it was mild or moderate, possibly future damage could be stopped and surgery avoided, but not with the damage that severe causes.

Many people with aortic stenosis don't have arterial plaque. Two different problems.

There are many scientific studies showing that arterial plaque can be stopped and sometimes reversed, but no studies showing aortic stenosis can be stopped or reversed.

### Huck — 2019-07-21T16:26:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-274717>

I've been exploring the idea that if LDL isn't the cause of atherosclerosis, maybe I should look at the triglyceride side of the equation, and I don't mean by looking at triglyceride value in a lab test. Chylomicrons and VLDL carry triglycerides to the cells and after they have given up most of their triglycerides, they become remnants.

Just as the liver removes LDL from circulation, it also removes chylomicron and vldl (idl) remnants. At least it is supposed to. But what happens if the liver is unable to remove all the remnants?

In going back to the weird blood pressure spikes I was having, my thought was that something in my blood wasn't being cleared as it should be and that some circadian process that starts in late afternoon caused blood pressure to spike and then around 1 am, another circadian process kicked off that actually cleared these particles.

I was pretty sure it was related to my genetic predisposition to have trouble digesting and metabolizing saturated fat. I was eating a high fat diet at the time the bp spikes started and eventually put these 2 things together. Add a leaky gut and my liver was really being taxed.

I changed to a low fat, mostly plant based diet and over time, the BP spikes lessened and eventually stopped.Also was able to lose weight, which I had not been able to do on a high fat diet.

Recently I watched a video on lipoprotein metabolism. There is so much more than what shows up in a cholesterol test. That's where I got the idea to look deeper into the fat side of the triglyceride/cholesterol equation. When I saw that chylomicrons were transporting triglycerides and the remnant was cleared by the liver, things fell into place. If my cells weren't metabolizing (genetic) all the fat from chylomicrons and vldl, what happened to them? Did they stay in circulation or was the liver removing them?

And most importantly, were they atherogenic? Is there a lab test to measure remnants? And guess what I found?

"CONCLUSIONS: I**ncreased concentrations of both calculated and measured remnant cholesterol were associated with increased all-cause mortality in patients with ischemic heart disease,** which was **not the case for increased concentrations of measured LDL cholesterol**. This suggests that **increased concentrations of remnant cholesterol explain part of the residual risk of all-cause mortality in patients with ischemic heart disease**.© 2016 American Association for Clinical Chemistry"​

[http://clinchem.aaccjnls.org/content/clinchem/early/2016/02/17/clinchem.2015.253757.full.pdf](http://clinchem.aaccjnls.org/content/clinchem/early/2016/02/17/clinchem.2015.253757.full.pdf)

"It is now evident that **elevated circulating levels of triglycerides in the non-fasting state, a marker for triglyceride (TG)-rich remnant particles, are associated with increased risk of premature cardiovascular disease** (CVD)."
​

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27216847](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27216847)

"However, despite success in reducing LDL-C, significant residual cardiovascular risk remains.4 **Emerging evidence suggests that** triglycerides (TG) and /or the cholesterol content within triglyceride-rich lipoproteins (TGRL), (e.g., **remnant cholesterol** \[RC\]), **may be important contributors to residual risk and should perhaps be targets of therapy**.5"
​

[https://www.acc.org/latest-in-cardi...nt-cholesterol-and-atherosclerotic-cv-disease](https://www.acc.org/latest-in-cardiology/articles/2019/02/07/09/47/triglycerides-remnant-cholesterol-and-atherosclerotic-cv-disease)

### Anne V — 2019-09-17T11:19:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-278014>

**Minimally invasive aortic valve replacement**
Aortic valve replacement may be required due to narrowing, or regurgitation where the valve doesn’t close properly. In a healthy heart, the aortic valve regulates the flow of oxygen-rich blood from the heart into the main blood vessel. The aortic valve is made up of three triangular-shaped flaps which fit neatly together.

The flaps open up to allow blood to enter into the artery, which then close to prevent regurgitation. However, the valve may wear out or become caked in calcium deposits rendering it inefficient.

The heart has to work harder to pump blood around the body. Symptoms resulting include chest pains, breathlessness and fainting – if untreated, it could lead to heart failure and death.

Traditionally, mechanical valves have been used to replace worn out aortic valves – but, of course, this is invasive surgery and patients will need to take warfarin for the rest of their lives. New technologies such as the Inspiris Resilia valve are also providing great results for patients. The indications are that it will last longer than other animal tissue valves, which – although have advantages over mechanical valves – need to be replaced every eight to ten years.

It’s made from tissue from cow pericardium (the heart lining) and it uses new anti-calcification technology which prevents the valve becoming stiff and damaged, meaning it lasts a lot longer.

The new Edwards Intuity valve, which is also made from cow tissue, has all of these advantages too. It doesn’t need stitches as it’s held in place by a fibrous ring at the base of where the original valve was. The new valve is fixed to a stent (tube-like mesh) made of stainless steel which holds it under the fibrous ring.

These new technologies are game-changers, as they last for longer than previous animal tissue valves.

### Huck — 2019-09-25T12:33:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-278826>

Had TAVR (Aortic valve replacement via transcatheter) Monday morning and released 2PM Tuesday. Doc said it went perfectly. Other than taking it easy for a couple of weeks and keeping the catheter entry point clean, I am pretty much back to normal. Can't drive for a week or 2 and can't lift anything heavy for a while, but that's about the only restrictions.

On the days leading up to the procedure I did at least an hour nude sunbathing and grounding daily and had several long Epsom salts bath to try to load up on magnesium and sulfur. My magnesium and potassium were both within range, but they like them to be top of range for heart patients. I still need to raise them, so I am going to start making magnesium water.

I pleaded with them to release me on Tuesday so I could go home and heal. Hospitals are terrible for healing. This was my 1 night in the hospital:

-   7 pm - Check blood pressure, heart rate, and temperature
-   11 pm - Check blood pressure, heart rate, and temperature

-   12 am (midnight) - blood draw
-   3 am - Check blood pressure, heart rate, and temperature
-   4 am - Weighed me (really, I had to get out of bed and get weighed)

-   5 am - EKG
-   7 am - Check blood pressure, heart rate, and temperature

I had a 2 hour car ride home. Had lots of blood pooling in my groin, so I guess I should have asked my friend to stop every half hour so I could walk around a little. Didn't get any worse over night, so hopefully drinking lots of water will solve the problem.

For anyone in US, TAVR is now covered for low risk surgical patients by Medicare. The valve I had put in by TAVR rates slightly better than surgical replacement in many critical areas.

### Huck — 2023-04-24T22:58:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-320700>

I've been reading over my posts from 4 years ago during the time I had TAVR (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement). I think I was on to something with the idea that for some people, saturated fat and a leaky gut can result in aortic stenosis.

> During gram-negative bacterial infection and other acute illness such as absorption from the gut infection, **lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a major mediator in sepsis**. LPS is able to trigger inflammation through both intracellular and extracellular pathways. pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34004605/

I think that means there are 3 possible stages:
1\. The Liver is able to keep up with the removal of LPSs and other contaminants in the blood.
2\. The Liver is not able to keep up
3\. The Liver is not able to keep up and the person becomes septic.

The one thing I have learned is the body wants to stay alive, so there has to be more to stage 2. Would storing LPSs and other junk from the blood in plaque be better than Sepsis? Yes, you die later rather than sooner.

> The complex pathophysiology of sepsis makes it a syndrome with limited therapeutic options and a high mortality rate. Gram-negative bacteria containing lipopolysaccharides (LPS) in their outer membrane correspond to the most common cause of sepsis. Since the gut is considered an important source of LPS, intestinal damage has been considered a cause and a consequence of sepsis. Although important in the maintenance of the intestinal epithelial cell homeostasis, the microbiota has been considered a source of LPS. Recent studies have started to shed light on how sepsis is triggered by dysbiosis, and an increased inflammatory state of the intestinal epithelial cells, expanding the understanding of the gut-liver axis in sepsis. [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34229252/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34229252/)

Dr. Paul Marik has develpoped a protocol for treating Sepsis which is combination of Hydrocortisone, **Ascorbic Acid** and Thiamine (HAT therapy) may reduce organ failure and mortality in patients with sepsis and septic shock. I gave also found a protocol for people having a blood pressure spike after eating a high fat/high carb meal which is Vitamin C and Vitamin E. I have used this with some success. I bring this up because the protocol for sepsis and for a sudden BP spike both include vitamin C.

I also think this may explain why people with cardiac disease who go on a plant based, whole foods diet tend to stabilize their level of plaque.

The piece I still can't find is what happens to saturated fat in the blood, that either has not been digested or has been digested but not metabolized, when the liver is not able to keep up with the demand. I would think it is very much like the way LPSs are handled.

My Nurse Practioner agreed to write a script for me to have a lab done testing LPS Binding Protein (LBP). I hoped this would give me a baseline to work from, but LabCorp doesn't do the lab and when I searched online, it appears it is experimental and costs at least $800 just for the kit. I need to find some way to find out what is in my blood that might be causing the plaque.

I also stumbled on this during my research.

> There is a **link between high lipopolysaccharide (LPS) levels in the blood and the metabolic syndrome, and metabolic syndrome predisposes patients to severe COVID-19.** [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7799037/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7799037/)

**Menaquinone-7** (MK-7), also known as vitamin K2, is a cofactor for the carboxylation of proteins involved in the inhibition of arterial calcification and has been suggested to reduce the progression rate of aortic valve calcification (AVC) in patients with **aortic stenosis**.

The only time it wasn't high was when I was on a vegetarian diet. The vegetarian diet also stopped the blood pressure spikes. Those were 2 of the clues that led me to suspect saturated fat is the problem. I just can't find what happens to the fat in the blood when the liver can't handle it.

When I had BP spikes, they would tend to clear starting at about 1 am, so I assume some circadian process is getting rid of the fat in the blood at that time.

I have seen claims about vit K MK-7.
It tells calcium where to go.
When MK-7 is low, calcium instead of heading for bones goes to the vascular system.

This one talks generically about vit K
But there is a multitude of various vit K's, the MK-7 seems to be the one that should be helpful to you.

### [Vitamin K2—a neglected player in cardiovascular health: a narrative review](https://openheart.bmj.com/content/8/2/e001715)

Vitamin K2 serves an important role in cardiovascular health through regulation of calcium homeostasis. Its effects on the cardiovascular system are mediated through activation of the anti-calcific protein known as matrix Gla protein. In its inactive form, this protein is associated with various...

I restarted the Leptin Reset a little over a week ago. My blood pressure (BP) has been very stable over the last couple of months. Rarely did I have any readings over 135/85.

Now I am having high readings, as high as 190/104, during the morning and afternoon. By about 6 pm, BP is close to 120/80.

Could this be from the master circadian clock resetting and hormones starting to normalize? My heart rate also tends to go up along with BP.

I had a recent appointment with cardiologist who said heart looks fine (including EKG and electrocardiogram).

### Huck — 2024-07-06T21:21:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-336752>

I am moving to south Louisiana in about a month. Within an hour's drive, I have access to surf fishing in the gulf, fishing in lakes and bayous, catching crawfish, crabs, and shrimp, and possibly collecting oysters. As a senior, one fishing license covers it all, and it is not very expensive.

If you have never been to south Louisiana, it is a lot like visiting a foreign country. The area was settled by Cajuns, who are the descendants of French Acadians who were forced out of Nova Scotia by the British, and this part of Louisiana is still known as Acadiana.

### Huck — 2024-12-25T18:07:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/huck-my-journey-bp-and-aortic-stenosis.7685/post-344338>

I arrived in S Louisiana in late August. I spent about 10 days in a motel and then the next 4 months in an apartment. In the year before my move, I had plantar fasciitis and some lower back stiffness. Just before moving, I developed some discomfort in my upper back. During the drive from Virginia to Louisiana via a stop in Arkansas, no significant pain.

Once I got here, I started having back pain again. After moving into the apartment, it got worse. After a few weeks, I developed severe pain in both knees and both hips. I'm a side sleeper, so I wasn't getting much sleep because of the pain. I went to 2 chiropractors for a diagnosis and treatment. The 1st chiropractor managed to make it worse (first time that ever happened from an adjustment) and the 2nd made no difference.

The pain got worse and I could barely walk. I tried different things, but nothing seemed to help. Based on what the 2nd chiropractor said and some research, I determined what I had was hip and knee bursitis. It seemed strange that it affected both knees and both hips at exactly the same time. I thought there must be something in common that caused the bursitis to flare up. I could walk about 20-30 steps and then the pain would get bad enough to cause a severe limp.

I bought a better mattress and that didn't help. I then bought an expensive mattress topper and that provided some help. After a few weeks, the pain in my right knee and right hip stopped. Two months later, I still have the pain in my left knee and hip. I have started taping both the knee and the hip and that helps a lot, but I still am limited in how far I can walk before the leg just feels painful and weak.

In doing research, a couple of weeks ago, I found that dehydration can cause hip and knee pain. I drank about 80 oz of spring water the next day and saw immediate improvement. I thought that must be the problem, but after 2 weeks of increased water and adding electrolytes, the pain is almost as bad as 2 weeks ago.

I just started watching Jack's video - Top Brain Surgeon Instantly Banned After Revealing This! - and it dawned on me what I think is going on. Yes, I am dehydrated and until I limit blue light exposure, I will remain dehydrated regardless of how much water I drink. First staying in a motel and now in an apartment and finding it very painful to walk, I spent most of my time on my computer or watching tv.

I just bought a place and will be moving in over the next month. While not perfect, it does have a nice courtyard with lots of plants, grass, and a covered patio. It has good natural light and is in a great place for walking.

I wonder if dehydration might be an easy marker for tracking health? Can you be fully hydrated at the cellular level and be sick?

ETA: Upper and lower back pain is gone.
Pain in right knee and hip is gone
Still have pain in left knee and hip.

Did you have to have yours changed already?

# Thread: Nearly free CT baths
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nearly-free-ct-baths.7686/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-07-23T03:44:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nearly-free-ct-baths.7686/post-90608>

After driving 20 miles round trip to get 40 lbs of ice for $4 i figured there had to be a better way.

Finally the obvious became clear

A deep freeze and 4 1-gallon buckets is all u need. Actually a regular freezer will will work fine if u have the room.

Just fill your buckets of water and freeze them

Mine froze solid in less than 20 hours

Then fill your tub with water and put the buckets in the tub

The tub water will melt the ice in the bucket enough for a massive cub to come out.

My water out of the faucet was 78 degrees.

In 40 minutes my 4 massive cubes melted and my water was 55 degrees

No more buying ice and no more driving for ice - i luv it

### caroline — 2013-07-23T04:17:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nearly-free-ct-baths.7686/post-90612>

There was a joke around here - long ago ....our fridges and freezers are for ice and water - not food!

I just spent all day yesterday defrosting my freezer ...... my large baggies of water had leaked and everything in my freezer was frozen solid together! I have put off doing that for ages - finally - yesterday was the day! horrible job!

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-23T16:01:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nearly-free-ct-baths.7686/post-90631>

This reminds me. DH and I need to figure out how to get our 2nd deep freezer working....

### Lahelada — 2013-07-23T16:41:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nearly-free-ct-baths.7686/post-90638>

Great idea. I don't have the freezer space for the moment but will keep the idea in mind.
Caroline, I freeze PET water bottles to avoid the mess of leaky plastic bags.

### PrimalPam — 2013-07-24T02:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nearly-free-ct-baths.7686/post-90696>

I have two entire shelves in my freezer devoted to freezing ice for my baths. I use plastic containers of varying sizes and stack them on top of each other. Rubbermaid makes rectangle-shaped containers with lids that are BPA-free, and they are great for large blocks of ice. I combine those with smaller plastic containers, which melt faster. With those two shelves' worth of ice, I can get it down to 55 degrees on a hot day like today, or 50 degrees in the winter.

And yes, it's free!

# Thread: The state of evidence based medicine.......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-state-of-evidence-based-medicine.7689/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-23T06:32:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-state-of-evidence-based-medicine.7689/post-90618>

What's the Evidence?

Modern medicineâ€™s recent mantra is to become â€œevidence based.â€ No more listening to anecdote. No more acceptance of poorly performed studies. Now we will look at â€œhard evidenceâ€ and properly designed trials to determine how to best treat human beings.

And for evidence based medicine, death is a â€œhardâ€ end point. These days many drug studies only observe â€œrisk factors,â€ not â€œreal endpoints.â€

So letâ€™s include death as a real endpoint.

Which is why a recent article by Dr. Lawrence Altman in the New York Times should wake us up â€“ to the false reporting of death.

Not the ultimate outcome, itself. The people in the morgues do not rise up to protest the false attributions of their demise.

Yet death records are used for many purposes, including figuring out what causes death. And the studies Altman cites makes clear that when it comes to apportioning cause, death is not the end â€“ just another, incorrect data point.

Things To Write Up In NY When Youâ€™re Dead

The studies were performed in NY, in hospitals of the sort I trained in. Have things changed much?

Not as much as they should.

\-Over half of physicians reported knowlingly writing the wrong cause of death.

Why? Drop down menus off electronic records donâ€™t allow correct answers. Which can also be a real problem when youâ€™re writing notes and ordering treatments for living people (but thatâ€™s another story.)

\-No one told the residents in training how to write up death records. No training at all.

Here, the tradition continues. One example I remember from internship â€“ in California â€“ was of an intern asked to pronounce someone dead. He promptly walked into the hospital room, declaimed â€œI pronounce you dead,â€ and left.

So what happened when a group of trainees were instructed in proper death records?

Suddenly all the numbers changed.

Heart disease deaths fell by 54% - far more effective than even the most efficient CCU.

Influenza and pneumonia deaths tripled â€“ rising up to 11%. Cancer increased from 11% to 16%.

The conclusion â€“ after doctor training, the causes of death change after death.

But whatâ€™s truly disturbing about all these â€œcausesâ€ and presumed â€œcausesâ€ of demise? Theyâ€™re not accurate, no matter how well trained doctors are in â€œchartingâ€.

Because the real cause of death is best understood through autopsies.

And they are now rarely performed.

Why? Cost. Insurers wonâ€™t pay. Effort. Trouble with families providing consent.

And great institutional reluctance. Hospitals and doctors often donâ€™t want to know what killed a lot of people.

Because autopsies will show what they missed. And they miss a lot.

More than we can know with present day technology.

The Evidence of History

Overall, perhaps medicos should not feel so bad. Evidence based studies are not performing well in other arenas.

Consider the strange case of Giovanni Patalucci â€“ the Italian Schindler.

Patalucci was police chief in Fiume, a part of the Dalmatian coast which is now Croatian and no longer Italian. He was said to save the lives of 5000 Jews before dying, himself another victim of the Nazis, at Dachau.

Patalucci is a martyr of the Catholic Church. A film was made of his life. Piazzas throughout Italy are named for him and his heroic resistance. His name bears witness at Yad Vashem in Jerusalem as one of the just. Michael Bloomberg honored him in New York.

All of these â€œfactsâ€ are true. Then the NY Times broke the story:

Patalucci was not police chief but an assistant commissioner enforcing race laws.

A higher percentage of Jews from Fiume died than in almost any part of Italy â€“ more than 80%. Many were probably sped on their way to the gas chambers by Patalucci.

He was an active fascist and Nazi collaborator, except the Nazis suspected him of embezzlement and treason before they killed him.

In other words the evidence â€“ and the history, the story, the heroism â€“ were all wrong. Dead wrong.

Fabrications. Aided by a Vatican interested in an Italian hero, a family that wanted a pension, a bishop cousin, and the search to find Italian â€œheroesâ€ who had not played ball with the Germans.

Bottom Line

Evidence is only as good as the methods used to obtain it. Much of present â€œknowledgeâ€ is the result of shoddy record keeping, inadvertent survival of written reports, ideological selection, and outright lies.

So reader, beware. All those â€œpooled analysesâ€ used to define what treatments work and donâ€™t work may have been infected by a host of factors â€“ including unconscious biases â€“ that always afflict clinical studies.

We can learn from history. We need to. Including that what is written is often wrong.

Even if the records â€“ and the evidence â€“ said it was right.

From Dr. Breus. Truth Bomb.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-29T17:57:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-state-of-evidence-based-medicine.7689/post-91104>

The real problem with evidence based medicine is that we report positive findings 200% more in PEER review journals than we do negative results. This leads us to many false beliefs.

This point really sunk in when we studied Meta-Analysis in Uni...it really didn't sit well with me to know that the more studies are accumulated with positive findings (the closer you approach infinity) the GREATER THE LIKELIHOOD YOU CAN FIND A CONNECTION/CORRELATION BETWEEN ANYTHING..even if that connection is not based in truth/experience for someone!

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-07-29T18:38:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-state-of-evidence-based-medicine.7689/post-91109>

Follow the money ...... negative results = funds for studies dry up

Thus, researchers have a strong bias towards positive results.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-29T19:18:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-state-of-evidence-based-medicine.7689/post-91112>

The irony is that scientist know this is going on yet they call for more studies.......WHY? It keeps them in business.......It really is a dog chasing its tail.

# Thread: 2 months on leptin reset--few specific questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-months-on-leptin-reset-few-specific-questions.7693/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### windycity88 — 2013-07-23T17:39:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-months-on-leptin-reset-few-specific-questions.7693/post-90648>

Hi all--

I've been on the leptin reset since early May, and I've seen a ton of positive signs. However, there are a few areas I'm looking to troubleshoot--and I'd appreciate any thoughts! Sorry if this is long--I'll give some background, then ask my questions.

Some background:

\- I started the leptin reset in May, 8 months after three HCG cycles and a diagnosis of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. (One symptom was persistent weight gain, even after HCG, despite very careful eating.) I gained 40 lbs as a kid, pretty much out of the blue, and have struggled with weight most of my life.

\- I've been paleo since 2008, auto-immune paleo (no eggs, nuts or seeds, nightshades, dairy, chocolate, alcohol) since November of 2012. I've been working on my adrenals (adaptogens, no caffeine since March 2013) and leaky gut (bone broth, L-Glutamine, NAC + zinc + C, DGL). I had a long, long exercise rest as part of healing my adrenals, and have also done a bunch of acupuncture, and I've seen some real improvements, including way more energy and focus in the morning.

The leptin reset was something I tried because my weight just kept climbing (10 lbs total), despite rigorous adherence to a low-carb, easy-on-the-gut AI paleo diet. Sometimes I could trace it to supplements--a probiotic that had traces of dairy, a Vitamin C powder that was derived from corn, and the weight gain would stop (but not reverse) when I removed it. Sometimes, it seemed arbitrary. Dr. Kruse's explanation of the hormone cascade made so much sense to me, and aligns with my experience of my own body.

I did the leptin reset throughout May, without cheating, and saw tons of improvements--dropped 5 of the 10 lbs I've gained, had tons more energy in the morning, improved sex drive. I then cheated for a few days on a vacation (went over the carb limit, did some snacking--no really unhealthy foods, though), and resumed the diet for four weeks in June. I re-lost the same 5 lbs (it had all come right back on), saw the color of my skin and nails improve.

Then, in early July, my weight started climbing again. I put back on the 5 lbs I had lost again, despite eating exactly the same things I'd been eating before (I work at home and cook all my food). So I thought maybe it was time to add in weight lifting and sprints. I also thought that perhaps it was time to switch to the "after leptin" diet--so, relatively smaller breakfast (25-30g protein) and bigger dinner. I also added in some fruit and starchy vegetables--which actually made me feel a lot better, probably since I've been very low carb since last fall. I've now been lifting for two weeks, and I added in sprinting yesterday. However, after two weeks of stability (which made me think the starchy vegetables--sweet potatoes, squash--and fruit were okay), my weight has started to climb again. I am still seeing tons of good signs--I sweat during exercise, my nails are growing like crazy, my weight is more stable rather than bouncing around, it's just headed in the wrong direction.

I sleep 8+ hours a night, get morning sun, avoid screens after dark, and go to bed when the sun goes down. I also have good relationships and fairly low stress in my life.

So, my questions:

1\. Is it common to gain weight before losing it on the leptin reset? With all the other good indicators, should I just keep going, or is the increased weight a sign I need to pay attention to?

2\. Was I wrong to switch to the "after leptin" diet? Should I stay on the initial "gigantic breakfast" reset as long as I need to lose weight, even when all the positive indicators are in place?

Obviously, I want to get these 10 pounds off now. But I'm more interested in restoring my long-term health, which I think will eventually lead to the weight loss. Any thoughts?

### caroline — 2013-07-24T02:49:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-months-on-leptin-reset-few-specific-questions.7693/post-90698>

Hi Windy.....you have been working at all this really hard! I don't know what I can offer - I want to think about this for awhile.

Have you checked for EMFs in your home? Did you listen to the EMF webinars with Michael Nuert? They were gold. Very important to see what your exposure is

What about water? Grounding? C/T? Drinking spring water is hugely important as is C/T to reduce inflammation
Grounding and getting sun everyday - also huge...
Do you have a smart meter? Do your neighbors?

Have you had labs done? A salivary ASI with diurnal cortisol would be really important to do..
Post your labs and someone will have a look as soon as they have some spare time.

Please keep readin Jack's blogs and asking questions.

### caroline — 2013-07-24T02:57:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2-months-on-leptin-reset-few-specific-questions.7693/post-90699>

BTW ....this isn't really about weightloss - it is about regaining your health. Something is obviously going on that you haven't addressed. Have you read the Hormone 101 blog? And we now have Hormone 102 that Jack just posted.
Have you read Jack's Book? It explains a lot in simple terms for those of us who don't have a science background.

It is a long but amazing journey to get to your optimal health....

# Thread: Cold Showers... Just as good as CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-showers-just-as-good-as-ct.7694/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MeghanK — 2013-07-23T18:27:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-showers-just-as-good-as-ct.7694/post-90664>

I have noticed that taking a cold shower is harder and feels colder than just sitting in cold water... the water moving across your body from the shower and hitting different parts of your body etc. makes is feel super cold and makes me shiver vs. just standing still under the faucet or sitting still in a tub? So since it's causing more "shiver" and a feeling of colder... is it better or at least = to 20 minutes in a cold tub?
My shower only gets to about 60 degrees and as of right now I do not have the freezer space for freezing water or the $$ to buy ice....

### Ake Bono — 2015-03-14T21:23:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-showers-just-as-good-as-ct.7694/post-158530>

yeah, that is a good point.
and if I may continue your question - are the benefits similar or is just the cold tub which bring benefits?

Im very sensitive to either cold or hot water so i started a "pseudo-version"
by using a wet piece of fabric (cold water) all over my body ?
is it any good in doing this? I cant do any cold tub bath.

### Cpt.Tired — 2015-04-30T02:46:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cold-showers-just-as-good-as-ct.7694/post-162806>

>
> I have noticed that taking a cold shower is harder and feels colder than just sitting in cold water... the water moving across your body from the shower and hitting different parts of your body etc. makes is feel super cold and makes me shiver vs. just standing still under the faucet or sitting still in a tub? So since it's causing more "shiver" and a feeling of colder... is it better or at least = to 20 minutes in a cold tub?
> My shower only gets to about 60 degrees and as of right now I do not have the freezer space for freezing water or the $$ to buy ice....

I agree and I totally notice this effect too.
It seems to feel colder when the skin temperature is not consistent, like it would be in a shower or wet skin in wind.

I have already been diagnosed with early stage MD and from this post, I gather it could be causing all sorts of problems. Are people with this mutation more or less helpless?

# Thread: Magnesium Threonate dosage for neurodegenerative disorders

**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx

### taidan — 2013-07-23T21:28:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate-dosage-for-neurodegenerative-disorders.7696/post-90674>

Hi everyone.

I'm 22 years old and have a neurodegenerative autoimmune disorder along with the slew of hormonal and health issues that goes along with that.

What is the dosage of Mag threonate that dr. Kruse normally reccommends? is 2 grams tid too much?

Also as an aside, anyone know if there is common anti inflammatory that dr. Kruse recommends?

Thanks

Tai

### kleinb87 — 2013-07-24T01:24:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate-dosage-for-neurodegenerative-disorders.7696/post-90685>

Hi Tai,

I can relate to you because I was there recently with tons of hormonal and health issues only at 26. The thing with Magnesium is that it is not useful when your body is dehydrated (or your semi-conductors fully loaded), which from just hearing your issues appears to be the case. Have you started CT and eliminating EMFs? That will help immensely. For anti-inflammatory, Resveratrol and Turmeric are great sources. Are you eating epipaleo and sticking with a low carb/ high fat leptin reset protocol? It may sound like a lot of questions, but can help the rest of us who are trying to help you.

Bobbi

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-24T02:07:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate-dosage-for-neurodegenerative-disorders.7696/post-90691>

Mg Threonate is only available in 37.5 mgs each as far as I know.......I have experimented with very high doses during some of power line biohacks........but I can not advocate for these doses on the internet.

ADD: the biohacks showed no MG effect therefore I dont recommend.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-24T04:38:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate-dosage-for-neurodegenerative-disorders.7696/post-90704>

### taidan — 2013-08-12T01:36:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-threonate-dosage-for-neurodegenerative-disorders.7696/post-92180>

Hi Everyone,

I appreciate all your replies!

Sorry for the late response, things have been kinda rough and I'v been having some problems logging in.

Anyway, I am on the verge of starting a third attempt at everyting- epi-paleo, LR, CT , Mito etc.

The problem is recently any willpower required activities seem to stress my PFC to the point where I get a headache and foggy. I think it might be an energy issue because it is worse on low sleep and no MCTs...

Anyway, I live in an apartment with other peoples WIFI all around, but I am also starting to shut off my phone and router at least. I don't have a tub to CT, but I can do my face I guess.

I think my issue is this: MHTFR/CBS/COMT snp -> toxin or pathogen accumulation -> everything jack talks about cascading from there - > Inflammation and autoimmune tendency triggered. Unfortunately now my immune system is very sensitive to either suppression or activation. I can't tolerate methylation supplements and even anti inflammatory make my neuropathy worse (I saw a study showing TNFa supression triggered MS..how?)

My Dr hopes that mito support and gut healing will help bring down inflammation and allow us to pursue other therapies. I plan on doing the full gamut to Jack's intuitive (at least to me) protocols rather than just taking an isolated COq10 supp.

Question regarding PQQ: I have heard rumors that it is better to fix mitchondria first before taking it as PQQ will only be regenerating broken mitchondria. Is this true?

Questions regarding Coq10: As Coq10 auto oxidizes and ibedenone does not, would Jack recommend taking the latter instead?

(also going to post these on the mito forums)

Thanks!

### Beverly — 2013-07-24T04:19:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beverly-requesting-some-lab-help.7699/post-90701>

Hi Jack and members. I posted some of my information on the comments page of the Not so hard truth about hair loss article. I will repost here as well as my recent labs.

I am early 40's and female. Gallbladder out at 28 in 2000. 3 miscarriages in a year in 2010. I have 3 children. ACDF c6/c7 2012. For the past several years I have had hair loss that started in my late teens early twenties. I have felt somewhat unwell for the past few years also (a sore feeling in my chest (it feels like my heart to me), nausea, heartburn, difficulty swallowing) the only thing that showed up with those sx was the finding of a cold thyroid nodule 8 yrs ago.

And now after the birth of my last son 2 years ago I feel extremely tired. Like I get tired just walking to my elevator down the hall. Or doing simple things like showering. I have seen several dr's over the years but until now I have never really pushed to get any blood work done.

The week that I went in to have my blood work done (2 weeks ago) I also started getting sore finger joints and sore hip joints as well as a tingling feeling that is pretty general. Please have a look and let me know what you think of my labs and where I should start looking.
The only test I haven't done yet is a celiac panel because I'm doing a gluten challenge for a month first. My lab result is the one on top and the reference range for it is always right below it. Thank you.

Nucleolar pattern in high titre is seen
most often with scleroderma and in
polymyositis/scleroderma overlap syndrome.
In lower titres it is seen in some patients
with SLE, and rarely, in other rheumatic
diseases. Consider specific testing for
anti-Scl-70 (included in the ENA antibody
test) if clinically indicated.

of antibodies to Sm, SS-A, SS-B, Scl-70,
Jo-1 and RNP.

# Thread: Feeling Really Discouraged
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fireforlunch — 2013-07-24T04:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90703>

Been on the Reset for over a month now and I'm really feeling discouraged especially after getting some labs back that looked poor and the Dr saying they looked fine to her so she wouldn't do anything.

JanSz recommended that I not exercise at all until I improve my labs - and that really sucks as I was really hoping to start some weights again after 2 months on the reset.

I have even gained a few pounds.

And recently butter has begun to upset my stomach so no more of my delightful coconut oil/butter mix or butter on my foods.

I had hoped that the I would have seen the positive results of becoming a fat burner by now (more energy, fat loss etc) but it's just not happening.

To top it off I've got this nasty thrush on my tongue - I can keep it at bay as long as I don't eat any fruit.

I have been CTing every day if nothing but a cold shower and trying to ice bath 3 times per week.

I feel like the only good news as of late is that I'll be moving (hopefully in the next 2 weeks) to a slower station (fire dept) so I won't be getting woke up nearly as much in the middle of the night.

Out of town right now but plan to try to hook up with a low carb doc when I get back.

### caroline — 2013-07-24T07:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90709>

Please, please remember that it took you years to get to where you are now .....and so it is going to take time.

Great news that you will be changing stations soon - that should help somewhat.

Have you addressed all your EMF issues?

I will read back over your journal and see if we are Missing something.

Please have a positive attitude ......remember what Dr. K. Says .....a thought can change your DNA.

Have you read Meg's story? They have sold their house and all their possessions and trying to sort out where they want to be and what they want to do.

Dr. K. Told her to -
do what she needed to do ....Not what she wanted to do .....and that is her mantra now.
She is very inspiring....

### Linz — 2013-07-24T13:32:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90721>

Fireforlunch,

I would look at the changes as a good thing - they are changes - and remember, it takes many months to become a fat burner. Many times I became over sensitive to things and had uncomfortable 'symptoms' that seemed interminable at the time but nearly always resulted in a big jump in progress afterwards. Often it was somewhere I was not even aware that I needed improvement. Look for small signs like realising it's 2 hours past lunch and you didn't notice and many others you will begin to see for yourself.

On a practical level, I would leave out the butter for a while but you could try using ghee instead of butter or just heat it and drain off the milk solids, and use butter from Channel Island breeds as they are more likely to produce A2 milk which is safer.

Again it might not be much comfort now but I am a middle-aged female, do little 'exercise' and never in the gym, just an active lifestyle, and I have taken some heavy doses of prednisolone in the past to control a difficult ai problem but last week I spent a day doing several hours of uncharacteristic repetitive heavy lifting with no more breaks than the men and barely a muscle ached the next day.

As Caroline says, it takes time. Persevere for a few months just taking each day as it comes and then look back just as you have done for the first month - it is a change in lifestyle for the better rather than a restriction. Hindsight can be magical and sometimes it is the things that have taken a year or two that are the most surprising. I looked back one week, one month and gradually stretched the intervals which gave me a much different perspective on the setbacks. It's much easier now Dr Kruse has given us so many answers, but do I feel any less like Alice in Wonderland yet?

Great news that you can reduce the night duties,

All the best

### fireforlunch — 2013-07-24T15:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90724>

Thank you for the insight.

I will keep pressing on.

I have addressed EMF issues.

Use a candle after sunset, no wireless or electricity or phone in bedroom

Our house is currently on the market as we want to move to a smaller house on some land

### SeaHorse — 2013-07-24T15:23:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90725>

Just another bit of support, if you have thrush (candida) you are going to need to get rid of it to feel better. Not eating sugar, as you are doing, is key. Candida is really a bugger to treat...it can go all through your system. If you aren't already, I'd add some anti-fungal supplements to all that you are doing.

It can be a good thing to have these symptoms arise....signs that your body is trying to purge and cleanse. Good luck and try not to get discouraged after such a short time....this is not a math equation but a quantum one....we are all different in our abilities to recover health.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-24T15:24:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90726>

> [fireforlunch said:](/goto/post?id=90724)
>
> I've read many places that it takes 2 to 3 weeks to become a fat burner - obviously that's not the case for everyone.

Fire - JAck has said it can take btwn 18-24mths to become an efficient fat burner. In order to become one you have to regularly enter the Penthosephospate Pathway also know as PPP here on the forum.

My own N=1, I've been paleo since 2010... its only been in the past 3-4 months that I've been easily entering the PPP and burning fat. Every one is different. each of will forge our own paths on this journey using the tools Jack has given us. I think adding all the protocols will help get you to the fat burning phase easier..

one of the purposes of the Leptin Rest - is to help you move from a sugar burner - to a fat burner... the BAB leverages High protein and Fat to get you there - your body must begin using the protein to may any glucose it needs... then eventually it moves to burning fat... hang in there... you'll get there...

I fought Jack... every step of the way the first 3 months on the LR. but eventually I starting following the protocol to a T. and I started seeing progress... This isn't something that happens over night, or even in a month... you're reversing years of what's been happening to your body... give your body a chance to do what it knows how to do...heal itself...

### nonchalant — 2013-07-24T17:09:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90734>

What SeaHorse said.

Sounds like you are shaking things up in your body. That's good. For thrush, I would also consider gargling with iodine water. Sometimes when I eat too many fruits or starches, my ears will itch, along with the other end of the eustachian tube in my mouth. Lugol's on a cotton swab stops it almost immediately.

Doctors used to treat thrush with Gentian violet. No idea what they use now. Probably an antibiotic.

### Huck — 2013-07-24T17:15:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feeling-really-discouraged.7700/post-90736>

The one thing that has helped me more than anything else is getting lots of sun in the summer. If you want to really give your hormones a bump, get outside in the sun as much as you can the rest of the summer. Barefoot is good. Nude even better. You want to get sun on parts of your body that don't usually get any.

The other thing I have learned is that taking hormones "may" be self defeating. Few doctors understand hormones, so you take one without understanding the effect it has on 2 others. If you eat epi-paleo, ct, exercise as appropriate, follow circadian rhythm, ground, etc, you should see your hormones normalize over time. I had huge improvements for the 1st 10 months, then backwards for about 6 months, and then more improvements in the last 4 months. So I am right in that 18-24 month range and am now at the lowest weight I have been in about 30 years.

Patience grasshopper.

# Thread: 20 yr old young man lost color vision
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-yr-old-young-man-lost-color-vision.7701/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lynn RN — 2013-07-24T06:53:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-yr-old-young-man-lost-color-vision.7701/post-90708>

Hey Dr K,
Not sure how to get this message to you, I hope this is OK.
I am an RN and I recently encountered a young man that began 4 years ago with a sharp stabbing pain in his head, he points to his forehead above his right eye. He states he went to many Doctors and specialists. He had several MRI's, Lab work and other dx test. All were normal and no one can diagnose or help his headache. He has tried all types of medication, massage, acupuncture and nothing helped. The intensity does vary but with no common thread. Last November 2012 he was watching TV and he had a flash in his left eye and instantly lost all color vision. He went to the ER, was examined by a variety of Doctors and even transferred from Phoebe Putney in Albany, GA to Emory in Atlanta. He has an episode of syncope that they diagnosed as TIA. January 2013 the same flash and loss of color happened in his right eye. Now his vision is black and white and he has the continuous headache. I asked about optic nerve neuritis and he said he was told he did not have this, but had optic nerve inflammation. A round of IV steroids did not help and again all test are normal. He is not on any medication presently and all doctors are baffled. He has just completed a degree in Physics at Darton college and is transferring to GA Tech in the fall. Do you have a clue what this might be or do you have a suggestion.
Thank you for all you do!
Lynn

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-24T16:29:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-yr-old-young-man-lost-color-vision.7701/post-90732>

yep......My bet is he got blue light toxic and destroyed several of his light NT's rhodopsin and opsin in his retina that cover color vision. It likely extended out from these cones and now got to his black membrane in his eye. This is a junction between two semiconductors in the eye........Brian D. Josephson predicted that certain types of super currents would pass between two semiconductors separated by a thin insulating layer that can influence weak magnetic fields and electric currents. He won a Nobel for this in 1971 after he wrote about this in 1963. My bet is fMRI and Spect scanning were not done and they would show hypometabolism in his macula retina and in his lateral geniculate nucleus and in his posterior midline occipital lobes where color is coded in the occipital lobes. This is the same process as how macular degeneration occurs. There is a gradual loss of semiconductor cells in the macula and then the ganglion cells.......Loss of the thin insulator causes avalanche collapse and loss of superconduction in the CNS. That is a possibility if nothing anatomically has showed up on CT or MRI......you must begin to look for the metabolic cues to source the issue. A squid magnetotomer would also show abnormal signals in the eye and the LGN and in V1 portions of the occipital lobes if I am correct. If he is a physics PHD he will understand the physics of what I am saying here.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-25T04:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-yr-old-young-man-lost-color-vision.7701/post-90773>

Just so you know there is a correlate for this in real life from NASA.......the astronauts went up into the ISS and they came back reporting in the mid nineties that when they closed their eyes they ocassionally would see sparks and flashes of light inside their closed eyes........these were particles ejected from the sun in the solar wind that their photo-chemicals were triggered to respond to. They are highly energized photo electric particles normally blocked out by our magnetic field......in space there is no protection. The 20 yr old environment likely was also highly charged and over came the Earth's protective magnetic field.......and he went color blind because the energy in these particles permanently damaged his macula from their energy, their EMF energy. ......see action potentials in nerve must respond all or none to a photon/or charged electron.......but the outside portion of the retina has other connective cells called ganglion cells that are part of the connective tissue framework of the CNS. They also respond.......to these bursts of energy to the SCN and Pineal gland in order to tell quantum time.........be sure even a blind person can sense light to create a circadian rhythm.......My bet is this 20 yr old slowly ruined his retina......with lots of highly charge particles from techno gadgets.......and then that fateful day at 20 yr old........he lost it because of another burst of highly charged particles zapped parts of macula.......where color vision receptors are housed.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-25T04:47:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-yr-old-young-man-lost-color-vision.7701/post-90776>

Interesting. Sometimes when I am going to bed, navigating in very low light with my eyes opened wide, I can see a flash of light. In this flash I can see my eyes -- the whites of my eyes, and the darker circle which would be the iris and pupils. This doesn't sound good.

### Lynn RN — 2013-07-25T07:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/20-yr-old-young-man-lost-color-vision.7701/post-90784>

**Thanks so much**

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=90773)
>
> Just so you know there is a correlate for this in real life from NASA.......the astronauts went up into the ISS and they came back reporting in the mid nineties that when they closed their eyes they ocassionally would see sparks and flashes of light inside their closed eyes........these were particles ejected from the sun in the solar wind that their photo-chemicals were triggered to respond to. They are highly energized photo electric particles normally blocked out by our magnetic field......in space there is no protection. The 20 yr old environment likely was also highly charged and over came the Earth's protective magnetic field.......and he went color blind because the energy in these particles permanently damaged his macula from their energy, their EMF energy. ......see action potentials in nerve must respond all or none to a photon/or charged electron.......but the outside portion of the retina has other connective cells called ganglion cells that are part of the connective tissue framework of the CNS. They also respond.......to these bursts of energy to the SCN and Pineal gland in order to tell quantum time.........be sure even a blind person can sense light to create a circadian rhythm.......My bet is this 20 yr old slowly ruined his retina......with lots of highly charge particles from techno gadgets.......and then that fateful day at 20 yr old........he lost it because of another burst of highly charged particles zapped parts of macula.......where color vision receptors are housed.

Thank you Dr K,
I have passed the information on to the young man and I am in hopes he will come on board your ship of knowledge and contact you.
Lynn RN

# Thread: Beverly
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beverly.7705/>

Hi Dr K and members. I have been posting to Dr K on the not so hard truth about hair loss article and I am now posting my labs here. My name is Beverly. Caucasian female early 40's. 5'3 125 lbs. Haven't been feeling well for several years.

Diffuse hair loss since late teens. Nausea, fatigue, difficulty swallowing. Had a scope for the swallowing and a thyroid scan 8 yrs ago and I have 1 cold nodule. Gallbladder out in 2000 at 28. 3 miscarriages in a row in 2010. I also have 3 children. ACDF c6/c7 2012. My diet has always been your typical western high carb diet.

A few months ago I started getting dry red eyes. Then 2 weeks ago I started having finger joint pain and hip pain. I also still have fatigue nausea and difficulty swallowing too. I have to double swallow. I am doing a gluten challenge right now and in a month I will do a celiac panel.
