# Thread: New with questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AnnieW — 2013-07-24T23:53:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90753>

Hi Friends,

I found out about this website through MDA. I am working my way through the monster thread. Lots of good info in there!

I'm 31, female, 343 lbs . I'm 6' tall and classic apple shape. I have severe PCOS and I started showing symptoms when I was about 8. I am insulin resistant but not technically diabetic yet according to my doctor. I just went to see her and she scheduled me for some labs but I don't think they are as comprehensive as what Dr. K recommends.

I started the reset on Monday but today I caved and ate pizza. I could have kicked myself after all the $ I spent on good food over the weekend. I'm hoping to get a lot of good info and support on this forum, maybe even join the members area if I can stick with this!

I had a question starting out. I saw the food list Dr. K posted on his blog. I was wondering if we had a more comprehensive list with specific vegetables and things listed. For example, I had specific questions on whether or not things like carrots, avocados, and sunflower seeds were ok. Also should I be using macadamia nut oil or olive oil for salad dressings? How about coconut manna? Is that ok to have during mealtimes? Should we limit it? I think I could stomach that a lot more than straight oil. I don't drink coffee and last night I just took some hard off the spoon and it took everything in my willpower to get it down LOL. Hopefully I can get used to it. Also does it matter what kind of bacon I buy? I've been buying the nitrate/nitrite free bacon which is really expensive.

Overall I have had really good energy and sleeping well the last two nights. The only thing is that I have been dealing with some diarrhea (TMI). Not sure if it's the coconut oil or not. I might be eating 3 tbs at the most during the day. Wish I hadn't screwed up today. Tomorrow is a new day!

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T01:40:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90757>

Hi and welcome. I've been following JAcks protocols since summer 2011. Its a long road, and there are days you may not be "perfect" in your diet. that's ok. just keep focusing on the BAB, meal timing, and eliminating artificial light at night. If you're having cravings, up both protein and FAT at breakfast.

BAB should mostly be Protein/fat.

Lunch and dinner should be protein/fat/carbs - eat as much seafood as you can - preferably shellfish and crustaceans.

skip the sunflower seeds. for some people coconut mana can trigger binges... if you don't drink coffee or green tea - try some camomile made bullet proof style 1-2tbs of grassfed butter/ghee, and 1-2tb of coconut oil or MCT. blend it up and drink.

as for bacon, buy what you can afford. -we buy the thick cut kind from Costco... same price as 1lb of local free-range hogs but for 4lbs!

Getting into the grove with the LR for some can take time...but this isn't a sprint to the finish. this is a long distance journey to optimal health.

post questions. there's great people here on the thread.

as for the labs- when you get them back, after you've digested some of the info here... we can give you a list to ask your doc to run for the next set of labs, on the day you discuss your current set with her.

welcome

gretchen

### caroline — 2013-07-25T02:48:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90759>

Welcome Annie ....Gretchen has lots of great advice. Try not to stress - just do what you can and keep reading and reading and then asks lots of questions. There is so much to take in ....but it does start to sink in. I need to read the blogs many times to "get it" .....as you start connecting dots you will have lots of "ah ha" moments and then you will read an old blog and go - oh - now I get it!

### AnnieW — 2013-07-25T17:05:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90804>

Thanks so much for replying! It's good to know I don't have to spend a bajillion dollars on bacon anymore. I love Costco.

Can we use coconut flour? I use it sometimes in crab cakes and to dust fish filets before pan-frying. Also, what's bullet-proof coffee? Is that Dr. K's book? I need to order that...

Thank you ladies again for replying. I have lost 4 lbs in 4 days and although I know I shouldn't be looking at the scale this soon, I was feeling so good that I couldn't help it!

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T17:22:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90807>

yes you can use coconut flour...

here's how to make your coffee bulletproof....

[http://www.bulletproofexec.com/how-to-make-your-coffee-bulletproof-and-your-morning-too/](http://www.bulletproofexec.com/how-to-make-your-coffee-bulletproof-and-your-morning-too/)

focus on how you feel, and how your clothes fit. not the scale. If you can put the away you'll find your focus will shift to how you feel....

I recommend keeping a journal of how you feel... things that are going on etc... also start tracking your FBG through out your cycle in the am... I bet you see a rise at one point in your cycle.... due to the PCOS....

### AnnieW — 2013-07-25T18:07:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90812>

Hi Gretchen,

Sorry, what is FBG? Is that good to have a rise or no? I am definitely feeling better. Well, today not so much. I do have a headache and I'm tired. I think it's because yesterday I fell off the Leptin Reset wagon and then let it run over me. LOL. I did have two burgers with three eggs this morning. I can tell it's not enough food though because I'm starting to feel grouchy. I was thinking of adding avocado and coconut manna to breakfast. I wanted something in addition to the meat. I understand about the manna inducing cravings. It's obviously popular because it's out of stock LOL. So is it ok to add avocado to breakfast? I ran out of bacon so I have to get some more. I do have some seafood in the freezer but since we're on a budget I'd prefer to save that for dinner.

Can we use the bulletproof method for regular hot tea as well? I've never had green tea but we have some other tea at home that my husband drinks.

Thanks so much again, hope you don't get tired of me asking questions.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T18:15:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90815>

FBG = fasting blood glucose PCOS women make excess tesosterone in their ovaries, and at the same time they make more insulin than they need in their ovaries as well. there are points in your cycle - that if you journal and track your FBG - you'll begin to see a trend and can pinpoint where in your cycle your Testosterone is rising.

Cook your burgers in coconut oil. and go ahead and have your avocado - be aware though one runs towards 20g. so it will eat into your carbs for the day... you really want to keep your carbs btwn 25-50g for the day.

yes you can make your tea bulletproof- its a great fat delivery system...

### AnnieW — 2013-07-25T18:27:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90819>

Wow I feel stupid. FBG is so obvious now LOL. Yes, my testosterone is definitely out of control. I have severe PCOS. My facial hair is insane and my head hair is thinning so quickly. I'll be surprised if I have any left by the time I'm 40.  I've been off birth control and spironolactone for about 2 years now. When I went off the pill and my weight skyrocketed, that's when things got really bad.

So if I see a trend in the testosterone rising, what should I do?

Thanks again for your help!

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T18:36:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-with-questions.7707/post-90820>

if you FBG is rising at a specific point in your cycle, and your eating high fat/moderate protein/low carb - you can pinpoint when the T is rising.

one option is using metformin to control this... you might also want to find a BHRT Doc who can help you as well.... you most likely need Bioidentical progesterone to help you reverse the upside down Pg E2 you've got going on.

for now just focus on the Leptin reset, and follow circadian cycles... take small steps...

# Thread: Sepsis......is it just like levee one says?  A lack of energy?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sepsis-is-it-just-like-levee-one-says-a-lack-of-energy.7709/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-25T03:08:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sepsis-is-it-just-like-levee-one-says-a-lack-of-energy.7709/post-90763>

Researchers looking for a test to predict whether someone has a potentially deadly condition called sepsis have made a surprise finding â€“ they can predict who will die from it.

Their test accurately picked out who would develop severe sepsis and die, versus those who had fairly innocuous infections and lived.

They hope they can develop it into a tool that will help doctors decide who needs immediate hospitalization and intensive treatment, versus those who can tough it out. Eventually, they hope they may be able to predict who might be susceptible to infections of all kinds.

â€œThe test is looking for a signature that will guide a physician â€“ is this patient going to have a catastrophic illness or is he going to have a mild infection?â€ said Dr. Stephen Kingsmore, president and chief executive of the National Center for Genome Resources in Santa Fe, N.M., who led the team.

Thomas J. Gagliano / Lovelace Respiratory Institute
Researchers have developed a new test that can identify molecular signatures that may indicate whether a patient will die of sepsis, a life-threatening infection that affects 1.6 million people treated in U.S. hospitals each year.
Sepsis is the 10th leading cause of death in the United States. Itâ€™s still a mystery to doctors. At first defined as a blood infection, itâ€™s now more widely understood as a devastating reponse to infections and injuries of many different kinds. Patients can go from being perfectly well to being on the verge of death. They can lose limbs, and die within hours.

The number of people treated in the hospital for sepsis in the U.S. has more than doubled in recent years, to about 1.6 million a year, according to a 2011 report from the Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality. Thereâ€™s not a good test for sepsis, so itâ€™s not clear how many people who develop sepsis die of it.

Kingsmoreâ€™s team started looking for that answer. They took a unique approach, running a test for just about every protein and metabolite in human blood. Thatâ€™s tens of thousands of compounds, in a study akin to mapping the entire human genome, says Kingsmore.

â€œYou measure everything and you see what falls out. That is what is kind of cool about this study,â€ Kingsmore said in a telephone interview.

They tested 1,152 people who developed sepsis from 2005 to 2009 at Henry Ford Hospital in Detroit, and Duke University Hospital and Durham Veterans Affairs Medical Center in North Carolina. The patients had their blood sampled when they first came in to be treated, and then later on, also.

â€œWhat we found in this study was really surprising to us,â€ Kingsmore told NBC News. â€œThe study was designed to identify a signature that would just tell us if it was sepsis or not because it so difficult for a doctor to identify sepsis. Much to our surprise, as we got into the study and identified the results, we found that we have these really big differences between these patients.â€

The patients who developed severe sepsis or died had different levels of many different blood chemicals, the team reports in the journal Science Translational Medicine.

â€œThere were hundreds or differences,â€ Kingsmore said. Surprisingly, it didnâ€™t matter which bacteria was causing the infection â€“ the signature was similar. There was a clear pattern, and it involved how cells use energy.

â€œThey are measures of the bodyâ€™s ability to metabolize, to generate energy,â€ Kingsmore said. â€œOne group had a robust body response to serious infection. They generate a lot of energy and they have a fever. In ways we donâ€™t understand, that contributes to their survival.â€

But many of the patients did not do well. Their blood tests, taken when they first showed up at the hospital, showed there were already differences. â€œIn people with bad outcomes, they donâ€™t have that response," Kingsmore said. "They have a kind of partial response. They mobilize fatty acids, but their cells are not able to harvest that energy.â€

Much more testing is required to find out just what is happening in these patients, noted Kingsmore, who also heads the pediatric genomic medicine program at Childrenâ€™s Mercy Hospital in Kansas City.

But he believes it has great potential. â€œThis signature is going to be good for a broad spectrum of infectious diseases. That would include the flu and other severe viral infections as well as the common bacterial infections and even infections with fungi,â€ he said.

It may even be a signal for patients who will die of trauma, perhaps after a car accident, he said.

The team tested their findings over and over again to be sure, said Kingsmore. The work lasted eight years.

â€œWe talk about survival of the fittest and here have defined the fittest in a new way, a really surprising way,â€ he said. â€œThe people donâ€™t have the type of robust response that you need to survive an infection. The hope is we can reverse that if we identify the patients early,â€ he added.

â€œThe goal is not to tell a patient you are going to die.â€

Thatâ€™s a new challenge, because there are not any good drugs for sepsis. Thereâ€™s a device that identifies the most likely bacteria to cause sepsis, but no highly effective drugs. Patients get intravenous antibiotics and fluids, as well as careful monitoring, but can still die.

From here: [http://www.nbcnews.com/health/new-blood-test-might-predict-sepsis-deaths-6C10732135](http://www.nbcnews.com/health/new-blood-test-might-predict-sepsis-deaths-6C10732135)

### nonchalant — 2013-07-25T04:39:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sepsis-is-it-just-like-levee-one-says-a-lack-of-energy.7709/post-90774>

I now see a lack of energy behind so many problems we have. Disease, depression, procrastination, apathy, economic, political and religious issues, lack of imagination, cookbook medicine.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T16:20:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sepsis-is-it-just-like-levee-one-says-a-lack-of-energy.7709/post-90802>

This article really hit home for me. b/c we recently lost our 3yo lab to sepis and a necrotic bowl. As I understand how it happened he basically stopped functioning at the mitochondrial level.... for a while It looked like he was going to recover, from the surgery, and removal and re-attachment of small intestine - but in a short 3hrs he went from a 50/50 prognosis to a prognosis of 30/70 and by the end of the day he was at a 10% survival rate. if we had elected a second surgery, he wouldn't have survived the anesthesia - he passed when they gave him a small amount before the phenobarbital injection.

so, while we no longer have a dog, and have a huge vet bill...but I have fully come to understand how it all links back together..

# Thread: So you think light does not matter?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-light-does-not-matter.7710/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-25T03:38:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-light-does-not-matter.7710/post-90765>

[http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2013/07/130720-night-dark-light-pollution-science-stars/](http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2013/07/130720-night-dark-light-pollution-science-stars/)
Paul Bogard
for National Geographic
Published July 19, 2013

We live immersed in artificial light.

Astronomers rate the darkness of our skies on a scale (the Bortle Scale) of 9 (brightest) to 1 (darkest). Most of us spend our lives in the radiance of levels 5 through 8, only rarely venturing into areas ranked 3 or darker. Because of the rapid growth of light pollution over recent decades, most Americans under 40 have never known real darkness. All over the globe our nights are growing brighter, and almost nowhere are they growing darker.

We are just beginning to learn the true cost of all this light. Studies increasingly link our overuse of light at night with health concerns such as sleep disorders, diabetes, obesity, and cancer. Other studies report the damaging ecological consequences, the tremendous waste of energy, and even the decrease in safety and security. But the steady loss of darkness from our lives is not easily quantified, for like the similarly endangered qualities of solitude and quiet, the true value of darkness is often intangible.

Take a brilliantly starry sky. Since the beginning of time, a sky plush with stars was part of the common human experience. Everywhere on Earth, on most nights, our ancestors came face to face with the universe. This experience influenced their religious beliefs, mythologies, artâ€”their very understanding of their place in creation. Today, because of light pollution, two-thirds of those in the United States and western Europe live where they cannot see the Milky Way, and 99 percent of us live under skies polluted by light.

For the tens of millions who live under a night sky showing 25 stars or fewer, it is nearly impossible to imagine a natural sky of some 2,500 individual stars backed by great swathes of uncountable billions. Our night sky continues to shape us, but now it is the absence of the universe around us that influences our religious beliefs, our myths, our impulse to create. We are being shaped by a diminished experience of darkness, and most of us don't even know what we are missing.

Imagine the world without van Gogh's "Starry Night," Chopin's "Nocturnes," Beethoven's "Moonlight Sonata." Imagine the world without the astral myths of the Greeks and every other ancient and primitive civilization, without countless biblical stories that rely on darkness, without St. John of the Cross's book Dark Night of the Soul. Imagine the world without the gathered inspiration of countless generations before us looking into the night sky and being filled with wonder, with questions, with desire to know and understand. Most of us have lost this opportunity to be awed at the sight, to feel similarly inspiredâ€”to create, to change our lives, to gain a greater understanding of our God.

Our Milky Way galaxy is home to several hundred billion stars, and the universe home to several hundred billion other galaxies. Because of light pollution we no longer have a nightly reminder of how small we are, nor of how large. A sky wiped clear of stars tempts us to inflate our importance, to imagine humanity as the center of all things. Face to face with the endless immensity of the universe, we have the chance to know humility. But we might also realize the true largeness of our living on this beautiful Earth, and realize that we have an enormous responsibility to care, that there is no other place to go, that home is here.

Night's natural darkness can teach us about metaphorical darkness as well. Ancient cultures included in their hero myths an experience of the darkâ€”the dark wood, the underworldâ€”for to become fully human was to acknowledge darkness as part of life's journey. It certainly is todayâ€”we often live in the darkness of not knowing, unable to see the future, with no crystal ball. Genuine faith isn't about finding clarity, but rather living with doubt. Those who profess to have no doubt, to have all the answers, to believe theirs is the only truthâ€”they ignore the reality of metaphorical darkness. All life on Earth evolved in both bright days and dark nights, and we need both for optimal health. This is as true for our spirit as for our body.

And what of beauty? "Everyone needs beauty as well as bread," wrote John Muir, and darkness is rich with this intangible resource. Lighting designers in Paris understand that without darkness, there is no "city of light," and work constantly to create their city's atmospheric beauty by subtly mixing artificial light with darkness. And with night's moonlit geographies, its scents of sage-infused desert rain and autumn fires, its pulsing cricket symphonies punctuated by a loon's solo call on a northern lake, natural darkness offers endless beauties of its own.

Yet we live immersed in artificial light. Much of this lighting is wholly unnecessary, born of habit and lack of awareness. So let us become aware: Simply by shielding our existing lights we could significantly reduce their negative effects on our body, our mind, our soul. Artificial light at night is a miracle, a wonder, a quality that enriches our lives. But the same has always been true of darkness, and can be again.

Paul Bogard is the author of The End of Night: Searching for Natural Darkness in an Age of Artificial Light, which was published this month.

### SeaHorse — 2013-07-25T03:54:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-light-does-not-matter.7710/post-90767>

In Derreck Jansen's book "A Language Older than Words" he describes how during his horrific childhood the relationship he had with the stars at night saved him. Without access to the natural world (at a quantum, mythic, spiritual, physical and biological level) we starve. And now our world is so terribly changed..
I'm grateful that I can still see the Milky Way on a clear night.

### caroline — 2013-07-25T03:58:52+0300

Thaankyou for posting that ....... You made me "aware" a year or so ago ....grateful as ever...

I grew up on a farm and this made me realise and remember what a huge part the night sky and moon and stars were in our daily lives. We would spend hours around a bonfire looking at the stars. I didn't think that I had many happy memories of childhood, but that is absolutely one that I took for granted - thanks for reminding me.

### JanSz — 2013-07-25T04:27:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-light-does-not-matter.7710/post-90770>

From age 5 to 13 I have spend on the farm, far away from light sources. 1945-1953
At the time I have not thought about it as anything unusual to have dark nights, specially when without moon.
Life in big cities began, light is everywhere. At night it actually interferes with seeing.
1990-1997 I got my biggest boat, 29' fisherman.
At night, 20 miles off shore from Point Pleasant it is still bright at night from NYC lights.
I have spend many nights fishing Monster Ledge 20 miles off Pt Pleasant.
But the biggest fun I always had when just two of us, me and DW would go 90 miles out to the mouth of Hudson Canyon.
When you get there, vision is accommodated and all you need is few stars and it is easy to see for miles.

My 40yo children had no opportunity to see dark nights.

.

### gsteppe14@gmail.com — 2013-08-21T07:41:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beverly-posting-labs.7712/post-93325>

I agree with JanSz. They don't jump out at you but when you look at the pattern, they're all low-normal. Not enough to concern a busy physician but if you're a health gumshoe with time to burn like us ... and we all are in this forum! ... then they're vital clues. I think it may have to do with the positive ANA susceptible for Scleroderma. The other antibody test excluded Sjogren's, Sjogren's-come-lupus, and RA to some degree. Why did your doc test you for these antibodies anyway?

Is your esophagus dry? Is the inside of your mouth also dry? How about your eyes? Do you have joint-related symptoms aside from the lone finger? Muscle carmps? Chronic fatigue? Did you ever find out the reasons behind your miscarriages? Were you ever tested for antiphospholipids?

You might as well get some of the other antibodies excluded. Your ENA excluded some but not all. The most important of which is Anti DS-DNA, B Centromere, HLA-B 27 and thyroid antibodies, TSI, TPO/TG. You definitely need to get the DS DNA tested to exclude SLE, especially if you had anti-phospholipids and miscarriages in late trimesters. You're very midly anemic but this could also be due to autoimmunity (rule out w DS DNA). The other clue is your lowish WBC and non-lymphs, also autoimmune signatures.

Whys is your creatinine so low? Did you drink too much water before testing? Where is your BUN? You seem to be ketogenic so that should be high.

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=90966)
>
> All above is low. There are preliminary suggestions there, but for now you may want to wait with any supplementation until we get test results.

### gsteppe14@gmail.com — 2013-08-21T07:52:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beverly-posting-labs.7712/post-93326>

Your cholesterol is a non-issue when you don't know the components. Your very low trigs suggest you're ketogenic. That's all I can say. Your Neuts are also a non-issue since it's based on your low-normal total WBCs. Your lymph % is high so one of your non-lymphs is bound to be low. Your non-lymphs are 4.2-2.2 = 2.0. As you go below 2.0, you become more susceptible to infection. Not too low now but I'd keep an eye on your total WBCs for now.

You're very midly anemic but this could also be due to autoimmunity (rule out w DS DNA). The other clue is your lowish WBC and non-lymphs, also autoimmune signatures.
>
> Whys is your creatinine so low? Did you drink too much water before testing? Where is your BUN? You seem to be ketogenic so that should be high.

### gsteppe14@gmail.com — 2013-08-23T06:04:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/beverly-posting-labs.7712/post-93469>

You're ANA positive and the ENA test excluded your seropositivity for Sm, SS-A, SS-B, Scl-70, Jo-1 and RNP. That's a select few and those are mostly Sjogren's,Lupus and some other antibodies. But that's not all such antibodies. I keep saying you need the DS DNA and antiphospho because they could be related to pregnancy complications. There are countless antibodies. Just because you're negative doesn't mean you don't have the disease. Especially if you have symptoms, you are probably in the process of becoming seropositive, especially for Sjogren's, whose symptoms you seem to have. Whoever tested you and said you don't have autoimmunity is an idiot. You may not have systemic scleroderma, but you could have Crest. You're seronegative for RA based on CCP, but you could be positive based on RA Factor and symptoms, which also was not tested. Unless you go to a knowledgeable rheumy, you'll be chasing your tail like this. Autoimmune patients with connective tissue issues get tested every 3 months to see what else becomes positive. They piggyback one after another. It's not like you test once and it's all clear. Go see a rheumy. Tell her your symptoms. Tell her you need the remaining antibody tests to rule out SLE, RA and other issues. Funny you mention C6/C7. You need C3/C4. See how low they are. I bet they're below or at the low end of the reference range. Also need to test TPO/TG/TSI, as they overlap frequently with ANA. At this point, I think you need a rheumy more than a neuro. What you need are more antibody tests, not MRI.

> [Beverly said:](/goto/post?id=93456)
>
> Thanks for having a look at my labs. As far as I am aware, and someone can step in and correct me if I'm mistaken, but part of my ena test was for scleroderma specifically. The scl-70. Mine was negative. And the reason I was tested originally was to humour me. The dr said my results were normal too, but I don't feel well. I felt off and tired and had the sore finger joint first.
>
> As far as sx now, I have sore finger joints, at least 4, I have also been having difficulty picking up small things with my fingers. I have sore hip joints and when I wear my flip flop sandals my toe joints feel strange now. I do have dry red eyes. I have tingling that goes down my left side from chin to my toes.
>
> And my esophagus is not dry nor is my mouth. I was not tested for antiphospholipids. My miscarriages were not worked up, they were early miscarriages, 7 weeks, 8 weeks and 12 weeks. They put it down to my age, I was late thirties. No muscle cramps. I do have chronic fatigue though. But I have had that for years.
>
> I saw my neurosurgeon yesterday for my 1 yr checkup from my acdf c6/c7 and he ordered an urgent head and neck mri and is sending me to a neurologist. I was hoping maybe these sx were coming from my c5/c6 that also has a bulge but I am now wondering if these problems are more suited to a rheum instead. My sx seem complicated and the joint sx don't seem to fit with something neurological.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonal-eating-in-brazil-and-thermogenesis-without-bathtub.7713/post-90794>

hi eveyone! i am a 21 year old girl that has been eating a traditional diet since i discovered the work of weston a price about 4 years ago. i listened to jacks interview over at sean croxtons and from what i understood he said we should eat seasonally (and mostly try to be in ketosis?). i am from sweden but now i live in rio de janeiro, the seasonal available food there is much different from the one where i come from. should i follow the brazilian rhythm or should i follow the scandinavian one and go into ketosis in the winter months (despite the 90 degree heat in rio) and then in the summer months add apples, pears and berries (which are pretty much the fruits available where i come from), in brazil there is fruit and starches available all year round.

then i have a question about cold thermogenesis, i would really like to try it but i don't have a bathtub. i live near the sea but the temperature rarely goes down below 70 degrees. could i do it with cold water in the shower? even if it's not possible to get cold adapted that way could having cold showers from now on have any benefits?

/pauline

Need really some help with the labs I just got. I could spend months looking up all of this info (if I can even find it) and see how it all relates but I am afraid I would still get it wrong or could have been doing something in addition to the Leptin reset to get the hormones fixed. I am not sure if my doctor would be able to help me decipher all of this but he is willing to listen so if I can get some help in figuring all of this out, if I need more help he will help.

I just had some blood test done and it showed that I am low on leptin

â€¢ 6.3 with 8 being the lower end of the range (awake for 5 hours fasting).

â€¢ DHEA (ng/dL) was 233 on a scale of 102 â€“ 1185 so I think I am low on this. Trying to up my supplements.

â€¢ BUN (mg/dL) was 17, down from 18 a year ago but still high on a scale of 7-19.

â€¢ Vitamin D 25-OH was 46 down from 56, 18 months ago on a scale of 30-100. I will try upping my vitamin D3 supplements. Plus is it summer here and the first results were from the middle of January.

â€¢ HDL â€“ 85 which is really good on the scale of 35-65

â€¢ T3 free is 2.3 pg/mL, down from 2.8, 18 months ago on a scale of 2.3-4.2. I am on 90 mg of Armour once a day and am a little worried that this dropped even more. I asked for a reverse T3 test to be done but it was not. I can ask him to see about upping the dose but I want to find out why they dropped and if I can get it better without the increase in meds.

â€¢ B12 is 546 pg/mL on a scale of 200-1100. Not bad but I think it should be better considering I am taking a B supplement and eat lots of meat.

â€¢ Ferritin is 49 ng/mL on a scale of 10-232. I think this is pretty low and I am taking some iron supplements already and eat red meat.

And then there was all of the other hormone testing. I have read that what the optimal levels are depends on where you are in the cycle and age. I just turned 40 and the tests were taking 14 days after the 1 day of the start of my cycle or about 1 day after ovulation (I have an app that tracks this). I was not given what the ranges were for these so i had to look them up so not sure how accurate they are.

â€¢ Progesterone was 4.1 ng/mL on a scale of 1.5 to 15 (from what I could find)

â€¢ Prenenolone lvl was 39 ng/mL and from what I could find 200 is optimal?

â€¢ Estrogen was 108 pg/mL and from what I could find 50 to 400 is optimal based on where one is in the cycle.

â€¢ Testoserone was 38 ng/dL on a scale between 2 and 45. Not sure if this is too high for a female?

Should also say that for years I was a chronic under eater and over exerciser and I think that is what tanked my leptin. I would rarely get hungry even after a hard workout. I am thinking about doing another round of HCG in September but with my hormones being so wacky, not sure if the weight I would lose would stay off again.

If anyone has any specific blogs that they can point me too I would greatly appreciate it since I am having a hard time finding ones that relate to low leptin.

Also if anyone knows of a great "non-traditional" doctor in the Minneapolis area that they could recommend that would be great.

### MsYo — 2013-07-28T03:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/really-need-some-feedback-please.7714/post-91026>

Hormone testing should be done on days 1-3 or day 21 of your cycle for accurate results.
Your iron is on the lower end. I use SSS tonic (liquid) to help me with past anemia issues.
You should be supplementing with Bcomplex. Jack recommends a higher B12.
Definitely get some sun (D3) and consider a liquid Vit d3 for dosing. 10000iu would be a good start. I use Country Life liquid. My D3 went from 25 to 75+ in less than a year after finding this site/forum.
Progesterone is lower than it should be. BHRT will likely help. Also check out the iodine thread. Iodine will help in hormonal issues.
You will likely need more accurate labs for hormone values. The reverse T3 is needed too. Check out the all things T3 thread for more info on the targets. Be patient with the process and learn as much as you can from this site. No worries, someone will respond and help.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-28T16:12:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/really-need-some-feedback-please.7714/post-91036>

Simplicity is ultimately a matter of focus and vision. The eye sees more than the mind can comprehend, and we go through life self-blinded to much that lies before us. Simplicity is a great virtue but it requires hard work to achieve it and education to appreciate it. And to make matters worse: complexity sells better in today's world. Every modern food convenience comes with a severe health sacrifice with time. We need to become entangled with nature and her rules for health. Simple can be harder than complex: You have to work hard to get your thinking clean to make it simple. Life itself is simple...but no one has every said it is designed to be easy. Once you embrace nature's simplicity you begin to sense its worth. In the end, once you get there, you find you have tremendous leverage over disease and health. Life obstacles that were once considered mountains, become mole hills and speed bumps. That is the magic of evolutionary design.

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=90803)
>
> I just turned 40 and the tests were taking 14 days after the 1 day of the start of my cycle or about 1 day after ovulation

When you want to optimize your prog & estradiol,
do blood test on time periods where there is little of expected variability
from one day to the next one.
Day #14 does not qualify.
It is in section of menstrual cycle where large gradients happen.

Good times to take blood for this type of testing are
days 2-4 (low levels)
and
days 20-22 (high levels)

To provide systematic approach,
between the two, start investigation and optimization with days 2-4.

Only,
after you figured out and optimized beginning of cycle, you may put more attention on days 20-22.

.....
.......

# Thread: WHat type of mattress do you use
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T18:03:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90811>

Ok.

we've got the magnetico sleep pad thread... I'm interested in seeing what mattresses everyone here is using?

Currently we've got a select comfort Sleep Number bed - While I love it, I'm beginning to think its BAD for me... all that electricity need to fill/decrease the airbladders. I keep it set at one level... and then we turn the breakers in the room off at night...

So, our next big purchase will most likely be a new mattress. One I'm looking at is tempurpedic brand. I traditionally love a firm mattress...

So what mattresses is everyone using? what's your pros/cons about it?

### SeaHorse — 2013-07-25T19:38:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90829>

We had a a tempurpedic mattress for 10 years. In the beginning I loved it, but they do break down, they are made with material that off gasses for quite a while and I think they increase heat a lot. I wouldn't buy the whole mattress now, though we still have a topper. We have pretty run of the mill mattresses, but I've put the magnetico pad underneath, a smaller tempurpedic topper and a really thick wool pad on top. I like the way the wool dissipates the heat and provides extra cushioning which prevents pressure pain in my hips. I will buy an whole mattress made with cotton and wool when I can....I prefer being surrounded by natural materials when I sleep.

### Shijin13 — 2013-07-25T19:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90831>

We've had our sleep# mattress for close to 10yrs... and its started degrading... the controls barely work... etc... and the cost to replace them both is the same as a really cheap mattress from a local store...

### Lava — 2013-07-25T20:04:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90833>

We use a tempurpedic mattress also with a wool topper...It does cause hip pressure for sure..I need to find something to put on top of the mattress to stop that but it's hard to find something that is natural and thick enough to stop the pain..The pressure wakes me up at night  I am still looking at the Magnetico but the shipping to Hawaii is almost as much as the pad itself.......

### caroline — 2013-07-26T00:36:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90853>

I have a latex mattress. While I love how comfy it is - it is way too hot. It has a really gorgeous cushiony topper but that is where the problem lies. I was able to turn mine over and that helps a lot. A friend bought one because we loved our so much and they had the same problem - way too hot- and they aren't able to turn theirs over. Interestingly - when we were buying it - my husband didn't want the topper - but I insisted because it was so lovely! Big mistake! He always had great instincts.

There is a mattress site that I will try and find again -,the guy knew a lot about mattresses. People were beside themselves trying to figure what to do about mattresses being too hot - and spending a fortune on toppers to try to dissipate the heat.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-26T00:39:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90856>

Royal Pedic.......old school and very heavy and durable. It is the same ones used in the White House.

### Hope — 2013-07-26T02:11:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90871>

I love my organic life kind mattress we got as a gift. It's an organic latex and you can adjust firmness. I must say though, when we are due for another one, in 2023 (LOL), I am hoping we can get a Tempurpedic. I have slept on it a few nights and seem to sleep amazing on it. a chiropractic said it's good too. I try to avoid anything with metals in it, but not sure it makes a difference.

Some hotels offer a tempurpedic - such as the Princess Royale in Ocean City MD. But I think trying what's best for you is key......N=1 I guess

### cinnamon — 2013-07-26T03:15:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-90880>

My brother got himself a tempur-pedic mattress a couple of years ago. He spent a lot on it. And he absolutely hates it!

He told me that both he and my sister-in-law thought it was amazing at first. They slept very well and were very pleased with the purchase. Then about 3 or 4 months later, they started having trouble. He said it actually does not support the body well because of how you sort of sink into it. They both have been having back and neck problems and do not wake rested. They also mentioned it's not good for sex.

I think it's only because it was such a big investment that they haven't replaced it already. Given the importance of sleep, if it was me, I would have given up a while back.

Anyway, I know people do rave about the tempur-pedic. If you are reading reviews, consider if they are new users or long-term users. My brother would have given it 5 stars in the first month or two; now he wishes he hadn't bought it.

### cjhf — 2013-08-22T16:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-type-of-mattress-do-you-use.7716/post-93407>

If you are still researching mattresses this is one I learned about last year [http://www.intellibed.com/store/](http://www.intellibed.com/store/)

After 3 bedroom switches I now have a Praga portable double bed frame, 20 g magnetico and the 4 inch intellibed topper.
I tried the topper on a regular mattress but I like firmer and didn't care for that arrangement. It is not very high now but it is in my lower level and works for me.
This week I added some foam pads I had available to add about another inch between magnetico and intellibed.
Since there needs to be 4 in above I was concerned body compression was not optimizing magnetico.
I may have gotten a regular bed but since I must move things myself I got the more portable topper.

There is a webinar for intellibed on website for [www.greensmoothiegirl.com](http://www.greensmoothiegirl.com) Type intellibed in search field and click on sleep webinar link in article.
After initial talk the company gives info.
If this is of interest to anyone, for 2 weeks there is 10% discount plus free mattress pad with coupon code greensmoothiegirl

An excerpt from QB 4: "a sphere within a sphere creates and electrical tension or a difference.............a mitochondria within a cell is also a sphere within a sphere.......surprise corollary. It too resonates in the schumann or ELF band to control energy oscillations along the ECT. The more energy inefficient we are the more reverse flow we get in ECT and we get electron flow that results in backward flow and leakiness on cytochrome one. The flow cause a sharp change in the NAD+ and NADH ratio and results in superoxide formation. How you ask? When we eat food it is broken down to electrons from its substrate foodstuffs. The electron density of said foods are determined by which seasons they normally grow in. For example foods low in electron density grow in summer time because the power of the sun with water in our environment is offset by lower density of electrons in food. This is called energy balance and what leptin is an accountant for in the brain. When foods with lots of fructose or glucose are eaten a lot of NADH is made. When lots of NADH is around and we eat more foods, the electrons begin to access other points to the electron transport chain's CoEnQ10 coupling mechanism. These cytochromes are called electron transporting proteins that work via quantum tunneling mechanisms. Their names are flavoprotein dehydrogenase, mtG3Pdehydrogease, NADPH dehydrogenase for example. By transferring electrons from the food to the cytochrome it chemically reduces the proteins that CoEnQ10 couple to, and this promotes excessive reverse electron flow through complex I, superoxide generation and insulin resistance. This is if the person can not uncouple ECT. To uncouple requires UCP, high T3 levels, and leptin sensitivity. When you are LR, as I mentioned in the Oprah blog post.......you can't uncouple and you instead reverse the flow of electrons on your inner mitochondrial membrane and you develop Metabolic syndrome or diabetes."

I'd suggest you go re read this blog post.....[http://www.jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-4-metabolic-syndrome/](http://www.jackkruse.com/quantum-biology-4-metabolic-syndrome/)

# Thread: newbie called "Nyseides"  makes progress
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-called-nyseides-makes-progress.7721/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nyseides — 2013-07-26T06:01:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-called-nyseides-makes-progress.7721/post-90887>

I've never posted here before but have been reading for months. I'm 49, hashi's hypoT with persistent symptoms even on NDT when tsh totally suppressed, even when I've had decent Ft3 and 4 levels.

I have been low carb for 18 months. I lost weight but got clinical depressive relapse after 6 month VLC... argh. I'd "carb-up" , not be able to stop, and gain a bunch of weight. tried again. same. vlc x 6 months, become suicidal, eat for a month, gain 15 pounds. my all time high was 185 lbs. i'm 5'4 currently 160. bf around 38%.

read in kruse forums that i might have Iodine deficiency problem.

sure enough got tested at hakala labs and they said my I uptake only 69% (optimal 90). Don't have fluoridated water here so didn't test that. did test for bromide. My bromide excretion was 4.19mg/24 hrs (range <5mg/24 hours though interestingly, i have reverse slope hearing loss...the very very very rare form of hearing loss of the low sounds that bromide exposure as a kid can cause. so. i was interested if that was related...I dunno)

i did not begin Iodine supp yet because i knew in the past whenever my thyroid was working again my adrenals tanked, so I thought I would explore adrenal health first.

I began by following leptin reset x 10 weeks, autoimmune epipaleo diet, (i eat very high fat diet. moderate protein very low carb. daily i have seafood. eg canned oysters mussels, tuna. or frozen scallops, shrimp, fish, occassional fresh fish caught in northwestern ontario. trout. yum. also eat some beef pork baconfat. main fuel is coconut oil in tea, or i make it into "bars" like pemican. no eggs. i use goat milk for tea but no cheese. i eat fermented food eg miso, strained goat yogurt, gonna make pickles with the whey. i love kombucha, bubbies pickles $$. eat raw almonds. some greens, cucs, no goitrogen veggies...makes me very hypoT.

bought bluelight blocker glasses, in bed by 11, turned off the wifi. no clue what else I'm 'posed to do about emf's but willing. magnetico could be this month because i getting a bucket of money end of july. is that a priority? i am organic gardener and am already outside in the dirt every day.

have done CT x 2 weeks so far. shopping for neoprene.

pretty significant changes to my life. holy. giving up eggs was hard but i don't miss it now. giving up the microwave was hard but now i make tea in a copper teapot and cook every day in cast iron.

got first set of labs, and based on that, added supplements.
4 June 2013

ecg =normal

B12= 330(198-615 pmol/L)

(note started taking high potency liquid multi B with tons of B12, every second day)

hemoglobin = 137 (120-160 g/L)

ketones= >8 mmol/L) = hi

fasting glucose = 4.8 (3-6 mmol/L)

creatine =57 (45-97 umol/L)

ALT=28 (10-44 U/L)

alkaline phophatase = 63 (45-129 U/L)

ck = 52 (29-165 U/L)

LDL =3.44 mmol/L (high?)

HDL = 1.86

chol = 5.75

TG =1

(note these labs reflect me still on NDT 1.25 grains - signs of severe hypoT)

FT3= 4.9 (3.5-6.5 pmol/L)

TSH =<.05 mIU/L = lo

FT4 = 16 (9-23 pmol/L)

anti TPO = 60 = hi (5 IU/mL)

anti-TG = 746 = hih (<40 IU/mL)

(Note am open to the idea of low dose naltrexone therapy for autoantibodies. my doc said no. of course. she refuses to write t3 only therapy too, but she is willing to refer if I find someone who will. so far I have not found anyone in ontario. I'm in thunder bay. I asked jack on facebook if he has any buddies in toronto or winnipeg he said nope... i bought t3 online from mexico and began on my own. went off NDT.

ferritin =38 (10-291 ug/L)

(note started taking 350 mg ferrous gluconate 3 x/day with vit c, eating liver pate, cooking in my cast iron)

albumin creatinine ration U = .7 (<2.8 mg/mmol)

25 hyydroxy vit d = 108 (75-250 nmol/L)

(note starting taking 10,000 IU and more time outside, as soon as i wake up, 7 am go for a walk. got rid of sunglasses.)

part two of post to follow

### nyseides — 2013-07-26T06:24:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-called-nyseides-makes-progress.7721/post-90889>

was reading about Reverse T3, but could not find a way to get the test here. Made a lot of sense that I was not getting T3 into cells even though labs are normal.

Several years ago when I first went on dessicated thryoid, I was accidentally over-medicated, but for two years. It was a nightmare. Doc was not used to the dosing. I was asking for increases, and she agreed. I ended up with what i think was severe adrenal problems although I did not fail synacthen (ACTH test) so its not Addisons. But I never got Saliva so not sure what my cortisol status was. medical labs said normal right. i self treated with hydrocortisone x one year, took up yoga, and gardening. i think it saved my life. i recovered, but still had that terrible fear of adrenal crashes. I was in and out of emerg so many times. i resolved to never get over-medication on dessicated again. but never really got rid of my hypothyroid symptoms. (i suspect most conventionally treated hashi's never do, because of course medicine does not treat the illness, only symptoms.)

now, I'm determined to get rid of symptoms of hypothyroidism and adrenal weakness. i do not accept being depressed and fat and fatigued and balding unless there is nothing left to try. lol.

so I was reading with great interest about circadian T3 method to strengthen adrenals, and decided it was a good experiment. so july 8 i quit taking my dessicated thryoid, and went on T3 only.

began T3 only with 10 mcg , 1.5 hrs before waking. in one day on T3 only, I felt like my old self. I mean, holy relief from hypo-T symtpoms : feeling cold, brain fog, depression, joint pain, fatigue, carpal tunnel in both arms, constipation, swollen tongue. i can't tell if my hair is growing back but I feel so much better. just had TOM and it was normal. no more heavy heavy bleeding. like it was 5 times lighter than lately. also, no more migraines. wow. could T3 help so much so fast? a few days later I tried adding 5 mcg in afternoon. Bad idea.

I am having (what i think are ) terrible adrenal crashes since I switched to t3 only therapy. Its like any demand like stress or exercise, trying to start a new job, disagreement, sunshine, working outside when its warm, any demand on me and my BP crashes, blood sugar crashes, I get dizzy, diarrhea, stomach pain, shooting pain in the kidney area, nausea, weak, fainty, brittle, completely unable to cope with any stress, fall to pieces, cannot think, cannot stand. Lying down, my BP goes back up a little. I discovered drinking licorice tea (someone said 1 T powdered licorice = 22.5 mg hydrocortisone?) and miso broth (sodium?) and sips of coconut water (potassium) helps. I will start to feel better after a while, but it leaves me feeling like I got run over by a mac truck.

I'm a mess. but i don't see hydrocortisone as a good thing for me. when I was on it, (20mg) before is when my weight went up to 185. sucks. i ordered licorice root powdered at the health food store.

hscrp = 1 (<1 =low risk of cardiovascular event 1-3 =average risk, and >3 =high risk)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-26T15:01:05+0300

I agree with Caroline. You're in the right spot but you lack the info to create the wisdom you need right now.......you will get there if you put in the effort.

If you have a plan in life and you are using someone elses energy to get there; this is not a plan, it is a problem. Our goal should be to create our masterpiece from our passions and efforts. At the moment of our greatest accomplishments, human nature is to slow down and begin to enjoy the moment. Success breeds comfort and comfort leads us to slack from our mission. If the slide is short, a slacker can still maintain great potential. But if it lasts more than a few days, the result can be the formation of a 'lacker' because they begin to refuse to use their efforts.

Even successful people can become slackers because we can bask in the glory of our accomplishments and retire early. **Remember there are no parking lots in the road to your optimal.** Don’t give in to the inertia of ordinary living or average performance.

Begin to live as though you will be judged one day by "what your potential was" not on "what you did in life". **_Give life 100% of what you got every day you can, no matter your success or failures._**

It is utterly amazing that a cosmetics company could realize a link between the photoelectric effect and skin health and then employ a hospital group to test their observation and their customers observations. A cosmetics company beats science to the punch.

We are doomed if this continues with research funding.

We need medicine to pay more attn to patients observations and less to drug companies RCT. Patients and doctors observe the laws of nature daily.............the drug companies want us to avoid looking at nature and focus on their reality.

The choice is ours.

# Thread: How does a doc see medicine through a neutron star?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-a-doc-see-medicine-through-a-neutron-star.7724/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-26T15:43:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-a-doc-see-medicine-through-a-neutron-star.7724/post-90912>

Read this........[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/07/130726043406.htm](http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2013/07/130726043406.htm)

Largest Magnetic Fields in the Universe
July 26, 2013 â€” Numerical simulations by AEI scientists show for the first time the occurrence of an instability in the interior of neutron stars that can lead to gigantic magnetic fields, possibly triggering one of the most dramatic explosions observed in the Universe.
Share This:

9
An ultra-dense ("hypermassive") neutron star is formed when two neutron stars in a binary system finally merge. Its short life ends with the catastrophic collapse to a black hole, possibly powering a short gamma-ray burst, one of the brightest explosions observed in the universe. Short gamma-ray bursts as observed with satellites like XMM Newton, Fermi or Swift release within a second the same amount of energy as our Galaxy in one year. It has been speculated for a long time that enormous magnetic field strengths, possibly higher than what has been observed in any known astrophysical system, are a key ingredient in explaining such emission. Scientists at the Max Planck Institute for Gravitational Physics (Albert Einstein Institute/AEI) have now succeeded in simulating a mechanism which could produce such strong magnetic fields prior to the collapse to a black hole.
How can such ultra-high magnetic fields -- stronger than ten or hundred million billion times Earth's magnetic field -- be generated from the much lower initial neutron star magnetic fields?
This could be explained by a phenomenon that can be triggered in a differentially rotating plasma in the presence of magnetic fields: neighbouring plasma layers, which rotate at different speeds, "rub against each other," eventually setting the plasma into turbulent motion. In this process called magnetorotational instability magnetic fields can be strongly amplified. This mechanism is known to play an important role in many astrophysical systems such as accretion disks and core-collapse supernovae. It had been speculated for a long time that magnetohydrodynamic instabilities in the interior of hypermassive neutron stars could bring about the necessary magnetic field amplification. The actual demonstration that this is possible has only now been achieved with the present numerical simulations.
The scientists of the Gravitational Wave Modelling Group at the AEI simulated a hypermassive neutron star with an initially ordered ("poloidal") magnetic field, whose structure is subsequently made more complex by the star's rotation. Since the star is dynamically unstable, it eventually collapses to a black hole surrounded by a cloud of matter, until the latter is swallowed by the black hole.
These simulations have unambiguously shown the presence of an exponentially rapid amplification mechanism in the stellar interior -- the magnetorotational instability. This mechanism has so far remained essentially unexplored under the extreme conditions of ultra-strong gravity as found in the interior of hypermassive neutron stars. This is because the physical conditions in the interior of these stars are extremely challenging.
The discovery is interesting for at least two reasons. First, it shows for the first time unambiguously the development of the magnetorotational instability in the framework of Einstein's theory of general relativity, in which there exist no analytical criteria to date to predict the instability. Second, this discovery can have a profound astrophysical impact, supporting the idea that ultra strong magnetic fields can be the key ingredient in explaining the huge amount of energy released by short gamma-ray bursts.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-26T15:45:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-a-doc-see-medicine-through-a-neutron-star.7724/post-90913>

My response to the article and for docs to think about:

when blood gets thicker it loses its zeta potential as I told you in the EMF and QB series......blood is now known to be paramagnetic. It is designed to flow and it creates its own magnetic field as it flows. As blood flow slows it clots......as it clots, energy is consumed to a greater degree and life force is diminished........and life force declines first disease presents and eventually gives way to an early death. Here you have a thread presents how a neutron star dies.......and I show you how we decline in similar fashion. The macrocosm mimics the microcosm when your powers of observation are on........and remember this not so subtle fact about the matter of life......we all are made from the remnants of an exploding star. Yep........it is all quantum theory wrapped up in one thread.............but it is our life and how it works.

### cinnamon — 2013-07-29T19:03:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-a-doc-see-medicine-through-a-neutron-star.7724/post-91110>

Fascinating. As I was reading along, I found myself wondering if there might be a clue here for how to increase the power of earth's fading magnetic field.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-does-a-doc-see-medicine-through-a-neutron-star.7724/post-91125>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parkinsons-disease.7725/post-90915>

Hi Friends,

My dear grandfather just passed away on the 10th from complications of Parkinson's Disease. He was 82.  This has been very difficult for me to see such a healthy man go down hill so quickly. It really progressed rapidly over the last couple of years.

I was wondering if this way of eating can help with preventing one from developing PD. My grandfather started showing symptoms when he was in his early 70s. Of course all of my family is wondering now what we can do to prevent it if it is hereditary. I've read mixed studies about whether or not it's hereditary.

Thanks for any suggestions.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-26T19:36:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/parkinsons-disease.7725/post-90930>

Hi AnnieW. Both of my parents have PD. They pretty much do the opposite of all of Dr. K's advice. I have managed to get them to eat butter instead of margarine, and they are starting to drink more non-fluoridated water. But they are in a severe energy deficit, with too much pulsed EMF around them.

Yes, all of Jack's advice would help them. The advice on eating, getting sun, synchronizing circadian clocks, grounding, hydrating, avoiding EMF, and CT would all help. The tendency towards PD is inherited, but whether that gene is expressed depends on ourselves. If we remove energy from our bodies, our brains will compensate anyway they can.

A biopsy (done via a scope with anesthetics) is invasive and expensive. Not sure if 3 weeks of eating gluten is enough to damage the villi that quickly if she has not been eating gluten. That would be a good Ask Jack question.

What I have learned is that if you test high doing the IgA then there is no point in further testing including a biopsy because you obviously have a gluten issue that is not going away. We all want that confirmed diagnosis of a yes or no including myself which has been inconclusive; but, since I have the gene and my daughter has it, I realize I need to stay gluten free no matter what. I am 1 step away from celiac if I "don't" have it already.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-29T21:12:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-tests-or-test-for-gluten-sensitivity-celiac.7728/post-91126>

> [Beverly said:](/goto/post?id=91120)
>
> Thanks for your help. Questions for you. I will be doing a full celiac panel through my gp. Is an IgA part of that? And I notice enterolabs Iga test is a stool test. Is that a more sensitive test? The celiac panel I am having done is just blood tests.

The blood test for celiac is good for when your small intestine has already been perforated by the gluten (turned into swiss cheese). The ....um food/poo seeps into the body cavity and the body launches a huge fight with the large proteins and other toxins that now invade the blood supply.

The fecal test for celiac is good for a small intestine that is still relatively healthy. The blood will not have gluten antibodies because the small intestine is still holding in the food. There may, however, be antibodies inside the intestine to fight the effects of the gluten, and the fecal test will reveal those. Before the intestine gives way.

So a blood test is good to rule out a huge problem. I have taken both tests. My blood test showed a tiny amount of antibodies, well within range, while my fecal test showed that there was a very large amount of antibodies patrolling my gut, though I had rarely eaten wheat for a couple years, and my last bite of wheat had been 4-5 months earlier.

### Beverly — 2013-07-30T00:02:35+0300

My blood test showed a tiny amount of antibodies, well within range, while my fecal test showed that there was a very large amount of antibodies patrolling my gut, though I had rarely eaten wheat for a couple years, and my last bite of wheat had been 4-5 months earlier.\[/QUOTE\]

First thank you for the explanation. That's very interesting. I had the tissue transglutiminase blood test done a few months back without knowing that I needed to be eating gluten for at least a month for accurate results. Which I hadn't. So that test came back

Tissue Transglutaminase Ab IgA <5 ref <20 Units

Makes me wonder what it would have been had I been eating gluten. I am going to look into the stool test while I am doing my gluten challenge. I am glad you found that you had/have gluten issues.

# Thread: back spasms wiki

**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jinron — 2013-07-26T21:15:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-spasms-wiki.7730/post-90940>

Hello respectful all .Here I am a newbie and junior member. Does anyone get back muscle spasms in the middle part of their lower back ? I get them every few months, typically after having done a yoga class or other stretching. The muscle spasm then takes 4-5 days to disappear and is quite painful, especially so when getting up from a period of sitting / lying down. I am always being told by specialists that I need to get more flexible - but every time I try to become more flexible my [back spasms wiki](http://www.backspasmadvice.com/types-of-lower-back-spasms-causes/)!No stretching and my back is basically ok - crazy I know. Does anyone have any similar experience and any ideas as to how to get more flexible without the back going into spasm after being stretched? Any thoughts really appreciated !
Many thanks,

# Thread: Earth's Magnetic Field Is Fading
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### digital — 2013-07-27T16:51:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91006>

[http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2004/09/0909\_040909\_earthmagfield.html](http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2004/09/0909_040909_earthmagfield.html)
[http://www.reuters.com/article/2012/10/03/us-science-earth-magneticfield-idUSBRE8920X620121003](http://www.reuters.com/article/2012/10/03/us-science-earth-magneticfield-idUSBRE8920X620121003)
[http://www.treeoflife.nu/DRCOUSENS/...ault.aspx?tabid=364&PostID=152&language=en-US](http://www.treeoflife.nu/DRCOUSENS/DRCOUSENSBLOG/tabid/364/language/en-US/~/Default.aspx?tabid=364&PostID=152&language=en-US)

> earth has lost over 70-80% of its magnetic field over the last 4,000 years. At 250 million years ago, it was at 300 gauss. Today it is Â½ gauss. Gauss is a measure of the magnetic field of the earth and it is now estimated to be going down to as little as .1 gauss.

That requires quite a bit of Magnetico pads's to make up the difference!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-27T16:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91007>

Just wait til the July webinar.........

### digital — 2013-07-27T17:22:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91009>

While on this subject:

Magnetic effects on water
[http://www.lsbu.ac.uk/water/magnetic.html#r1522](http://www.lsbu.ac.uk/water/magnetic.html#r1522)

\[Note: a small field of even 2000 Gauss can help structure water\]

Liquid water is affected by magnetic fields \[1522, 1597\] and such fields can assist its purification \[1651\]. Water is diamagnetic and may be levitated in very high magnetic fields (10 T, compare Earth's magnetic field 50 μT) \[170\]. Lower, but still powerful, magnetic fields (**0.2 T**) have been shown, in simulations, to increase the number of monomer water molecules \[192\] but, rather surprisingly, they increase the tetrahedrality at the same time. Other studies show an increase in cluster size in liquid water is caused by a magnetic field \[1597\]. Salt mobility is enhanced in strong magnetic fields (1-10 T) causing some disruption to the hydrogen bonding \[1431\]. However this only causes a net reduction in hydrogen bonding at high salt concentrations (for example 5 M NaCl), whereas at lower concentrations (1 M NaCl) the increase in water hydrogen bonding in the presence of such high magnetic fields more than compensates for this effect \[1431\]. They may also assist clathrate formation \[485\]. The increase in refractive index with magnetic field has been attributed to increased hydrogen bond strength \[647\]. Weak magnetic fields (15 mT) have also been shown to increase the evaporation rate \[1278\]. These effects are consistent with the magnetic fields weakening the van der Waals bonding between the water moleculesa and the water molecules being more tightly bound, due to the magnetic field reducing the thermal motion of the inherent charges by generating dampening forces \[703\].
...
The magnetic susceptibility of water increases from negative towards positive with magnetic frequency and is reported to be positive (i.e. it is slightly paramagnetic) in the range of 0.4-1 MHz \[1761\] for ambient water.
...
Static magnetic effects have been shown to cause strengthened hydrogen bonding \[1693\] and an increase in the ordered structure of water formed around hydrophobic molecules and colloids \[106\], as shown by the increase in fluorescence of dissolved probes \[108\]. Also, magnetic fields affect the infrared spectrum of water (showing its effect on water clustering) and these effects remain for considerable time after the magnetic field is removed \[1697\]. Surprisingly, even very small magnetic fields may affect the solubility of gasses in seawater (solubility increasing with magnetic field (20-50 ÂµT) \[1492\], probably by their effect on the clathrate stability. This reinforces the view that it is the movement through a magnetic field, and it associated electromagnetic effect, that is important for disrupting the hydrogen bonding. Such fields can also increase the evaporation rate of water and the dissolution rate of oxygen (due to its paramagnetic nature) but cannot, despite claims by certain expensive water preparations, increase the equilibrium amount of oxygen dissolved in water above its established, and rather low, equilibrium concentration \[176\]. Magnetic fields can also increase proton spin relaxation \[623\], which may speed up some reactions dependent on proton transfer. Treatment of water with magnetic fields of about one Tesla increases the strength of mortar due to its greater hydration \[426\].

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-27T17:46:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91011>

This is how low level ELF's affect the dipoles of hydrogen bonding........the more the bonds are affected by the magnetic field the more energy can be carried on water's chemistry as it binds to the semiconductor proteins like collagen, actin, myosin. It is all about energy transfers.......water is the perfect chemical to do it......but it requires the perfect field to work optimally..........when it does you do not need a ton of ATP......because you approach the zero entropy system I mentioned in QB 1. It also means your disease free and you live a long life because you do not have to replace your mitochondria often.

### nonchalant — 2013-07-27T19:54:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91015>

Very interesting article, Digital. Thanks.
I was surprised by this coment:

> Also, magnetic fields affect the infrared spectrum of water (showing its effect on water clustering) and these effects remain for considerable time after the magnetic field is removed \[1697\].

My experience is that the effect lasts a short time, like 30 minutes.

They later clarify this, saying

> Both temperature and magnetic fields affect the infrared spectrum of water (showing their effect on water clustering) and these effects remain for considerable time (~1 hour) after the magnetic field is removed or temperature changed \[1697\]. It has been shown that (still) mineral water can be magnetised and retain this magnetisation for more than a day, supposedly due to the production of magnetic nanobubbles. \[1780\].

Perhaps in a lab they get better results than I have, with different magnets and such. And their mineral water isn't the same as my spring water. But if it can last up to a day, that would be fabulous.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-28T17:33:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91040>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=91007)
>
> Just wait til the July webinar.........

When is it?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-07-28T20:47:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earths-magnetic-field-is-fading.7739/post-91044>

not sure.....they have it. But it has to be soon

### MeghanK — 2013-07-29T02:41:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91058>

What does everyone bring to work for lunch/dinner... packable... easy to eat? Any bars or meal replacement considered "epi-paleo".. I brought shrimp, a nori wrap with sardines and veggie bag to steam today but it was a mess and tough to eat fast and the prep time is forever....
Thanks!

### caroline — 2013-07-29T06:40:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91067>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91076>

I do salmon salad, tuna sashimi, and grass fed ground beef patties frequently. If I need to do an errand on my lunch hour like a grocery store stop, I visit he seafood counter and have them sseason and steam a nice fillet for me.

Leftovers from dinner are another staple in my lunches...can't believe I actually used to eat Lean Cuisines! Yuck

### caroline — 2013-07-30T03:39:21+0300

### caroline — 2013-07-30T03:42:52+0300

### Inger — 2013-07-30T19:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91176>

For work I usually buy some frozen minced lamb from New Zealand, 1 pound. I put it on a warm place and when it is time to eat it is thawed. I then sprincle some salt on the raw meat and add some EVOO and balsamic vinegar on it and eat. Yum yum. Fills me up and gives awesome energy.
Or I eat raw salmon that I have put some apple cider vinegar / lemon juice and salt on at home, I just put it in a glass jar (I take quite a lot, about a pound) and eat it when time to eat. No need to refridgerate it for just a few hours. Sometimes also precooked mussels. Or a big piece of raw Roquefort cheese and a cucumber with it. maybe a handful of Brazil nuts too.

Or, I make jerky and eat with coconut oil. Or I take raw lambs hearts and cut them into slices. Just eat them plain. At work I eat for fuel, no fuzzing around..lol I eat stuff that gives me the best energy and holds me satiated for long.

### MeghanK — 2013-07-31T00:53:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91198>

Does anyone eat veggies? I feel like these are all meat and protein and seafood suggestions... I LOVE my seafood but I always have it with S#@T TON of veggies....

### caroline — 2013-07-31T01:57:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91203>

salad, spinach, cucumbers, tomatoes, red onions, celery
I limit carbs - it is winter here.....

### PrimalPam — 2013-08-03T17:21:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91428>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=91144)
>
> What about taking a BPC in a thermos for lunch? and some coconut manna and dark chocolate......

^^^ This is exactly what I do! BPC in a stainless thermos (coffee, turmeric, CO, CO milk), a little container of coconut manna, and a few squares of super dark chocolate.

My BAB keeps me full until around 1-2 pm, when I snack on the above items, and holds me till dinner (these days, around 5-6 pm).

I had to travel recently, and I brought ziploc snack bags with dark chocolate squares and Coconut cream (it's solid, rather than manna-like; comes in a little rectangle box, sold at Whole Foods), Brazil nuts, and jerky. I ordered as close to a BAB as I could get in the hotel, and when lunch was served, I pulled out my little bags.

### GlenPDQ — 2013-08-03T22:24:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-the-go-or-at-work-meals.7742/post-91446>

I found a tasty Portugese brand of sardines called GONSALVES Sardines in Olive Oil. Sometimes I'll throw 2 cans in my carry bag for lunch at work. An online review rated them 3rd or 4th out of 30 brands of sardines. They are not the 3.75 ounce cans like you find in WM, but are 4-3/8 ounce cans which contain just 3 LARGE sardines having a bit of a tuna flavor. They can be bought in quantity on Amazon for price savings.
