# Thread: In need of support and direction...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### K123 — 2013-08-05T01:44:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91517>

Hi all, my first post here or on any primal related website. I will be 32 yrs old this month. Being an Indian I was brought up eating a mainly carb diet. And since I left home and struggled my way through uni I ate an even more poor diet of bad carbs like instant noodles and tonnes of rice. Also worked night shifts to support myself so messed up my circadian rhythm for a good three years. After which I have been through a lot of emotional stress with my church and in spite of having an MBA found that I had lost my confidence to go after any of my ambitions. So I haven't worked for the last 5 years almost. I was always a chubby kid but last year my father passed away after a lot unexpected struggle and I was the only one by his side and that stress made me put on another 10 kgs in two months. So right now, I am about 26 kgs over-weight for my height. I was diagnosed with PCOS about 6 years back but my guess is that I probably had PCOS for almost 12 years or so. It took a long journey to come to Jack's website. Started with the Shwartzbein Principle and then to the Harcombe Diet and Primal Blueprint and after much trial and error over the last two years I was finally led to Jack's site and knew it is the real deal. I think Jack is a real genius who the world will recognise only too late as always. Everytime I have tried to eat by his precepts I start losing weight and my feet stop hurting. Although I have been trying to eat primal, I noticed a few months back that I was getting some vitiligo/ white spots on my face. I realised that it was because of auto-immunity and I can't do eggs for breakfast anymore. I am basically struggling to actually start a leptin reset for an auto-immune disorder and to stick to it. I start but end up giving up in a week or so. The thought of sticking to it for 90 days seems so challenging. Although I know that a lot of my troubles - spots, facial hair, falling hair on my head, a lot of excess weight, heavy periods and pain in various parts of my body...I would definitely see improvement in all of it. I just need some support to start and stick with it. Also some advice on what a person with auto-immunity should do would help. I am confused about whether cooking peppers and tomatoes actually makes it safer for me or if I need to avoid it altogether. I know Dr.K said I should do a lot of CT so trying to get used to it. Sorry for this crazy long post but thought I should give the proper background to get relevant advice. Thank you in advance to all the heroes that have gone before me!

### nonchalant — 2013-08-05T02:05:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91519>

Hi, K123, welcome! Do you eat seafood?

### caroline — 2013-08-05T02:19:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91521>

Welcome K123 .....You have definitely come to the right place! I don't know a lot about autoimmune but there are lots of people here that do and they will help out as soon as they have some time.

Please try to move this to the journal section - maybe open a support ticket and ask them to do that. Journals are great because you get to track your progress and we can all offer some help and support you. It would be our great pleasure to do that and you can help us as well .....everyone's story impacts all of us..

You have done so well already inspite of all the negative stuff in your life.

In my opinion - the more simple your meals are - the easier it is. That is definitely how it is for me.
I am a little confused - I need to read your post again ..... are you doing the leptin reset?
If you are having a BAB first thing in the morning \[at least 50 +gr protein\] with lots of good fat ...then that will set you up for the day. If you do this - it becomes much easier. Your cravings will go away and you will slowly need less food later in the day. Sometimes you may need even more protein than 50 g for your BAB - I did.

Have you addressed your EMF exposure? and are you following light cycles? these are the most important things to take care of.

Have you listened to Jack's latest podcasts? they help alot to connect the dots in an easy to understand way. Are you able to see the webinars? Jack is very easy to listen to and they are great for getting to the point quickly for specific things.
Also - Jack's book is a great overview of all this and it is a great reference tool to have at hand.

There is a great deal to take onboard here .... so please keep reading and asking questions - we are here to help \[and we have all been there\]

I just re read your post.....
Please don't look at this as a 90 day challenge. You need to commit to this 100% and be fully engaged. This is a journey of a lifetime - I can promise you that for sure. You will get your life back - your head will clear - so many things will improve but it is going to take time. It took you a lifetime to get here ...so it is going to take more than 90 days to fix all this stuff. Personally - I can't imagine living any other way. I am having the time of my life trying to get my head around all this science and I feel great.

It is a very amazing thing to take control of your life and your health and that is what Dr. Kruse is teaching us. You need to become informed about all aspects of your health. You need to be in the driver's seat. It truly is the journey of a life time!

### K123 — 2013-08-05T12:43:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91545>

I love seafood nonchalant! But it gets quite expensive in London, UK to find wild/ fresh water seafood. Ate Salmon fillets this morning but to get 50 grams of protein I had to eat 2 fillets which costs about $8.70 for breakfast alone. Can't do eggs anymore since I realise I am getting white spots on my skin which is a sign of auto-immune disorder vitiligo.

### K123 — 2013-08-05T13:16:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91546>

Hi Caroline! Thank you so much for your time and effort  I couldn't find where to raise a support ticket so I just wrote to them on the contact us page and asked how to go about it. Still getting a hang of this website and the features. I apologise for my ramblings last night...I really wanted to just start writing and stop putting it off and therefore the lack of structure and clarity. To answer some of your questions :
1) I have tried to start the Leptin Reset a few times. I manage to stick to it for about a week or so and then end up cheating because I have a sweet tooth and still struggling with sugar metabolism symptoms...as in intense hunger at times and intense craving for sweet things. We tried the Whole 30 in November last year and saw some great results. Then came to read about Leptin reset this Feb and have been trying to really get going on the Leptin Reset. Usually I fail in about a week's time...when we have been so strict for a week and the weekend comes. So no, I can't say I am on Leptin reset yet...yesterday for example I ate the BAB but at night ended up eating chocolate cake!!!  So I am really trying to get back on the horse and stay there.
2) BAB: Like I mentioned I am having auto-immune issues showing up since I ate eggs for breakfast almost everyday. So then had to quit the eggs. Which means having to figure out some easy ways to eat breakfast...but wild seafood is really expensive here...not sure how it compares to the US. My breakfast salmon cost $8.70...is that about the same?
3) EMF: Hubby is a geek so of course our tiny London flat is filled with computers and a large TV and our flat has a lot of bright ceiling lighting and the tiny open plan kitchen has the washing machine and dishwasher and what not. And since I am trying to get back to work and work from home I am really surrounded by gadgets. DH says there is no point turning off the wi-fi because the neighbours wi-fi's are just as strong and all around us. Don't even know where to start.
4) Light Cycles: I tried it a little and even bought these tiny artificial candles for the night - [https://tinyurl.com/qh2wsdz](https://tinyurl.com/qh2wsdz). But I have to confess sleeping habits are all upside down right now.
5) Webinar: I did listen to the latest webinar that Gretchen did and Dr.K came in later. It was great. That is what made me want to actually join the forum.
I have been trying to battle this out alone for a while but I see I need people to be involved. To keep me on track. A friend of mine was doing something like weight-watchers and went to these weekly meetings and it really helped her to lose and to maintain her weight. Although she is still eating bread and what not. The reason I didn't want to do that is because I know that is not the path to optimal. I want to be the best I can be. But I also have just realised what a huge difference it makes to have others involved to spur you on. So that is why I have come to ask for help finally instead of struggling alone for months. I used to love biology in school and am quite into it even now. When my dad was hospitalised, his doctors kept asking me if I had a medical background because I could discuss things with them. So I absolutely love reading Dr.K's blogposts...although it is a LOT to take in. So I am totally with you on the fact that this needs to be for life, but I am thinking on the 90 day level because I am hoping in those 90 days I will get to the point like many others here have where your cravings are but dead. And your mind is clear enough that you can choose health and not feel enslaved by carbs  If you know what I mean??

### Lahelada — 2013-08-06T00:59:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91589>

Hello KT123 ,

I am a newbie here myself so cannot offer too much good advice. I think though there might be some shopping tips I could give you. Salmon is out of my price range here too so I looked at alternatives. Pollock,haddock,mackerel is a perfect everyday fish. You might want to have a look at Aldi's and see if they have frozen pollock. They usually have good prizes. Failing that and if you have the time Borough fish market . I hear the fish there is half the supermarket price and fresh every day ,on top of that locally sourced .Good luck.

Edit: Had a nose around .Lookee here lookee here,good prices even for wild salmon [https://www.aldi.co.uk/en/product-range/frozen/fish-seafood/](https://www.aldi.co.uk/en/product-range/frozen/fish-seafood/)

### caroline — 2013-08-06T01:53:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91593>

Hi K. .....You are not rambling at all!

Lets talk more about the BAB.....You need to eat alot of protein/fat within 30 minutes of waking. When I started I was 62K and I quite often ate around 80+g of protein for my BAB. You need to make sure you stuff alot down!

A lot of us here have/had eating disorders of one kind or another - me included - and please understand that obesity and anorexia are the same - only opposite ends of the spectrum. We are all in this together and we have Dr. K. shining his flashlite for us on this journey of a lifetime - we are very lucky...

Back to the BAB .....You need to eat that much regardless of how hungry \[or not\] you are .... adding in a lot of fat makes it easier. A lot of us find that seafood is just not filling enough for breakfast. I often throw a leg of lamb in the crockpot and eat that and maybe add in a can of sardines. Seafood is very expensive - so do what you can..... Dr. Kruse has said this many times. If you can afford oysters morning, noon and nite ....great - go for it....but most of us here can't so we do what we can. You can also have hamburgers for breakfast and I usually add some butter on top. It is easier to think - dinner for breakfast! Always, always think outside the box....be crazy and daring!

Honestly - without question ....if you eat enough protein and fat for breakfast your cravings will go away. I used to be hungry all the time and had terrible cravings but after starting the reset I shifted down to two meals a day - and absolutely no cravings. This happened for me in about 2-3 weeks - but it can take longer. I was very focused tho ...I hated all the food cravings ruling my life!

A lot of us have a BPC for breakfast .....Bullet proof coffee ...to get extra fat in. Green tea is fine too. I add a couple of TBSP of butter and coconut oil and sometimes pure cocoa. Sometimes I just eat a slab of butter - but then I have always loved butter

What else can I tell you?
lets just recap for a minute ....maybe re read the leptin reset to make sure that you haven't missed anything

\-Following light cycles is huge - get a pair of blue blocker glasses - no TV - limit computer time

Get lots of grounding - hug a tree - walk barefoot in the grass - swim in an ocean

C/T as often as possible - have you seen the easy start guide for C/T?

That is really all there is - don't over think it right now .... of course there is a ton to read and learn here - but just take baby steps....and ask lots and lots of questions.
I once asked Dr.K. what I thought was a very dopey question and he said - no its not dopey ....the dopey thing was not asking him sooner. That taught me a big lesson.

You are doing great ...have you listened to JK's podcasts \[home page\]? The last two or three tie lots of thing together.

PM me if you want.....

### caroline — 2013-08-06T02:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-need-of-support-and-direction.7802/post-91595>

A couple of other things - I just re read your last post! That is so great if you love biology and have an understanding...I don't have a scientific bone in my body and really struggle ....but I definitely get the overall picture

consider being a silver or gold member - of course you don't have to .....but think of this as an investment in your life and in your health and future.

All the webinars are so helpful and if you are a gold member the Q&A's are also included and you can ask questions.....

# Thread: Magnetic Sleep Pad Options in Australia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### yewwei.tan — 2013-08-05T06:55:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-91539>

Just finished listening to the July 2013 Webinar, and I'm pretty convinced that a magnetic sleep pad is something to get right away.

Trouble is that I'm all the way down under in Melbourne, Australia. So getting a magnetico pad is out of the question.

However, there's a local supplier who sells magnetic sleep pads which seem to be decently well made. A sample mattress can be seen here -- [http://www.magneticexperience.com.au/king-single-magnetic-underlay-australia-free-postage-p-117.html](http://www.magneticexperience.com.au/king-single-magnetic-underlay-australia-free-postage-p-117.html).

In sum, the King Single sized mattress that I linked to:

\- costs AU$210
\- has 250 magnets, which are all north facing (negative magnetic field)
\- where each magnet has a 1200 surface gauss

It's designed to be used on top of your mattress, but as the magnetico site explains, this could lead to exposure to alternating positive and negative fields.

The magnetico apparently uses 3950 core gauss magnets, which should correspond to a 1000-1100 surface gauss (I have no idea about the calculation, but those are the numbers that seem to pop up on the internet). Magnetico then claims that a single layer of such magnets deliver a 10 gauss magnetic field to the sleeper atop an 8-inch mattress. (the super sleep system relies on 2 layers)

Wouldn't that imply then, that if I place my Australian Magnetic mattress below a standard thickness mattress (8-10 inches), then I would get roughly the same effect as the magnetico? Wouldn't I also be able to stack up 2 of these mattress to achieve the 20 gauss magnetic field?

Thanks.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-05T07:34:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-91540>

I'd call magnetico and see if they can bulk ship......if not ask them about Quality of the Aussie product.....Dean surely would know.

### Matthew Cassidy — 2013-10-22T03:47:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-98538>

Hi Yewwei, Did you find out about this product at all. Am interested to hear what you learned..

### caroline — 2013-10-22T05:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-98549>

We have already asked about bulk shipping - can't do........

### caroline — 2013-10-22T05:51:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-98550>

Have you looked into the wool magnetic pads? Freesia talked about getting one to see what it is like....

Do you understand about being in an alternating field if it is on top?

### freesia — 2013-10-22T10:15:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-98560>

Hi Caroline, sorry I didn't get back to you before now on the wool underlays. I was looking at the Woolrest ones which are fairly commonly used here - and plenty of people seem happy with them too!..Then I read about the alternating fields on mattress topping pads, and I got cold feet on hitting the buy button.
Before reading that warning on the Magnetico site I knew there was a risk of alternating currents if the magnets weren't placed exactly right in relation to each other, but it seems there's more to it than that. It's clearly explained on the magnetico site and I trust their advice on this.
Re putting a couple of the cheaper ones under a mattress...I \*think\* you'd want to watch the positioning of the magnets (in relation to each other) quite carefully to make sure you were getting the gauss you expect at the distance you need.

### stuartfolkard@gmail.com — 2013-10-22T10:17:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-98561>

> Hi Yewwei, Did you find out about this product at all. Am interested to hear what you learned..

I got one about 5 days ago. I called the company for info and they were friendly but couldn't provide any info re possible positive field coming through, per yewwei's note above. Their claim (backed by a photo of a reading being taken) is that surface gauss is around 1200 although with the cheap gauss meter I have I couldn't get near that value consistently.
Took a bit of adjustment the first few nights (a hot feeling - but maybe from the material used in the pad as well as or instead of the magnets?). I have it under an earthing sheet plugged into the wall (I'm on the third floor). I've been using the sheet for 4-5 months. I've recently started ocean swimming too, so I've got a few changes going on and it will be hard to ascertain what's doing what..
Still, I thought it was cheap enough to experiment with.

### _J_ — 2019-02-25T04:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-sleep-pad-options-in-australia.7804/post-263918>

Something that might interest you..

I used the magnetico website and got some simple quotes + delivery for Australia

If you buy 1x Queen & 2x single, then it is cheaper to have it delivered in the following configuration: -
1x Queen (delivery #1)
2x Single (delivery #2)

You may not think much, but it was about $1000 cheaper. I guess its related to the way that UPS has to handle extremely large/weighted packages, or rather don't want to.

Perhaps UPS have been following the forums, and decided in a non-linear pricing structure ?

# Thread: OK, so you heard the July 2013 webinar, now this..............
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-05T15:02:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91550>

[http://thetrendingmachine.com/poll-shows-18-34-year-olds-are-more-forgetful-than-seniors/](http://thetrendingmachine.com/poll-shows-18-34-year-olds-are-more-forgetful-than-seniors/)

Two-fifths (39%) of Americans have forgotten or misplaced at least one of these everyday items in the past week. Usually we refer to these memory lapses as â€œsenior momentsâ€￾, but The Trending Machine National Poll tells a different story. Millennials 18-34, in fact, are significantly more likely than seniors ages 55 or older to forget what day it is (15% vs. 7%), where they put their keys (14% vs. 8%), forget to bring their lunch (9% vs. 3%) or even to take a bath or shower (6% vs. 2%).

Conversely, the only thing seniors 55+ are significantly more likely to forget are names (23% vs. 16%).

We asked Patricia Gutentag a leading family and occupational therapist what may be at the root of these relatively high levels of forgetfulness in millennials. â€œStress! Stress often leads to forgetfulness, depression and poor judgment.â€￾ Indeed studying for finals and preparing for job interviews, not to mention relationship issues are leading stressors in young adults. Gutentag quickly adds, â€œWe find higher rates of ADHD diagnoses in young adults. This is a population that has grown up multitasking using technology, often compounded by lack of sleep, all of which results in high levels of forgetfulness.â€￾

With regard to gender, the stress associated with work life balance and increased financial responsibilities has taken a toll on women. According to The Trending Machine Poll of 800 adults, women are generally more likely to forget or misplace these everyday items compared to men (43% vs. 31%). Specifically, women are more likely to forget or misplace their keys (14% vs. 8%), their phone (12% vs. 7%), and even to take a bath or shower (5% vs. 3%).

Regional stress differences can be inferred from the higher level of forgetfulness found in the Northeast compared to those reported in the laid back West (51% vs. 39% respectively).

### nonchalant — 2013-08-05T16:27:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91553>

We need another word for stress. Everyone always thinks of just emotion, pressure, busyness when the word 'stress' is used. Few people think about poor food, lack of energy, hostile environment.

### Martin — 2013-08-05T16:55:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91554>

Amen, nonchalant.

I've had this come up in discussions with my group of friends all the time. They think that just as one can "create" a parking space downtown with just their intention, they can go live in a beach house in Fukashima and get along with just their "thoughts" protecting them. "Stress" to them is ALL mental/spiritual, and imply that all we need to live well is "happy thoughts". And I agree... to a point, but when some disease is still kicking your ass, then one has to look at the environment one is in. And that point is demonstrated by canceling my time-share condo in Fukashima.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-06T00:32:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ok-so-you-heard-the-july-2013-webinar-now-this.7805/post-91583>

Stress in my vernacular is different than everyone else's and has been for a while.......go back and read the first few leptin blogs........when we are LR we have rises in NF kappa beta and IL-6.........anything that causes it.......is what I call stress.

Your field.......needs to lower your IL-6 and NF kappa beta, by the way.........EMF raises both.

### seanb4 — 2013-08-05T21:52:26+0300

I have tried various forms of magnesium but they usually stop working after a while and produce kind of messy stools.

Thanks, Sean.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-07T03:59:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/suns-magnetic-field-how-will-this-affect-us.7811/post-91712>

But then we have Maxwell's problems.........While Maxwell's equations (along with the rest of classical electromagnetism) are extraordinarily successful at explaining and predicting a variety of phenomena, they are not exact laws of the universe, but merely approximations. In some special situations, they can be noticeably inaccurate. Examples include extremely strong fields (see Euler–Heisenberg Lagrangian) and extremely short distances (see vacuum polarization). Moreover, various phenomena occur in the world even though Maxwell's equations predicts them to be impossible, such as "nonclassical light" (which really happens) and quantum entanglement (also proven by modern day physics) of electromagnetic fields themselves (quantum optics).

Finally, any phenomenon involving individual photons, such as the **photoelectric effect**, Planck's law, the Duane–Hunt law, single-photon light detectors, etc., would be difficult or impossible to explain if Maxwell's equations were exactly true, as Maxwell's equations do not involve photons. For the most accurate predictions in all situations, Maxwell's equations have been superseded by quantum electrodynamics. I started my learning with Maxwell........but I went to Einstein when I realized that life uses the photoelectric effect to make a zero entropy system for energy transfers.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-07T00:35:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/example-of-the-field-effect.7818/post-91670>

Who Has the Guts for Gluten?
By MOISES VELASQUEZ-MANOFF

WE know that the proteins called gluten, found in wheat and other grains, provoke celiac disease. And we know how to treat the illness: a gluten-free diet. But the rapidly increasing prevalence of celiac disease, which has quadrupled in the United States in just 50 years, is still mystifying.

Scientists are pursuing some intriguing possibilities. One is that breast-feeding may protect against the disease. Another is that we have neglected the teeming ecosystem of microbes in the gut — bacteria that may determine whether the immune system treats gluten as food or as a deadly invader.

Celiac disease is generally considered an autoimmune disorder. The name celiac derives from the Greek word for “hollow,” as in bowels. Gluten proteins in wheat, barley and rye prompt the body to turn on itself and attack the small intestine. Complications range from diarrhea and anemia to osteoporosis and, in extreme cases, lymphoma. Some important exceptions notwithstanding, the prevalence of celiac disease is estimated to range between 0.6 and 1 percent of the world’s population.

Nearly everyone with celiac disease has one of two versions of a cellular receptor called the human leukocyte antigen, or H.L.A. These receptors, the thinking goes, naturally increase carriers’ immune response to gluten.

This detailed understanding makes celiac disease unique among autoimmune disorders. Two factors — one a protein, another genetic — are clearly defined; and in most cases, eliminating gluten from the patient’s diet turns off the disease.

Yet the more scientists study celiac disease, the more some crucial component appears in need of identification. Roughly 30 percent of people with European ancestry carry predisposing genes, for example. Yet more than 95 percent of the carriers tolerate gluten just fine. So while these genes (plus gluten) are necessary to produce the disease, they’re evidently insufficient to cause it.

Animal studies have reinforced that impression. In mice engineered to express those H.L.A.’s, tolerance to gluten must be deliberately “broken.” Without an immunological trigger of some kind, the rodents happily tolerate the protein.

A recent study, which analyzed blood serum from more than 3,500 Americans who were followed since 1974, suggested that such a trigger could strike adults at any time. By 1989, the prevalence of celiac disease in this cohort had doubled.

“You’re talking about an autoimmune disease in which we thought we had all the dots connected,” says Alessio Fasano, head of the Center for Celiac Research and Treatment at the Massachusetts General Hospital for Children in Boston, and the senior author of the study. “Then we start to accumulate evidence that there was something else.”

Identifying that “something else” has gained some urgency. In the United States, improved diagnosis doesn’t seem to explain the rising prevalence. Scientists use the presence of certain self-directed antibodies to predict celiac disease. They have analyzed serum stored since the mid-20th century and compared it to serum from Americans today. Today’s serum is more than four times as likely to carry those antibodies.

BLAME for the increase of celiac disease sometimes falls on gluten-rich, modern wheat varietals; increased consumption of wheat, and the ubiquity of gluten in processed foods.

Yet the epidemiology of celiac disease doesn’t always support this idea. One comparative study involving some 5,500 subjects yielded a prevalence of roughly one in 100 among Finnish children, but using the same diagnostic methods, just one in 500 among their Russian counterparts.

Differing wheat consumption patterns can’t explain this disparity. If anything, Russians consume more wheat than Finns, and of similar varieties.

Neither can genetics. Although now bisected by the Finno-Russian border, Karelia, as the study region is known, was historically a single province. The two study populations are culturally, linguistically and genetically related. The predisposing gene variants are similarly prevalent in both groups.

Maybe more telling, this disparity holds for other autoimmune and allergic diseases. Finland ranks first in the world for Type 1 autoimmune diabetes. But among Russian Karelians, the disease is nearly six times less frequent. Antibodies indicative of autoimmune thyroiditis are also less prevalent, and the risk of developing allergies, as gauged by skin-prick tests, is one-fourth as common.

What’s the Russians’ secret?

“It’s a remote territory of Russia,” says Heikki Hyoty, a scientist at the University of Tampere in Finland. “They live like Finns 50 years ago.”

At the time of this research, roughly a decade ago, Russia’s per-capita income was one-fifteenth of Finland’s. Analysis of house dust and potable water suggests that the Russian Karelians encountered a greater variety and quantity of microbes, including many that were absent in Finland.

Not surprisingly, they also suffered from more fecal-oral infections. For example, three of four Russian Karelian children harbored Helicobacter pylori, a corkscrew-shaped bacterium, while just one in 20 Finnish children did. The bacterium can cause ulcers and stomach cancer, but mounting evidence suggests that it may also protect against asthma.

Professor Hyoty suspects that Russian Karelians’ microbial wealth protects them from autoimmune and allergic diseases by, essentially, strengthening the arm of the immune system that guards against such illnesses.

Meanwhile, Yolanda Sanz, a researcher at the Institute of Agrochemistry and Food Technology in Valencia, Spain, makes a compelling case for the importance of intestinal microbes.

Years ago, Dr. Sanz noted that a group of bacteria native to the intestine known as bifidobacteria were relatively depleted in children with celiac disease compared with healthy controls. Other microbes, including native E. coli strains, were overly abundant and oddly virulent.

How to determine cause or consequence?

In a test tube, she found that those E. coli amplified the inflammatory response of human intestinal cells to gluten. But bifidobacteria switched the response from inflammation to tolerance.

In rats, the E. coli again intensified inflammation to gluten, prompting what’s sometimes called a “leaky gut” — the milieu suspected of contributing to celiac disease. Conversely, bifidobacteria protected the intestinal barrier. Microbes, it seemed, could influence the immune response to gluten.

Bifidobacteria occur naturally in breast milk, which, along with protective antibodies and immune-signaling proteins, conveys hundreds of prebiotic sugars. These sugars selectively feed certain microbes in the infant gut, particularly bifidobacteria. Breast-fed infants tend to harbor more bifidobacteria than formula-fed ones.

All of which may explain a curious historical phenomenon — an “epidemic” of celiac disease that struck Sweden some 30 years ago. Anneli Ivarsson, a pediatrician at Umea University, recalled a sudden wave of “terribly sick” infants.

Sleuthing revealed that, just before the spike, official guidelines on infant feeding had changed. In an effort to prevent celiac disease, paradoxically, parents were instructed to delay the introduction of gluten until their babies were six months old. That also happened to be when many Swedish mothers weaned their children. Coincidentally, companies had increased the amount of gluten in baby food.

This confluence produced an unwitting “experiment with a whole population,” says Dr. Ivarsson — a large quantity of gluten introduced suddenly after weaning. Among Swedes born between 1984 and 1996, the prevalence of celiac disease tripled to 3 percent. The epidemic ebbed only when authorities again revised infant-feeding guidelines: keep breast-feeding, they urged, while simultaneously introducing small amounts of gluten. Food manufacturers also reduced the gluten content of infant foodstuffs. Dr. Ivarsson found that, during the epidemic, the longer children breast-fed after their first exposure to gluten, the more protected they were.

Not all subsequent studies have found nursing protective, but partly as a result of Sweden’s experience, the American Academy of Pediatrics now recommends that infants start consuming gluten while still breast-feeding.

Research by Dr. Sanz of Spain again illuminates how this may work. Some years back, she began following a cohort of 164 newborns with celiac disease in the immediate family. By four months, children with celiac-associated genotypes — 117 of them — had accrued a microbial community with fewer bifidobacteria compared to those without. If bifidobacteria help us tolerate gluten, these children appeared to be edging toward intolerance.

There was one notable exception: Breast-feeding “normalized” the microbes of at-risk children somewhat, boosting bifidobacterial counts.

Dr. Fasano of Boston has made another potentially important find. He followed 47 at-risk newborns, regularly collecting microbes from 16 of them, which he analyzed after two years. Like Dr. Sanz, he found these genetically at-risk children to accumulate a relatively impoverished, unstable microbial community.

But it’s a secondary observation that has Dr. Fasano particularly excited. Two of these children developed autoimmune disease: one celiac disease, another Type 1 diabetes, which shares genetic susceptibility with celiac disease. In both cases, a decline of lactobacilli preceded disease onset.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-07T00:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/example-of-the-field-effect.7818/post-91671>

Assuming that the pattern holds in larger studies, “imagine what would be the unbelievable consequences of this finding,” he says. “Keep the lactobacilli high enough in the guts of these kids, and you prevent autoimmunity.”

The caveats here are numerous: the tiny sample size in Dr. Fasano’s study; Dr. Sanz hasn’t yet revealed who actually developed celiac disease in her cohort; and even if these microbial shifts reliably precede disease onset — as they do in larger studies on allergic disease — they’re still bedeviled by the old “chicken or the egg” question: Which comes first, the aberrant microbial community, or the aberrant immune response?

Bana Jabri, director of research at the University of Chicago Celiac Disease Center, notes that immune disturbances change the microbial ecosystem. But here’s the catch: Even if the chicken comes first, she says, the egg can contribute. Rodent experiments show that intestinal inflammation can select for unfriendly bacteria that further inflame. “You can have a positive feedback loop,” she says.

SO your microbes change you, but your genes also shape your microbes — as do environment, breast milk, diet and antibiotics, among many other factors.

Such complexity both confounds notions of one-way causality and suggests different paths to the same disease. “You have the same endpoint,” Dr. Jabri says, “but how you get there may be variable.”

The intricacies don’t stop there.

Not all breast milk is the same. It varies according to diet and other factors. One study found that milk from overweight mothers had fewer of those bifidobacteria than milk from thinner mothers. Another observed that breast milk from farming mothers, who inhabit a microbially enriched environment, carried more anti-inflammatory proteins compared with urban mothers’ milk. “All these things are going to matter,” Dr. Jabri says. And they’re all potential nudge points in the quest to prevent disease.

The tangled web of possibilities should not, however, distract us from the facts on the ground. In a far-flung corner of Europe, people develop celiac disease and other autoimmune diseases as infrequently as Americans and Finns did a half-century ago. The same genes exposed to the same quantity of gluten do not, in that environment, produce the same frequency of disease.

“We could probably prevent celiac disease if we just give the same environment to the Finnish children as they have in Karelia,” says Dr. Hyoty. “But there’s no way to do it now, except to move the babies there.”

Moises Velasquez-Manoff is the author of “An Epidemic of Absence: A New Way of Understanding Allergies and Autoimmune Diseases.”

no one likes to look in a mirror they did not expect.

### CTforlife — 2013-08-08T20:03:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-obvious-a-9th-grader-gets-it.7819/post-91844>

### prAna303 — 2013-08-08T22:42:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-obvious-a-9th-grader-gets-it.7819/post-91863>

When a danger is invisible to our senses our minds cannot make something real out of it. Today we give kids iPads at hospitals in Sweden when they are treated for cancer. If i tell someone that i get a really bad feeling around technology today they just laugh and say that i was a gadget freak not so long ago, for sure that is true and because of that i can relate to the problems. Why would something hurt us when it have no smell, noise or presence at all? Our brains and DNA have never faced this fear before.

# Thread: My sleep leptin and adenosine links showing up:  Mainstream
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-08T03:30:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91784>

[http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2013/08/06/how-sleep-loss-adds-to-weight-gain/](http://well.blogs.nytimes.com/2013/08/06/how-sleep-loss-adds-to-weight-gain/)

Losing sleep tends to make people eat more and gain weight, and now a new study suggests that one reason may be the impact that sleep deprivation has on the brain. This is linked to orexin biology in the retina.

The research showed that depriving people of sleep for one night created pronounced changes in the way their brains responded to high-calorie junk foods. On days when the subjects had not had proper sleep, fattening foods like potato chips and sweets stimulated stronger responses in a part of the brain that helps govern the motivation to eat. But at the same time, the subjects experienced a sharp reduction in activity in the frontal cortex, a higher-level part of the brain where consequences are weighed and rational decisions are made.

The findings suggested that one unfortunate result of sleep loss is this â€œdouble hitâ€￾ in brain activity, said Matthew P. Walker, an author of the study and a professor of psychology and neuroscience at the University of California, Berkeley. A sleepy brain appears to not only respond more strongly to junk food but also has less ability to reign that impulse in.

Some experts have theorized that in a sleep-deprived state, people eat more food simply to make up for all the calories they expend as they burn the midnight oil. But the new study showed that the changes in brain activity were evident even when the subjects were fed extra food and not experiencing any increased sensations of hunger.

Their hunger was no different when they were sleep-deprived and when they had a normal night of sleep, Dr. Walker said. That's important because it suggests that the changes we're seeing are caused by sleep deprivation itself, rather than simply being perhaps more metabolically impaired when you're sleep deprived. This is due to a defect in the adenosine signal due to a loss of orexin neuron loss.

The relationship between sleep loss and weight gain is a strong one, borne out in a variety of studies over the years. Large population studies show that both adults and children are more likely to be overweight and obese the less they sleep at night. In smaller, controlled studies, scientists find that when people are allowed to sleep eight hours one night and then half that amount on another, they end up eating more on the days when they've had less sleep. One pivotal study at the University of Colorado in March showed that losing just a few hours of sleep a few nights in a row caused people to pack on an average of about two pounds.

Other studies have found that the underlying effects of sleep deprivation on the body can in many ways be pronounced. The stress hormone cortisol climbs and markers of inflammation rise. Hormones that stimulate appetite increase, while hormones that blunt it drop. People become less sensitive to insulin, raising their risk of Type 2 diabetes.

But until now, few if any studies have looked at precisely what goes on in the brain when people are starved of sleep and then faced with food decisions. In the new study, which was published in the journal Nature Communications, Dr. Walker and his colleagues recruited 23 healthy men and women and assigned them to two different regimes, each separated by about a week. On one occasion, the subjects came into the lab and got a normal night of rest roughly eight hours before waking up to a small breakfast of toast and strawberry jam.

The subjects then looked at 80 pictures of a variety of foods and were asked to rate how strongly they wanted them while an imaging machine measured brain activity. The subjects were told that after looking through the pictures, they would receive one of the foods that they rated the highest.

On another occasion, the subjects followed the same routine, but this time, instead of sleeping, they stayed awake through the night. They were also given snacks like apples and peanut butter crackers to offset any extra calories that they burned while staying awake.

The research showed that when the subjects were bleary-eyed and sleep-deprived, they strongly preferred the food choices that were highest in calories, like desserts, chocolate, and potato chips. The sleepier they felt, the more they wanted calorie-rich foods. In fact, the foods they requested when they were sleep-deprived added up to about 600 calories more than the foods that they wanted when they were well-rested.

At the same time, brain scans showed that on the morning after the subject's sleepless night, the heavily caloric foods produced intense activity in an almond-shaped structure called the amygdala, which helps regulate basic emotions as well as our desires for things like food and experiences. That was accompanied by sharply reduced responses in cortical areas of the frontal lobe that regulate decision-making, providing top-down control of the amygdala and other primitive brain structures.

One expert who was not involved in the new study, Dr. Kenneth P. Wright Jr., called the findings exciting and said that they help explain why people make poor dietary choices and eat much more than they need to when fatigued.

There is something that changes in our brain when we are sleepy that irrespective of how much energy we need,â€￾ said Dr. Wright, the director of the sleep and chronobiology lab at the University of Colorado at Boulder. The brain wants more even when the energy need has been fulfilled.

But why would a lack of sleep disrupt the brain's response to food?

Dr. Walker said he suspected that one factor that plays a role is a substance called adenosine, a metabolic byproduct that disrupts neural function and promotes sleepiness as it accumulates in the brain. One of the ways that caffeine stimulates wakefulness is by blocking adenosine. Adenosine is also cleared from the system when we sleep.

Without enough rest, adenosine builds up and may start to degrade communication between networks in the brain, Dr. Walker said. Getting sleep may be the equivalent of rebooting the brain.

â€œI think you have about 16 hours of optimal functioning before the brain needs to go offline and sleep, he said. If you go beyond these 16 hours into the realm of sleep deprivation, then those brain networks start to break down and become dysfunctional.

Dr. Walker said it was increasingly clear from the medical literature that there is not a single tissue in the body that is not beneficially affected by sleep.

It's the single most effective thing people can do every day to reset their brain and body health, he said.

### cinnamon — 2013-08-08T04:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91793>

Wow. I'm thinking this might help to explain the behaviour of those in a manic state. My experience is with someone dear to me who visits this state (definitely triggered by light cycles and disrupted circadian rhythm) at least once a year - usually for a couple of months. There are stages to his behaviour. What I notice is that it is very childlike in some ways. In a positive way, he is open to new experiences and sees the value of everything. In a negative way, he loses his ability to make rational decisions that go beyond "I want this". So factoring in "can I afford this", "how does it affect others", etc. all but disappears. Like a child, he also reacts very emotionally. And gets a bit paranoid. It's tough to watch and there is no real point in trying to reason with him until the manic episode is over.

Oh, and he gorges on junk food. Doesn't gain weight though. Maybe because he is very active while in this state...

I think I have some reading and rereading to do here to try to understand this a little better.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-08-08T05:32:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91798>

Dr. Kruse, how can you get around sleep deprivation when it happens, and if possible, how can you sleep less? Dave Asprey has a series of blogs on hacking sleep but most delve into technology.

For instance, in the D-Ribose thread, you stated that one day you took 35g of D-Ribose because you only got 4 hours of sleep. Is that a technique since the D-Ribose allows us to create/replenish ATP quickly? Since I'm starting to study the Buteyko method, many practitioners state that the better you get your CP (control pause) the less you should be able to sleep while retaining your health - they show people who have incredibly high CP's (in the 60's) who can sleep about 4 hours or less.

But then again, maybe that shouldn't be our goal, since you have stated before that sleep is our primordial state.

### cinnamon — 2013-08-08T17:39:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-sleep-leptin-and-adenosine-links-showing-up-mainstream.7823/post-91824>

Well, he definitely \*can\* gain weight at other times. That happens during the slightly depressive episode that follows. It is accompanied by a lot of late night television and still more junk food.

My thoughts so far are that he is somebody who could really benefit from paying strict attention to circadian rhythm. He sees choosing to stay up all night as a personal choice and a part of who he is. I can't argue against free will and self-determination! LOL

I'm hopeful that when he gets some more of his higher reasoning back we'll be able to research some of it together because he is going to have to see it himself before he will attempt to change anything.
What was it Dr. K said about reserving a hopeful place for those you care most about?

# Thread: Hey folks I'm the unfortunate  King of Auto Immune Castle. About at my wits end....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 94
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AutoMoonMonster — 2013-08-08T05:08:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91794>

Sick with three auto-immune diseases for over 12 years (Alopecia Areata, Celiacs, and Ankylosing Spondy), I've tried everything (I think), everything has been a rabbit trail to disappointment. Paleo for two years, been cheating a bit lately, but strong avoidance of Gluten. Dairy and white rice on occasion. Still yet, even before cheating on Paleo my AS symptoms have been on fire lately! My back pain is so sharp sometimes, I could vomit. Anyone here that believes in Jack's routine, or better yet, has had success with it (EMF Rx)? He answered my wife's questions on FB promptly, which I thought was very kind. I'm seeing the top Naturpathic/ M.D./Homeopathic Physician in the country (Dr. Abram Ber) lots of cash and no results. He is 80 yrs. and knows just about every alternative therapy.......but.......never mentioned this EMF stuff. His big thing is avoidance of GMO/ grains and, of course, homeopathic treatment with detox. How do you start this EMF Boot Camp? Is it not just a money making gimmick? Are the results there? Shouldn't an established Neurosurgeon be making bucket loads of cash and not involved in Paleo forums. I don't say this to be challenging or offensive to Dr. Kruse. It's just that I'm in a funk lately. I feel this auto immune shit is hopeless. So can anyone help me. Please. Gary

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-08T05:20:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91797>

help you.......begin to read what I sent you and your wife. Assimilate it.......it wont cost you anything but time.

### caroline — 2013-08-08T08:07:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91805>

Just the fact that Dr. Kruse has answered you and your wife twice now should tell you everything you need to know about him. If you read his blogs, listen to his podcasts and read this forum \[all for free\] you will read of lots of our success stories and our struggles ....but we keep reading and asking questions and he keeps providing information to help us figure all this out .....we have to do the work. Everyone's health issues are totally their own ....Dr. K. spoke about that at length in the last webinar and Q&A. Everyone's issues are so different and so everyone's journey is very different. It is a very personal journey to figure out our own n=1.

You do have to start reading and decide for yourself if all this makes sense for you. A lot of us here have very real issues with wifi and EMF exposure and have noticed a huge improvement when we limit them as best we can.

I can only really speak for myself - The Leptin reset and C/T and light cycles and limiting EMF has been huge for me. It is not a fast fix by any stretch of the imagination ...but I was broken most of my life. I have come a huge way in the past 18 months and I would not trade this journey I have been on with Dr. Kruse for anything .....and I am really grateful that he is sharing all this with us ....but we do have to do the work ....that is rule #1 around here.

### Lahelada — 2013-08-08T13:29:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91816>

Is the answer worth 10 bucks ? If so why don't you just try and sleep under an emergency blanket, the foil type used for reheating the body after a trauma or hypothermia. Supposedly they are a shield against EMF . You will need to be careful to put the insulating side to the outside though. If you sleep better you have an answer . If you don't, use the blanket the other way round and see if that brings effects. Is this a proven method ? No. However if my reasoning is right you should not get cellphone or wifi signal under that blanket and that to me in your shoes would be worth 10 bucks for me. I must add that this is my idea, not a recommendation by DR K . I have no Dr or Ing to my name so you will have to decide if my idea has merit .

If it does not, take a week off in the wilderness away from any maddening crowd, cell towers and see if you have any improvements . If you let us know where you live we might be able to help with EMF reduced/free zones. If it does not make you feel different in the slightest you have still had a nice break which you may have needed in the first place. Don't expect huge changes from this though, just enough to wonder if there is something to that EMF lark

It is a case though of help thyself and I do appreciate that you may be sick of just trying by now. However the only way up is through . Just to let you know where I stand on the subject. When I first came here I thought the EMF lark was just a bit of new age woo woo . (Sorry Dr K) I am reconsidering though as I read myself cleverer.
If you don't like reading go on youtube. At worst you will get to laugh at the expense of people who wear tinfoil hats. At best you will find some things that make you go hmmm and make you dig further. A lot of digging involved here. On this journey you have to be bullheaded and allow for being wrong at the same time and develop the wisdom to know to differentiate as to what is called for when.

Good luck whatever you decide to do.

### nonchalant — 2013-08-08T17:46:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91825>

Hi AutoMoon (love that!), welcome to the forum! I think you can find some help here.
As Lahelada mentioned, have you taken a vacation out to the country and felt much better? More in control?
I think if you have been avoiding gluten, dairy, eggs and nightshades for so many years, and you have gotten only temporary relief, then you are left with the One Big Thing. EMF. But you also need to make sure your circadian clocks are set correctly, so that your body knows when to release various hormones and when to repair cell damage, etc.

> [AutoMoonMonster said:](/goto/post?id=91794)
>
> Shouldn't an established Neurosurgeon be making bucket loads of cash and not involved in Paleo forums. I don't say this to be challenging or offensive to Dr. Kruse. It's just that I'm in a funk lately. I feel this auto immune shit is hopeless. So can anyone help me. Please. Gary

You probably haven't heard Dr. K's story. Yeah, he was making tons of money cutting on people, but he found that most of his patients weren't even healthy enough to have a good surgical outcome. He was having to fix their various diseases first (diabetes, osteoporosis, etc) before he could operate. His patients were doing much better than expected. Then he found that he could often fix his patients without operating. You can read the rest, but we are so fortunate that he has this website.

### AutoMoonMonster — 2013-08-09T03:54:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91885>

I have been reading since 3 a.m. I also spent the better part of the morning on the phone with the retailers and inventors of Resonators and EMF Protective Clothing. I just ordered Jack's book on kindle from Amazon. Strange thing, I posted what I thought was some very useful info for this community. Everyone seems to have no idea of what resonators do and which one is a true Tesla Coil. I provided all that I had learned on my lengthy phone calls and yet, it said the moderator has not approved your message? I'm a 20-yr. major airline pilot with a fair bit of knowledge on electronics, radio waves, radar etc.... I pick up on this stuff fairly quickly. Additionally, I've been sick for almost 15 yrs. with some of the most serious Autoimmune conditions next to MS. I've joined several support groups and organizations as well as my case having been reviewed by the N.I.H. I've also travelled near and far to over 32 allopaths, 5 chiros, 3 naturpaths, 2 Energy Medicine Docs, 2 NAET pract., 4 Acupuncturist, 2 Dietitians, Mayo Clinic, Stony Brook Medical Research Division.... of which I can remember. When I joined here I could honestly say that I've tried everything. The Cryo Thermogenesis and Leptin Reset is new to me and very intriguing. ### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-09T05:37:37+0300

youre a pilot Gary: You live a disconnected life from the Earth much like a cosmonaut........want to know why you have multiple AI's? Read this excerpt........Readers Summary

How did my initial podcast set the table?
How did my job help me figure things outs?
Why was osteoporosis the key to the puzzle?
Why is the Factor X everyoneâ€™s solution?
Why crying over spilled milk is a waste of resources. We need to come together and think.
Dedicated to Alex B. in the hope that he can get his Dad out of the haze of dogma.

All science is based upon constant discovery. When you discover something new, embrace it and your curiosity because it then it becomes a new jumping point for the next journey in discovery. Alex, you are a starfish and today you get to understand how many of the moving parts of the Quilt work.

If you remember my podcast several years back, I told you the disease that got me interested in all this was osteoporosis. This is a disease I treat a lot as a spine surgeon. I discussed Wolffâ€™s law in detail in the EMF 1 blog. When I realized that Wolffâ€™s law was null and void in leptin resistance states in bone, something else radical appeared to me. I knew that Leptin Resistance signaled inflammation, and inflammation could be seen as a positive charge and loss of a negative charge on MRIâ€™s, fMRI, and MEG studies. I also realized that MRI or NMR should show an increased flair signal on T2 weighted MRI images signifying an intracellular water loss coupled with a loss of collagen and of ATP. Collagen makes up the main protein of connective tissues in all mammals, typically 60â€“70% by weight. The water and collagen constitute a liquid crystalline matrix in which every cell in the body is embedded, making this system the ideal medium for intercommunication in eutherian mammals. Within this matrix we have learned about proton and electron superconduction that not only feed the underlying mechanistic pathways that most biochemistry recite but they also feed a rich â€œwet chemistryâ€ that is fundamentally based upon special qualities of water. It turns out water has some prodigious quantum flexibility when it is confined in closed spaces that make it do some unique quirky things that no classic chemist would predict. These things are all predicted by quantum electrodynamic field theory. One such quirk is the use of water reverse micelles around proteins that increase enzyme action and kinetics by the order 10 to the 10th power based upon the pioneering work of Frank Mayer and Jim Clegg. Classic physics and chemistry thinks of water in its bulk form only because that is all the classic sciences can explain about it. In quantum coherence, where water acts as a total cohesive unit because it has infinite chemical freedom, water becomes highly structured chemically, from its bulk form to do some amazing things, to become extremely flexible in phase transitions between ice crystals and liquid crystals and gas vapor as it gets confined in small diameter nanotubes in our cells. Water dynamics and actions are quantumly altered as it is confined within nanotubes it develops properties that allow it to collectively work dynamically within reverse micelles and in proton superconducting cables that align along collagen in the cells cytoarchitecture to allow for super fast semiconduction. This has been reproduced by many physics and molecular biology researchers in electron photomicrographs and X-ray diffraction studies. The most amazing property water develops as it is confined in these nanotubes, it becomes a super catalyst that organic chemistry has missed, because it accounts for the energy of activation of biochemical reactions and enzymatic kinetics. It is not ATP that does this, ever!! ATP has another more critical function. It unfolds protein in cells to open water binding sites to form these super conduction cables to create 100% energy efficiency.

Doc, are you saying we are a quantum being? We use semi-conduction for energy production for life? In a word, YES. When water in nanotubes is coherent it becomes a super facilitator of all chemical reactions whether in re-dox form of a liquid crystalline structure. It offers amazing energy of activation flux to cause specific attraction between enzyme and substrate to facilitate precise timing needed for precise molecular reactions. Dr.Philippa Wiggins found water has an uncanny ability to move between its two states, the first called bulk flow state (classic physics) and coherent flow (quantum physics) using dynamic switching controlled by quantum field dynamics of the electro-magnetic field water finds itself in as water becomes constrained by the diameter in a carbon nanotubes in every cell of the human body. You wont find this in any paleo biochemistry book either folks. It is how the brain and every other system in the body works.

Water, it turns out, is the medium and the message used to integrate life in a cell, but it also forms the direct means of life in all living things. The medium and message are controlled by physics, and the means is controlled by chemistry, but the sum of all forms the biology of quantum water dynamics which rewrites cell theory as we know it. Yes, it changes everything you know.

High resolution X-ray diffraction studies on both native and synthetic collagen reveals an extensive water bridge network surrounding the collagen fibers.

Time to enter the world of quantum field theory and the world of semiconduction, Understanding human semiconduction:

How bone works is pretty simple. Bone is a semi conductor. In fact, every system in your body works that way. Anything that works by semi conduction is controlled by the math or quantum field theory and not classic physics, chemistry, or biology. It means what they all believe is up in smoke. Current in a semiconductor can flow through a crystal lattice in many phases, much either as free electrons or as holes that can shift their positions much as holes migrate when you move the marbles in a game of Chinese checkers over the board. When you understand bone has been proven to work via semiconduction the game of life changes. Nothing biology has figured out yet can explain semiconduction. The work of Peter Mitchell just does not hold up thermodynamics or on kinetic basis. So I knew it had to be explained somehow. So I went and looked for an answer. In bone, since current can flow from a P-type (positive) to an N-type (negative) semiconductor, but not the other way, a junction is created which acts to â€œfilterâ€ or rectify the current. This is how it is described by electrical engineers. Bone has a P and N type semi conductor and this is how I figured the riddle of the cosmonaut out. All this was worked out by Dr. Robert Becker in the 1960′s while it appears biology and chemistry slept. Bone uses quantum mechanics to work. I knew from my own studies in residency 1988, that bone was a special tissue, but after reading the cosmonauts reports, my life changed in a moment.

Explain briefly how bone works as a semiconductor

A good example of this is how the needle on an old phonograph worked. A diamond or sapphire crystal of the stylus needle rode in a record groove. The grooveâ€™s changing shape shape then deformed the crystal ever so slightly. The transformed crystalline lattice then transduced the stresses in its chemical lattice into an electrical signal. That signal was then amplified until the ear could hear it. If the current was not rectified, the sound created would be an unintelligible hum because we would be hearing both the deformation pulse and the release pulse of the semiconducted circuit. If we used a rectifier in this circuit we could hear the sounds. A rectifier passes current in one direction only, so the impulses can not cancel one another out. This is how music is heard from a record.

Dr. Becker was a limb regeneration researcher who described the semi conduction of bone in mammals and his group realized right away, that bone was showing evidence of rectification because boneâ€™s released electric pulse was much smaller than the one from the stresses placed upon the bone in experiments. All of these observations showed him definitively that a very small pulsed piezoelectric current signaled bone to grow. What is a piezoelectric current? Youâ€™ll find out soon enough.

He then wondered could he induce bone growth artificially, by stimulating a negative electric current. His experiments showed large amount of bone growth did occur around the negative electrode. Spine surgery was transformed in this one experiment. In 2005, I was clueless about this, and most scientists still do not know the details of how bone really works. You do now. It is a spectacular display of how quantum physics and biology worked in unison in an organ I operated on daily. If we have evidence of semi conduction in humans. It means the rules we employ in a biologic system must move from classic physics to quantum field theory. This is something paleo, medicine, and the biologic sciences does not know, and has never realized.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-09T05:37:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91900>

“During my reading, I found out when the Russian cosmonaut who went into space on the MIR space station for 1.3 years, who lost a massive amount of his bone density. I thought to myself, he had to have had massive inflammatory changes in his labs when they returned to earth. I saw the same disease in my clinic patients I mentioned earlier.

The Russians never told us about all of his labs in the accounts of this MIR trip. They were more intent on the good PR response ( I thought) because their cosmonaut just set the world record for consistent space travel by a human.

The reports of his bone loss, however caught everyone’s attention who read the accounts. Scientists in Russia, however, quickly realized the reason for the stunning loss of his bone mass in a short period of time, was due to a constant zero gravity environment they faced in space for 14 months, because of the absence of the earth’s magnetic field. The entire time in space, he ate a balanced diet with sufficient calories. They solved this problem by redesigning their space capsule with a magnetic system that duplicated the Earth’s magnetic field. They did this to provide a constant source of “gravitational force” to apply to the bones to stimulate tensions to fulfill Wolff’s law. On Earth, forces like gravity and acceleration stress bone cells to increase bone growth. We use it daily in spine surgery to help people get better from spine surgery.

Then I realized something they missed, in fact, everyone missed.

That was when a magnetic field’s effect is no longer sensed by human tissues, it also means electrons had to be transferred from one mass, his, to another of lesser electric charge by the laws of electricity. He lost his electrons to space! We use this principle in bone stimulators in spine surgery, also in reverse. We use small magnets to generate magnetic fields to increase bone growth. Essentially, the Russian cosmonaut lost electrons from his body into space to cause this osteoporosis in 437 days! Electrons flow from the largest pool of electron density to the small pool, this is why the Earth sends electrons into our body when we walk upon it. We evolved in the water and land, with our feet planted on it for reason, and when we are not planted upon we lose our own electrons in our body!”

This sounded hard to fathom when you first read it, I’d imagine. Today’s blog is filling in the details of precisely how this happens in everyone of you. Why has no one connected these dots until now? The reason is in how poorly Becker’s research described his own work in the literature of 50 years ago. In his reports they confused the negative potentials found in a salamander’s DC current of injury when he amputated their legs in experiments in his work on limb regeneration, with the negative potentials that actually stimulated bone growth regeneration naturally when the bone was broken. The reports were written as if both negative currents were equivalent to one another, but they were not physiologically. I picked this up and I went to the nth degree with it.

The cosmonaut observation made me realize this incongruency. Dr. Becker was an orthopedic surgeon and limb regeneration expert, not a quantum physics expert. He never realized how big this error was for man today. He realized this later in his own life in the 1990′s, because of how badly his bone stimulation work was capitalized by the implant industry in my current specialty, and said so publicly many times, as I later found out.

We use a negative electric current to stimulate bone today, but we never use the periosteal piezoelectric current Becker described in his work. This is how I knew no one caught the mistake I saw. There was not one implant ever made that copied Mother Nature’s semi-conduction design in Becker’s experiments. This is why bone growth stimulators have made a marginal impact in spine surgery and in orthopedics today.

In the 1950’s bone biology pioneer Robert Becker decided to see if he could figure out why salamanders could regenerate their entire leg when it was cut off, but mammals could not. He used detailed experiments to prove that bone regeneration was piezoelectric and worked by semi conduction. His results stunned the bone community. It seems it never made it to the rest of science, like biochemists to explain how it was powered. The inherent electron current of bone was described by Robert Becker, MD in detail in the 1960’s when he discovered that bone used piezoelectric currents to regenerate itself. Many people even today believe bone heals like any other tissue. That is not really true at all. Bone healing in humans is a vestige of true complete regeneration that we see in other species. Our bone regenerates just like a salamander regenerates its complete leg when it is amputated. He also showed definitively bone does not heal in the true sense. It totally regenerates itself using DC current to induce bone marrow cells to differentiate to osteoblasts

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-09T15:19:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-91923>

We all paint the picture of our lives with our actions and our perspectives, yet few of us realize we are the artist. What is important in life is life is how we choose to perceive the canvas; being happy doesn't mean that everything is perfect. It means that I have decided to look beyond the imperfections. People with the right perspective see opportunity everywhere, and they see problems as possibilities. We see the rainbow not just the storm. We see the potential in people. People in life are like gold. We must dig through a lot of dirt in order to experience their magic. When we find gold we must value it; look at the present moment with them as a gift, and stop using the future with them to escape the present. We need to marvel at the gifts we have now and everyday. We see the big picture in life. We realize the more we lift and light up others the more depth of field our painting takes on. The more we do this the more people are drawn to see how "we paint" our canvas. We are here to value life and the "right perspective" will often change everything.......a narrow perspective limits your vision of what is possible. It puts your mind in a box that is hard to escape. In life, we are always in the middle of someone's perspective, so we have to always stay in the shadow of our eternal promises, so we that we are at the head and not the foot of their perspectives. This is where the magic happens with others. As long as our perspective deals with what is beneficial and uplifting for others, there's really no problem at all. We can live in harmony with nature and with ourselves. Make these moments important, vital, and worth living. Do not let it slip away unnoticed and unused.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-08-12T01:21:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-92177>

> [AutoMoonMonster said:](/goto/post?id=92129)
>
> I read somewhere that no matter the destruction man causes, our Earth houses answers to remedy the problem. Seems a bit of a stretch. I mean, nuclear bombs? How to reverse that hell?

When I was in college I studied mycology--specifically medicinal mushrooms. After one of our week-long field trips collecting mushrooms up on the Olympic Peninsula of Washington, many of my fellow students and I were in the lab identifying our haul. One young man had brought in his guitar case and there was a sticker on it of a huge nuclear blast mushroom cloud. I had a distinct moment of clarity when I looked at that sticker and though, "The answer to the cancer is in the cloud," and indeed, many medicinal mushrooms have anti-tumor constituents. So, just as an example, I would agree with the idea that the Earth houses all the remedies. The problem is that humans are destroying things at such a phenomenal pace that it's unlikely that all the remedies can be found before they are lost, or before we are.

Do the things you can do, keep quiet ( I don't mean don't post but keep your mind quiet), ground yourself whenever you are not flying and do CT. I only know that you have exhausted the wisdom of the best naturopath in the country and that you have issues with just about everything that is being said to you, that you want incontrovertible proof,that you are able to understand because of your technical background. ### SeaHorse — 2013-08-12T16:01:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hey-folks-im-the-unfortunate-king-of-auto-immune-castle-about-at-my-wits-end.7824/post-92229>

I agree...great post.

Gary....your frustration is understandable...you want to feel better and you want to do something to make that happen. But Dr. K can't prescribe the magnetico like an absolute....it may be just the thing for one person and put another person into a detox that is too much for their system. Does it have an affect? Yes!, Do we know what it is, or how it will be for each person? No. Is it a good idea? It has been for me.

Searching for answers to feeling bad is so multifaceted that you can't just buy what everybody else is using and expect to feel better. Pretend you have all the time in the world....take one aspect of your life and truly change it...see what happens, take another one and do the same thing...then you can get to a place where you will have a clearer sense of what is going to work for you. AND it's ok to experiment and make mistakes. I stopped beating myself up about trying new things that didn't work out so well...isn't it better to try and keep moving than to freeze up with worry over the possibility of failure?

It's never ending....I'm long past the stage where I thought every new bit of information was going to be THE ONE that changed it all for me.....there is just too much. At the same time....it does all boil down to electrons, photons, water and the magnetic field you live in.....for me that has become the calming centre amidst all the swirling confusion of information. Do what you can at that level....it will change you.

Gary, start simply with the fundamentals that don't cost much or anything and give you tangible results.

1\. Implement as best you can the circadian guidelines. wake with the sun. Avoid artificial light after sunset with candles and blue blockers if necessary. don,t eat or drink any calories within four hours of going to sleep. very low carb in the low light cycles.

2\. Adopt a paleo or epi paleo diet. lots of animal fat.

3.If you think you are leptin resistant do the leptin rx found on this site.

the issues you are dealing with are complex and unique to you. lay down these foundations while you research the nuances and ways to deal with them. Don' t look at the scale and don,t worry about working out for now. walk barefoot foot if possible and go for a cold swim or shower everyday.

Okay, pilot, those are simple directions that will start you on the ancient pathway.You will feel better within days. When you start feeling better you can move on.
