### ColdKnight — 2013-08-14T05:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92469>

I drink lots of mineralized RO water. I haven't had a herniation in a few months. I know CT helps a ton. I did 7 days straight down to 50 degree water up to my neck, but I stopped for a week and a half or so. I'm not sure if I should start where I left off or start over with spot icing. I am 30 yr old male with low body fat. Been having trouble avoiding sugar.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-08-14T07:09:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92471>

Anybody who thinks marijuana is bad for us is sorely mistaken. We have an endocannabinoid system within us - it accounts for the cannabinoids found in marijuana (and some other things that activate it, like the anandamides in chocolate, I believe). There are hundreds of compounds in marijuana, and they all have different effects. CBD, for instance, is a cannabinoid that, if I remember correctly, doesn't get one high, but has a large therapeutic effect. Then there are 'hyper' concentrates such as budder, which are 99% THC compared to the upper 20's in high grade marijuana leaves (i.e. you can smoke much less and get an even stronger effect).

I no longer smoke weed because after an event which really screwed with my brain, hence how I wound up here - anytime I would use it I would not only get anxious, but I would almost have flashbacks, have odd pressure buildup in my head like my skull was going to implode, etc. That doesn't mean I think it is bad now, I think more people should use marijuana and there isn't enough research (although there is a lot, just not enough) on marijuana and psychedelics as options for healing and/or spiritual/consciousness progression.

Take a look at these pages for loads of FACTUAL information: [http://www.erowid.org/plants/cannabis/cannabis.shtml](http://www.erowid.org/plants/cannabis/cannabis.shtml) (this site is a fact based site on drugs to prevent deaths and other negative effects from using substances). Here is a rebuttal to one of the previous statements - [http://www.erowid.org/plants/cannabis/cannabis\_myth8.shtml](http://www.erowid.org/plants/cannabis/cannabis_myth8.shtml) marijuana does not cause brain damage or any IQ loss. There original study was used in Rhesus monkeys and I believe they even exposed them to THC via gas masks therefore they lacked oxygen AND they were taking in absurd amounts of THC in a short span.

Watch this: [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jxJmFSmOrqo](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jxJmFSmOrqo) (cannabis as a nutritious vegetable that can be eaten raw/juiced) AND:
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aGjC4HReFL0](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aGjC4HReFL0) (Rick Simpson Hemp Oil documentary - a 'hyper' concentrated source that has cured tumors, brain cancers, etc.)

This is not to advocate its use for everyone at every imaginable time, though. As I said, I no longer use it - I would in the future if my brain 'heals' itself and I feel safe with myself enough to use some, but right now that's not a choice.

### Eve — 2013-08-14T15:36:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92490>

> [ColdKnight said:](/goto/post?id=92465)
>
> What if one ingests it orally? or transdermally? Smoking is not the only method of ingestion. Those studies showing degraded iq could be propaganda from the pharmaceutical companies that don't want to lose a ton of money.

This is why for treating AIDS patients and others with debilitating conditions that have not responded to other treatment methods, researchers are looking into developing a THC patch, etc. Also, as I recall, ingesting marij. in food, for example, can have negative effects like nausea and vomiting for some people. Additionally, the effects of THC are significantly less through this method due to the first-pass effect.

Also, I would have to disagree that the study suggesting decreases in IQ in adolescents who smoked marij. into adulthood is "propoganda" from pharmaceutical companies. (Note that this study is different than the one conducted with rhesus monkeys. The sample in this case is humans). If anything, I think pharmaceutical companies would want people to think there are positive effects to THC, so they can market their synthetic versions (that might bypass the respiratory effects of smoked weed).Here is a link to the abstract of the former:[http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2012/08/22/1206820109.abstract](http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2012/08/22/1206820109.abstract) By clicking on "Author Affiliations", you can see that this study was conducted by authors affiliated with Duke University, etc.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-14T15:51:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92493>

### ColdKnight — 2013-08-14T17:53:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92502>

I'm always skeptical of these long term studies. They never account for other variables like the diet of each of their subjects. Another item they don't control for is other drugs used. I'm curious as to what is their control group? How do they know what their functioning would have been like without the cannabis? If one is not careful, cannabis can cause great appetite for brain devolving sugary foods. Starting at age 13 probably isn't the best idea either. For pain from inflammation, used carefully, I think it could be used without hampering ones brain power significantly or at all. As far as the pharmaceutical companies, they can't patent the herb so they are trying to separate out just the THC compound. The problem is, thc is only one of many components to the herb that are needed for therapeutic benefit. Therefore, they realize they can't make money from it so they demonize it.

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-08-14T19:47:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92526>

[http://libertycrier.com/top-10-cannabis-studies-the-government-wished-it-had-never-funded/](http://libertycrier.com/top-10-cannabis-studies-the-government-wished-it-had-never-funded/)
"Veterans Affairs scientists looked at whether heavy marijuana use as a young adult caused long-term problems later, studying identical twins in which one twin had been a heavy marijuana user for a year or longer but had stopped at least one month before the study, while the second twin had used marijuana no more than five times ever. Marijuana use had no significant impact on physical or mental health care utilization, health-related quality of life, or current socio-demographic characteristics."

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1253627/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1253627/) - here is a study that shows a synthetic cannabinoid increases hippocampal neurogenesis

[http://norml.org/component/zoo/category/cannabis-and-the-brain-a-user-s-guide:](http://norml.org/component/zoo/category/cannabis-and-the-brain-a-user-s-guide:) "Not only has modern science refuted the notion that marijuana is neurotoxic, recent scientific discoveries have indicated that cannabinoids are, in fact, neuroprotective, particularly against alcohol-induced brain damage. In a recent preclinical study -- the irony of which is obvious to anyone who reads it -- researchers at the US National Institutes of Mental Health (NIMH) reported that the administration of the non-psychoactive cannabinoid cannabidiol (CBD) reduced ethanol-induced cell death in the brain by up to 60 percent."

"the Journal of Neurological Sciences recently pronounced, "There is accumulating evidence ... to support the hypothesis that the cannabinoid system can limit the neurodegenerative processes that drive progressive disease,"

"But what about claims of cannabis' damaging effect of cognition? A review of the scientific literature indicates that rumors regarding the "stoner stupid" stereotype are unfounded. According to clinical trial data published this past spring in the American Journal of Addictions, cannabis use -- including heavy, long-term use of the drug -- has, at most, only a negligible impact on cognition and memory."

I'm sure this made me look like the largest pot head ever (I'm not!), but I just see NO reliable evidence whatsoever that ANY form of marijuana consumption is bad in any way. For instance, on smoking marijuana - "Researchers at the Kaiser-Permanente HMO, funded by NIDA, followed 65,000 patients for nearly a decade, comparing cancer rates among non-smokers, tobacco smokers, and marijuana smokers. Tobacco smokers had massively higher rates of lung cancer and other cancers. **Marijuana smokers who didn’t also use tobacco had no increase in risk of tobacco-related cancers or of cancer risk overall. In fact their rates of lung and most other cancers were slightly lower than non-smokers, though the difference did not reach statistical significance**."

It's all in how you use it.

### Eve — 2013-08-14T23:34:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92559>

My reply with quotes isn't working, but this is primarily directed at ColdKnight's question earlier...

I think you are (or any motivated reader is) right to be skeptical of any study before you read it in whole and evaluate its reliability and validity. The longitudinal study I was referencing earlier did have a control group (never used group) and they did exclude for such things as persistent tobacco dependence, persistent hard-drug and/or alcohol dependence, etc. As you probably are aware, it's really best to read the whole article (of any research study, whether it purports or negates benefits of cannabis use) if are able to given you have access to an Online Journal Database.

It might be true that pharmaceutical companies "demonize" the use of street cannabis, but I am not sure how you are deducing that. The studies I have read and I think even the ones the other poster linked to here, were NOT funded by any pharmaceutical company but were initiated in universities, medical centres, etc.
Additionally, as far as I'm aware, pharmaceutical companies are isolating other components of cannabis aside from THC that have been proven effective in clinical trials (for a particular population, e.g., patients with AIDS wasting syndrome).

### Eve — 2013-08-15T04:57:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-92596>

> Eve I did read what you posted. You didn't address the diet of these people. Also, I did concur on the fact that you probably shouldn't persistently use it starting at age 13. Isolating one part of the herb wont have the benefits we are talking about. The compounds seem to have a synergistic effect(meaning all terpenes and cannabinoids work towards a combined effect). Therefore, pharmaceutical companies can't profit, because they can't patent the whole herb.

I cannot be 100% certain but considering that SES (socioeconomic status -- e.g., income) (among other variables) was equally distributed among the groups that diet type was as well. Although they didn't measure this variable, based on principles of statistical analysis, I would bet that diet type (as was SES) was normally distributed (i.e., forming a bell curve) in the populations anyway. (Not to mention that the majority of the general population eats SAD). This would mean that when two groups (e.g., weed users vs. non-weed users) were compared that differences could not be attributed to diet.

I'd also like to point out the obvious...none of the studies (that I'm aware of) that purport the benefits of cannabis have controlled for diet either. I could just as well say that the pain-reducing effects seen in cancer patients using cannabis were because of their diet compared to controls and nothing to do with cannabis...A claim which is highly unlikely. My main point is that if one is going to criticize a study for lacking inclusion of X, then they have to apply the same critical principles when evaluating studies that they (seemingly) favour.

> Therefore, pharmaceutical companies can't profit, because they can't patent the whole herb

It would depend on what effect the pharmaceutical companies hoped to profit on.....I have more to say here but I'll just leave it at that. My goal is not to sway your opinion. And I really don't have the time to go back and forth about this (nothing to do with you or anything).

### BigPapaChakra777 — 2013-08-26T01:52:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-93768>

Just to restart this conversation, at least a bit, I found some interesting information after reviewing the video I posted about 'cannabis as an extremely nutritious vegetable'. Well, this just might be true:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eRLVyGfGcZs](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eRLVyGfGcZs) (Dr. William Courtney and the dietary benefits of raw Cannabis Pt.1)
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kMAug\_V5AX0](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kMAug_V5AX0) (Pt.2)
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qgEP9FdIzT8#t=141](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qgEP9FdIzT8#t=141) (Power of Raw Cannabis; Another video, much shorter, from Dr. William Courtney)
[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qa0nLdVJiIg#t=13](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qa0nLdVJiIg#t=13) (Leaf - Dr. William Courtney and an anecdotal report of a young woman and juicing raw cannabis)
[http://www.whydontyoutrythis.com/20...f-drinking-rather-than-smoking-cannabis.html:](http://www.whydontyoutrythis.com/2013/04/the-importance-of-drinking-rather-than-smoking-cannabis.html:)
"Cannabis clinician Dr. William Courtney recommends drinking 4 - 8 ounces of bud and leaf juice of any Hemp plant, 5 mg of CBD per kg of body weight, a salad of Hemp seed sprouts and 50 mg of THC daily, divided into 5 doses with an Arachadonic acid source, such as Hemp seed oil.

“If you heat the plant, you will decarboxylate THC-acid and you will get high, you"ll get your 10 mg. If you don't heat it, you can go up to five or six hundred milligrams & use it as a Dietary Cannabis. . . and push it up to the Anti-oxidant and Neuro-protective levels which come into play at hundreds of milligrams,” stated Dr. William Courtney.

“The amount of Cannabinoids we need would be difficult to get through smoked Cannabis without feeling the intoxicating effects,” said Kristen Courtney."

So, as you can see, this is something entirely different than using it strictly as a medicine in the form of smoke, vapor, edible, tincture, or any 'concentrate' (hash, oil, 'budder', 'honey') - it is used as a 'dietary essential'. I think this is also something entirely different than just going and getting hemp seeds or hemp protein, this is actual marijuana being juiced or eaten raw as a vegetable of sorts. CBD, a non-psychoactive cannabinoid, is apparently more potent than Vitamins C and E. Interesting to say the least.

>
> This is why for treating AIDS patients and others with debilitating conditions that have not responded to other treatment methods, researchers are looking into developing a THC patch, etc. Also, as I recall, ingesting marij. in food, for example, can have negative effects like nausea and vomiting for some people. Additionally, the effects of THC are significantly less through this method due to the first-pass effect.
>
> Also, I would have to disagree that the study suggesting decreases in IQ in adolescents who smoked marij. into adulthood is "propoganda" from pharmaceutical companies. (Note that this study is different than the one conducted with rhesus monkeys. The sample in this case is humans). If anything, I think pharmaceutical companies would want people to think there are positive effects to THC, so they can market their synthetic versions (that might bypass the respiratory effects of smoked weed).Here is a link to the abstract of the former:[http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2012/08/22/1206820109.abstract](http://www.pnas.org/content/early/2012/08/22/1206820109.abstract) By clicking on "Author Affiliations", you can see that this study was conducted by authors affiliated with Duke University, etc.

The study you linked used just over 50 adolescent onset of heavy use, which is a low number to draw any conclusions on. People do more than just smoke cannabis, maybe they chOw down on Doritos and suck back cola,too. Many lifestyle factors can lower one’s IQ.

> Valerie Curran, professor of psychopharmacology at University College London and a member of the UK's Independent Scientific Committee on Drugs, was more sceptical, saying that a other factors, such as depression, are also associated with heavy use and reduced motivation. “Although the overall sample size is excellent, the data on adolescent onset of heavy use is based on just over 50 people.

She also noted that the findings represent a very small decline in IQ as a result of **very heavy** (my bold) use over a number of years, “which doesn’t relate to recreational use”.”

This study using twins, with over 3000 subjects found no effect on iq :
[https://www.sciencealert.com/extens...between-marijuana-use-and-iq-decline-in-teens](https://www.sciencealert.com/extensive-twin-study-finds-no-link-between-marijuana-use-and-iq-decline-in-teens)

### Bic — 2017-11-20T13:20:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thoughts-on-the-use-of-medical-cannabis-to-fight-inflammation-and-high-blood-sugar.7892/post-228479>

I use this 16% CBD extract to help with pain and spasticity;
[http://shop.medropharm.ch/produkte/cbd-products-7/cbd-extract-11/16-cbd-extract-10g-50?p=0&d=2](http://shop.medropharm.ch/produkte/cbd-products-7/cbd-extract-11/16-cbd-extract-10g-50?p=0&d=2)
It tastes of the earth. I take a portion about the size of a pea once or twice a day. My CRP is low but blood sugar is on the higher side of normal.
Some people may think that 2 or 3 dollars a day id pricy, but if you can walk better. with less pain it is worth it to me.

# Thread: Falling off the CT wagon and getting back on
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdKnight — 2013-08-14T06:15:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92470>

I was successfully CT'ing 7 days in a row at 50 degrees everything but hands in for 25 minutes. I would reheat in about 30 minutes. I noticed various benefits even with just 7 days under my belt. I was more tolerable of the cold, felt more energetic and smarter, and was more resistant to pain to name a few. I did spot icing with 20lbs of ice on my torso for 3 days or so before I built an outdoor tub and started the full immersions. Does anyone have experience with stopping the protocol and getting back on the wagon? Should I start with spot icing again to make sure I don't get cold uticarra before switching back to the immersions again? How long does it take to unadapt?

### nonchalant — 2013-08-14T15:48:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92492>

No, just jump back in. Depending on how long it has been, you might take a couple baths before you are back to your normal reaction to the cold.

### ColdKnight — 2013-08-14T17:59:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92505>

Sweet! I will report my findings soon  I forgot to mention it has been about 2 weeks since I stopped.

### ColdKnight — 2013-08-15T20:20:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92653>

I'm so excited to find that after taking a break from CT for about 2.5 weeks, I was easily able to restart the full immersions. I feel like my thermoregulation was actually better than my last CT before the break. I do have to admit that I prepped myself pretty well by not eating sugar foods the day of, and working on keeping my o3 to o6 ratio along with magnesium optimal. I had two 3 egg yolk/cinnamon/vanilla/grassfed butter/cacao Bulletproof coffees spread out(one in morning, one midday,) 4 eggs fried over easy & bacon, and seared salmon with caramelized onions & carrots for dinner pre-CT. I went for 15 minutes as opposed to my usual 25 in 50-54 degree water.

### ColdKnight — 2013-08-15T20:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92655>

Anyone else have experience with starts and stops? What is the longest time someone has went without and came back? How was the experience starting again?

### endless — 2013-08-15T20:24:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92658>

I'm about to restart after a hiatus....and I've been craving it so I think re-entry is going to be just fine!

### cantweight — 2013-08-15T20:45:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92661>

Ive started and stopped 100 times...selling a house, moving, renovating....for a million reasons I lost tub time. I always jumped back in to a full soak and often with a renewed vigor for colder temps and longer times. My body craves CT. As soon as my kids are back to school I am back in the tub....draggin my hubby with me....it was fascinating for me to watch the effects it had on him as I am fat and he is fit so very different experiences

### nicld — 2013-08-15T20:58:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92663>

I would LOVE to get my hubby to try it but his response is H E Double Hockey sticks NO. Some of my healthy living is rubbing off on him but not enough and it just makes me kinda sad. Hate to say it, but I don't want to have to take care of him when his health is failing and mine is strong because of the food and lifestlye choices that he made. I want the both of use to grow old together and healthy. Maybe someday he'll get it and yes I have asked him to read some of the stuff I am reading but he declines.

### ColdKnight — 2013-08-16T01:05:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92692>

Ahh yes nicld quite the quandary. I haven't found a wife yet and I would love for her to be into this. Sadly, I feel we striving for optimal are a minority of the population. Maybe though. It's probably best to have faith that things will go the way you want them to.

### cantweight — 2013-08-17T02:10:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92874>

My hubs and kids thought I was nuts!

Hubs smiled and nodded as I went on and on and on about things I read in Dr. K's blog.

5 weeks later the twinkle in my eyes and spring in my step had them lining up to get in the tub!

I was shocked! Hubs ended up encouraging me to add more ice and up my frequency. He got on my case about EMF testing....I was speechless.

Just give it time

### caroline — 2013-08-17T02:20:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/falling-off-the-ct-wagon-and-getting-back-on.7894/post-92877>

As I have said before and found to be so true ..... when you walk the walk - you get to talk the talk......people are observing you - they really are paying attention ... make no mistake about that....

From my (very limited) understanding of this stuff...Dr. K talks at length about Seafood because it is some of the most electron/energy dense food on the planet.
Because we are living in a very altered field (which fundamentally dictates our epigenetics ) we "are constantly losing energy to our environment". So eating the most energy dense foods can have more of a buffering effect.
So if our field/environment is constantly weakening our immune system, stressing our beings, and adding to the cortisol/inflammatory response..our natural ability to DETOXIFY from exposure to GMOs, Chemicals, Metals etc etc is severely undermined.

Having said that I still feel that EACH of these things will affect every one of us completely differently. So testing makes the most sense.
I feel that ALL of these puzzle pieces: diet, detoxing, boosting immune system/organ function etc etc and limiting exposure (to EMFs) simultaneously makes the most sense to me. But the part they play in each of our health and well being will likely vary quite a bit.

# Thread: GERD without burning, heaviness in stomach
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gerd-without-burning-heaviness-in-stomach.7898/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 35
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-08-14T11:29:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gerd-without-burning-heaviness-in-stomach.7898/post-92479>

Hi guys,

So yeah my digestive health is a bit of a mess but two of the symptoms I have are really confusing. Whenever I eat, especially if it's a larger meal, I get these 2 symptoms, amongst overs:

1\. My stomach feels heavy and has an uncomfortable outward pressure type feeling. It feels like the food sits in my stomach for too long and makes me feel full even though my mouth feels hungry (if that makes any sense).

This sounded to me like gastroparesis or low stomach acidity but I have had a barium swallow which came back fine and I have experimented with very high doses of Betaine HCL to no good effect.

2\. If I have a decent sized tea (supper for Americans I think) when I got to lay down at night it feels as though the food is coming back up through my oesophagus. This does not burn at all and has never lead me to actually being sick.

Once again I would think this is gastroparesis...

This happens regardless of what foods I eat and has even happened with just water.

Any theories/opinions? Thanks.

### Dextery — 2013-08-15T19:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gerd-without-burning-heaviness-in-stomach.7898/post-92648>

This physician in Wales had her license suspended for advocating a paleo diet. She had to go before a board to make her case that she was not injuring anyone. She did get her license to practice restored. Dr Sara Myhill [http://www.drmyhill.co.uk/](http://www.drmyhill.co.uk/) She does not tow the line of the NHS. Maybe too far away from you but she has good reviews.

Grounding your laptop video: [http://jackkruse.com/watch/grounding-your-computer/](http://jackkruse.com/watch/grounding-your-computer/)

I also built a grounding pad under my desk by getting a piece of aluminum screen and anchoring the screen with duct tape to the hard plastic mat I have so my chair can roll. I had an old printer electrical cord that i cut off the female end, found the ground wire and attached that ground wire to an alligator clip which I clip onto the aluminum screen. On the male end of the old printer cord I cut off the two prongs leaving me with just the ground prong to plus into an electrical outlet. Now the aluminum screen is grounded and I always sit with barefeet firming in contact with the aluminum screen for grounding.

Sean, 2 years or so ago I had some bad stomach pain and ended up in the hospital. They did every test imaginable (typical hospital tests - blood or equipment) and found nothing. They told me I didn't have celiac (hadn't been eating gluten for over 4 months though) and they told me I didn't have Candida. I left the next day and continued with the leptin reset that I had started earlier that summer. Well, that didn't fix me. I ended up doing a metametrix gi effects test I believe after reading out here about it and that told me everything. I have had about 5 now since as I'm still working on my gut.

I have been low carb for years, paleo for a few after that and then a little more epi-paleo. What I can tell you is that I am working with a functional med doc on all of this including stage 7 adrenal fatigue, heavy metals, thyroid, gut disbyosis and hormomes and things are still not right. I have been treated for parasites 2 times now and still have them. Same with H-pylori and some yeasty thing. I have been on natural supps and standard meds to try and fix things and so far neither is working. I see my doc again this Wed to determine which route to go next. It takes a very good doc and a long time to fix the gut. Jack talks about epi-paleo fixing the gut. I haven't gone that route full on. My point is this, trying something for a couple of weeks isn't going to fix anything. You have to first really know what you are dealing with and go after it long and hard.

Hang in there and do what you can to build up the good bacteria.

# Thread: so, you think epigenetics is slowing down huh?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-epigenetics-is-slowing-down-huh.7899/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-14T15:27:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-you-think-epigenetics-is-slowing-down-huh.7899/post-92489>

[http://www.dailygalaxy.com/my\_weblo...Great+Discoveries+Channel:+Sci,+Space,+Tech.)](http://www.dailygalaxy.com/my_weblog/2013/08/human-evolutionary-change-100-times-higher-in-past-5000-years-todays-most-popular.html?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+TheDailyGalaxyNewsFromPlanetEarthBeyond+%28The+Daily+Galaxy+--Great+Discoveries+Channel%3A+Sci%2C+Space%2C+Tech.%29)

"We are more different genetically from people living 5,000 years ago than they were different from Neanderthals," according to John Hawks -University of Wisconsin anthropologist. "Five thousand years is such a small sliver of time - it's 100 to 200 generations ago. That's how long it's been since some of these genes originated, and today they are in 30 or 40 percent of people because they've had such an advantage. It's like 'invasion of the body snatchers.'What's really amazing about humans," Hawks continued, "that is not true with most other species, is that for a long time we were just a little ape species in one corner of Africa, and weren't genetically sampling anything like the potential we have now."

In a finding that countered a common theory that human evolution has slowed to a crawl or even stopped in modern humans, a study examining data from an international genomics project describes the past 40,000 years as a time of supercharged evolutionary change, driven by exponential population growth and cultural shifts. The findings may lead to a very broad rethinking of human evolution, especially in the view that modern culture has essentially relaxed the need for physical genetic changes in humans to improve survival.
In 2007, a team led by University of Wisconsin-Madison anthropologist John Hawks estimated that positive selection just in the past 5,000 years alone -dating back to the Stone Age - has occurred at a rate roughly 100 times higher than any other period of human evolution. Hawks is Professor of Anthropology at the University of Wisconsinâ€”Madison and Associate Chair of Anthropology, a Howard Hughes Medical Institute (HHMI) Faculty Fellow, and an associate member of both the Department of Zoology and the J. F. Crow Institute for the Study of Evolution. Many of the new genetic adjustments, Hawks observes, are occurring around changes in the human diet brought on by the advent of agriculture, and resistance to epidemic diseases that became major killers after the growth of human civilizations.

"In evolutionary terms, cultures that grow slowly are at a disadvantage, but the massive growth of human populations has led to far more genetic mutations," says Hawks. "And every mutation that is advantageous to people has a chance of being selected and driven toward fixation. What we are catching is an exceptional time."

While the correlation between population size and natural selection is nothing new - it was a core premise of Charles Darwin, Hawks says - the ability to bring quantifiable evidence to the table is a new and exciting outgrowth of the Human Genome Project.

In the hunt for recent genetic variation in the genome map the project has cataloged the individual differences in DNA called single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs). The project has mapped roughly 4 million of the estimated 10 million SNPs in the human genome. Hawks' research focuses on a phenomenon called linkage disequilibrium (LD). These are places on the genome where genetic variations are occurring more often than can be accounted for by chance, usually because these changes are affording some kind of selection advantage.

The researchers identify recent genetic change by finding long blocks of DNA base pairs that are connected. Because human DNA is constantly being reshuffled through recombination, a long, uninterrupted segment of LD is usually evidence of positive selection. Linkage disequilibrium decays quickly as recombination occurs across many generations, so finding these uninterrupted segments is strong evidence of recent adaptation, Hawks says.

Employing this test, the researchers found evidence of recent selection on approximately 1,800 genes, or 7 percent of all human genes.

This finding runs counter to conventional wisdom in many ways, Hawks says. For example, there's a strong record of skeletal changes that clearly show people became physically smaller, and their brains and teeth are also smaller. This is generally seen as a sign of relaxed selection - that size and strength are no longer key to survival.

But other pathways for evolution have opened, Hawks says, and genetic changes are now being driven by major changes in human culture. One good example is lactase, the gene that helps people digest milk. This gene normally declines and stops activity about the time one becomes a teenager, Hawks says. But northern Europeans developed a variation of the gene that allowed them to drink milk their whole lives - a relatively new adaptation that is directly tied to the advance of domestic farming and use of milk as an agricultural product.

The biggest new pathway for selection relates to disease resistance, Hawks says. As people starting living in much larger groups and settling in one place roughly 10,000 years ago, epidemic diseases such as malaria, smallpox and cholera began to dramatically shift mortality patterns in people. Malaria is one of the clearest examples, Hawks says, given that there are now more than two dozen identified genetic adaptations that relate to malaria resistance, including an entirely new blood type known as the Duffy blood type.

Another recently discovered gene, CCR5, originated about 4,000 years ago and now exists in about 10 percent of the European population. It was discovered recently because it makes people resistant to HIV/AIDS. But its original value might have come from obstructing the pathway for smallpox.

"There are many things under selection that are making it harder for pathogens to kill us," Hawks says.

Population growth is making all of this change occur much faster, Hawks says, giving a tribute to Charles Darwin. When Darwin wrote in "Origin of the Species" about challenges in animal breeding, he always emphasized that herd size "is of the highest importance for success" because large populations have more genetic variation, Hawks says.

The parallel to humans is obvious: The human population has grown from a few million people 10,000 years ago to about 200 million people at A.D. 0, to 600 million people in the year 1700, to more than 6.5 billion today. Prior to these times, the population was so small for so long that positive selection occurred at a glacial pace, Hawks says.

The Wisconsin study was published the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

The Daily Galaxy via University of Wisconsin and [http://johnhawks.net/weblog/hawks/hawks.html](http://johnhawks.net/weblog/hawks/hawks.html)

Highly interesting video from way back when from Swiss TV I believe about subjecting plants and fish to electric fields . It is in swiss german and unfortunately I have not got the time to translate today. Tomorrow I will . Basically the idea is that plants and fish where subjected to a static electric field for 4 weeks . The fish were the most impressive. Fish were 30 % bigger,meatier,more coloured ,stronger teeth and their survival rate increased. The trout that was the result of breeding in this electric field is actually a recreation of a trout that became extinct , a paleo fish if you will . This new old fish does not need mollycoddling it is fine as it is ,so does not need the chemistry normal farmed fish needs.
For the german speakers here it is today.

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mCxbqe6wn6U](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mCxbqe6wn6U)

When I first saw it I was impressed but having read this morning this interview with Dr Ludwig I all over sudden have a new perspective on what I would like to do with myself, no idea how yet but a new line of investigation.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urzeit-code-or-how-make-a-plant-or-animal-remember-what-it-looked-like-in-paleo-times.7901/post-92848>

There is electric tension between the ionosphere and the ground which mimics the parallel capacitance plates. JK has mentioned the (+) atmospheric tension previously.

[http://www.timewaver-gesundheitszen...ie--chevalier--source-of-earths-potential.pdf](http://www.timewaver-gesundheitszentrum.ch/de/docs/earthwaver-studie--chevalier--source-of-earths-potential.pdf)

I have been wondering if the density of the atmosphere plays a role in the atmospheric electric tension. There's a theory out there that the atmosphere was several hundred times denser in the Mesozoic era in which the buoyancy of the air could have helped support the weight of the big dinosaurs. If true, the atmosphere would have been akin to liquified gases at ground level. The air pressure wouldn't have been a problem since even at deep sea levels organisms already survive. So as a thought experiment I wondered whether the electric tension in the air would be greater or less in such an atmosphere. Wish I had a scientific background..

# Thread: CT for Bodybuilders
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 42
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Brick Fit House — 2013-08-14T19:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-92528>

This is a question for Dr. Kruse or anyone else who might have some insight/experience with this topic.

I am going to be competing in my first-ever bodybuilding competition. Today is 8/14/13 and the contest is on 4/5/14, so I've got LOTS of time. On September 1st I'll be moving into a basement apartment in Chicago (cold and grounded) that's about 15 minutes from Lake Michigan (easy access to natural CT!).

After studying Jack's writings for months, and with winter approaching in Chicago, I've decided to do the following to reap the benefits of cold adaptation:

\--keep my basement apartment at ~55ÂºF
\--take cold showers often (water utilities included in rent)
\--swim in Lake Michigan 3-4X/week
\--take advantage of 61ÂºF temp at workplace (I'm a trainer who works people out in the cold)
\--lift weights in ~45ÂºF room
\--eat like a Great White Shark (VLC, High Protein, High Fat, lots of seafood)
\--use a Cool Fat Burner icepack vest that I bought
\--whole-body cryotherapy 2X/week. I know it's not recommended or required, but I like to party.
\--practice my posing in trunks outside in the snow (just kidding)
\--seriously, I'm going to practice in the snow

The whole reason I'm doing this, what I'm really after is for the increased receptor binding affinity for the steroid hormones. If I understand correctly, the cold should jack up my anabolic growth factors while causing widespread apoptosis and a dramatic increase in BAT, killing my hunger and making me sleep like a dead guy.

I am not interested in athletic performance; not even a little bit. My only concern is accumulating the highest possible concentration of testosterone, hgh, etc., for the purpose of building the maximum-possible amount of muscle mass from now until April 5th, 2014.

So Dr. Kruse (et al)-- will chronic cold exposure get me contest-ready? Is there anything else you would recommend for a natural bodybuilder who is intent on raising his anabolic growth factors as high as they can naturally get? If YOU had to prepare for a bodybuilding competition, what would YOU do differently? Anything from the above list that you would exclude or that won't help much?

Any help/comments will be greatly appreciated. And, as this is my first post on the forum, thanks for having me here! Looking forward to sharing my experiences.

THANKS!

### Brick Fit House — 2013-08-15T01:37:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-92571>

> [ColdKnight said:](/goto/post?id=92560)
>
> Hmm I hope someone else can clarify for sure what Jack recommends.

As far as I'm aware, Dr. Kruse recommends an optimal o6/o3 ratio of 4:1. Cold-water marine mammals require closer to 1:1 and in this territory more subcutaneous fat is developed, which is great if you live in the cold ocean. Up to 6:1 is acceptable to Dr. Kruse, but much higher than that is SAD territory. If I remember correctly, when Dr. Kruse first drew his labs on this metric his o6/o3 ratio was something like 37:1. Many paleo folks are too low from supplementing heroic amounts of o3 while avoiding o6 like the plague.

Fasting
Sun.

### yewwei.tan — 2013-08-15T03:38:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-92585>

Interesting. One thing I wonder is how quickly cold adaptation comes to someone that lean (assuming Brick Fit House is not yet cold adapted)

I think I've heard that lean people don't have the capacity for a high rate of brown fat generation (because they don't have much white fat to begin with). Someone who is lean would then have to rely on muscular shivering while getting to a cold adapted state (muscles being able to utility fat, and body temp rising in response to cold).

One thing I wonder is whether or not the rise in testosterone (or maintenence of testosterone in face of severe dieting) is a chronic "side effect" to the cold, perhaps as a result of reducing systemic inflammation, increasing metabolic rate, etc ...

Some questions for you @Danco3636 if you'd be so kind:

1\. Did you start out cold adaptation when you were already 6-7% body fat?
2\. How long did your cold adaptation take?
3\. Do you notice lots of shivering thermogenesis during your CT sessions?
4\. If (1) is true, is it easier to maintain that low level of body fat today?

### Danco3636 — 2013-08-16T20:19:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-92819>

> [Brick Fit House said:](/goto/post?id=92630)
>
> Thanks for the insight, Danco. At 6-7% body fat, the changes is leanness are probably readily noticeable when you alter something. Has anything in particular stood out for either that or muscle growth? Like a "Damn, ever since I started \_\_\_\_\_\_\_ I'm much more shredded" kind of thing?
>
> Also, suppose you decided to get as lean as possible with as much muscle mass as possible, athletic benefits aside. Drawing on your experience and knowledge, would you do anything different than you're doing now? And why?

I find with CT and fasting it is easy to shed some fat.... I just up the CT a bit and do a 24 hour fast once or twice a week or a smaller daily fast.... skip breakfast or just have BP coffee and I can drop fat quickly...... With CT adaption I find my strength and hormones fair better.... Before when getting lean I would get aches, joint pains and irritable.... I am able to maintain 6-7% year round..... I can drop to 5% fairly easy with fasting and CT or added calorie control. When getting too lean your body wants to fight it so I find some calorie control is in order.... Sometimes with aggressive CT my hunger will spike up later on so I just eat more fat and control calories a bit..... but 6-7% on PPP - epi-paleo - 100% keto is pretty easy....... I find the longer I stay lean the lower the set point is.... I think there is an adaption that occurs where the body finds out that it is ok to be lean..... As long as nutrition is in check.....

My advice is when getting into the single digits take it slow at first...... Let the body adapt.....

CT seems to help maintain muscle mass when dropping body fat..... big time.... That and with heavy intense lifting with a minimalist approach. Not chronic daily lifting.... Any cardio would be walking or MovNat type of movement..... connecting outdoors/ grounding.

I did leangains before to get down to 5-6% BF but didn't feel as good as I do doing it with PPP - path......

Now it is just a natural result of doing things right. I am not a bodybuilder but I like to play and be lean and muscular......

Good luck and let us know how it comes out....

### Danco3636 — 2013-08-22T00:13:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-93372>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=93364)
>
> Have you ever counted calories or did you just have gut feeling of how much you should eat to lose weight?

I have counted calories and it works.... I go by feel now and add-in some extra fasting and CT as needed....

I think some accountability is needed when trying to reach body fat levels that would be a bit lower than normal or natural with a epi-paleo diet or any diet..... But CT - fasting & keto goes a long ways in cutting fat.

I do find when in the lower range of BF CT will drive hunger up a bit.... If I eat as hungry it may lay down some extra fat.... I feel that it's just CT just burns the fat so well that the body will react with wanting to replace it.... and more so in the lower range of BF.... For the over-weight or upper body fat ranges it isn't sensed as a threat and the body sheds the fat and CT kills the hunger. Going lower, this is where a bit of accountability and control of calories may be needed.... Remember when you start getting down in the lower range of the single digits of BF our hormones and signals take a hit.... Take is slow and let the body adapt.... Teach it that it is ok to be in the lower range......

That is my experience.... I am on a 40+ hour fast now doing some extra CT....

### sjoshua — 2013-08-22T02:52:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-93380>

> [Brick Fit House said:](/goto/post?id=92630)
>
> Also, suppose you decided to get as lean as possible with as much muscle mass as possible, athletic benefits aside. Drawing on your experience and knowledge, would you do anything different than you're doing now? And why?

Mentioned by Danco as well: leangains.com (or rippedbody.jp; uses same ideas but a little more simplified and easier to read). That is the best strategy I have ever used to increase muscle while staying very lean... I followed it for ~3 months at the end of last year, and put on ~5 lbs muscle while maintaining <8%. Wasn't specifically testing BF%, but that was the best lean:muscle ratio I've ever achieved in my life.

But I share the same sentiment as Danco... I feel much better without the carbs and effortlessly stay <8%. But that's not what you're asking

Currently I eat:
~60% of meat is GF beef
~25% of meat is seafood
~15% other
minimal carbs outside of cruciferous veg; occasional 80+% dark chocolate and then carbs from nuts (almonds, pistachios, walnuts, cashews) I consume.

I do:
\-Daily cold showers
\-Daily intermittent fasting (~20-22 hours; one evening meal when it is convenient)
\-Since March my workouts have been body weight incl. push/pull/chin ups, climbing trees, sprinting through grassy fields, hiking, doing as much as possible to have fun in nature... and commuting to work by bicycle when my lady needs the car (1-5x/week; 18 mi round trip) and other evening/weekend rides (~20 mi/wk).
\-I did ocean swims @ Santa Cruz ~12 times since April or May (roughly once/week as possible with schedule... 45 mins away)... I wish it was practical to do this daily (future goal...)

@Danco, the more I see your posts, the more similar I realize we are living. Kinda fun, and cool to think we naturally came to similar conclusions after experimenting

### yewwei.tan — 2013-08-22T04:48:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-93387>

Very interesting! I've always wondered how really lean people perform with CT.

Some questions to @sjoshua and @danco if you guys wouldn't mind replying:

1.  would you say that you're completely cold adapted now? (body temp goes up after CT)
2.  if so, how long did it take you to cold adapt given how lean you were when you started CT?
3.  do you notice lots of shivering with CT even today?

I've personally used leangains to get to sub 10% body fat last year, but ended up having poor cold tolerance and a maintenance caloric intake of around 2200kcal at 76kg (168lbs) body weight. Maintaining that weight required lots of effort for sure, and after 6 months of meticulous calorie counting, I decided to eat myself back up to the 13-15% body fat range (83kg now) to let my body recover.

I introduced CT (daily baths) and a ketogenic diet after, for close to 4 weeks now, and metabolism is definitely humming along right now -- not counting calories, but definitely above 3000kcal a day. I'm not yet cold adapted yet though

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-22T05:51:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-93389>

If want to gain happiness you must risk getting cut wide open by life. Risk being rejected. All of the great ones do. With regards to the lean CTer's.......one of the few pearls you will find with deep CT in a lean person is the effect of CT on the intracellular water chemistry.......the effect on free T3 and is nothing short of amazing. T3 inhibits CREB/CRE signaling pathways and suppresses inflammatory cytokine expression (NF kappa beta and IL-6) and VSMCs proliferation. This is why it is a great marker for real risk of the EMF field........it is quite a good indicator. It has been observed that people with similar serum levels of thyroid hormones can have very different thyroid hormone levels in tissues. ([http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16174716](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16174716) fig 1 and 2) Deiodinases can **_activate or inactivate_** thyroid hormones inside the cell, and therefore serum levels of thyroid hormones “do not faithfully reflect thyroid hormone signaling in cells.”[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3715916/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3715916/) and this one.....[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18815314/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18815314/)

Inside the cell, colder temperatures cause the switch of inactivation or activation of deiodinases.......this means what happens at the receptor level is completely independent of the serum levels. This is what makes CT real special in a semiconductive liquid plasma. CT changes bonding angles and network oscillations to increase energy transfers in all liquid crystalline networks. All receptors become supersensitive.

The lower free T3 is the more inflammation is in the system.......this is why low free T3 levels and diabetics always walk together. It is also why a low free T 3 correlates almost 100% with outcomes from CABG surgeries.......it is a very close measure of true energy balance in a person who is warm adapted. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23295181](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23295181)
A low resting metabolic rate is associated with metabolic syndrome and high delta psi on the inner mitochondrial membrane........and then we have people like Rosedale telling people to aspire to a low free T3. Craziness if you ask me.
See warm adapted humans believe their thyroid function is normal when they get in range labs back........but they are not. free T3 is probably one of the most accurate labs in a warm adapted mammal to their real EMF field risk. What they have is a true central "unrecognized" hypothyroidism.......and today this is believed to be normal.......because it is common and seen by endocrinologists in trials.......but just because something is common does not mean it is exactly correct. It means it is common for a reason.........it is a field effect. And this gives you a false sense of security. This is why a patients symptoms should trump a battery of tests when they are all put in context.

> Currently I eat:
> ~60% of meat is GF beef
> ~25% of meat is seafood
> ~15% other
> minimal carbs outside of cruciferous veg; occasional 80+% dark chocolate and then carbs from nuts (almonds, pistachios, walnuts, cashews) I consume.
>
> I do:
> \-Daily cold showers
> \-Daily intermittent fasting (~20-22 hours; one evening meal when it is convenient)
> \-Since March my workouts have been body weight incl. push/pull/chin ups, climbing trees, sprinting through grassy fields, hiking, doing as much as possible to have fun in nature... and commuting to work by bicycle when my lady needs the car (1-5x/week; 18 mi round trip) and other evening/weekend rides (~20 mi/wk).
> \-I did ocean swims @ Santa Cruz ~12 times since April or May (roughly once/week as possible with schedule... 45 mins away)... I wish it was practical to do this daily (future goal...)
>
> @Danco, the more I see your posts, the more similar I realize we are living. Kinda fun, and cool to think we naturally came to similar conclusions after experimenting

Santa Cruz! I was just there this month with the family... I live in Sacramento and go there oftern. Love the ocean swims in the 57 degree water. I went swimming out there with the young life-guards out there training.... They handle the cold well too. My son and I were hitting the waves by the boardwalk.... funny because I stayed out there a bit to long and had the shivers like crazy!!!! I lost track of time it was so much fun, got out and was cold to the core!

I too love movement...... and nature.... the similarities are cool! Great path to be on!

Here is what I am playing around with now:

Somethings I have been working on through a minimalist approach.
Been doing a hack with good results of 5 minutes sauna followed up with 5 minute cold shower post strength training. Then followed up with grounding and sunshine. Then my post workout nutrition 1-2 hours post fasted workout consists of a protein rich meal containing oysters, other seafood and good fats.

### DLO — 2016-10-14T16:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-for-bodybuilders.7904/post-202796>

For those of us doing the Body by Science Big 5 without a nautilus machine this is a amazing hack IMO. Helps get the cam/curve of muscle strength peak like those machines do but without taking up alot of space or cost.
[http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2016/10/14/resistance-band-exercises.aspx](http://fitness.mercola.com/sites/fitness/archive/2016/10/14/resistance-band-exercises.aspx)
**Reverse Banding Exercises**
In his article, Rusin also shares a reverse banding method that allows you to bench press heavier loads. The reverse banding involves attaching thick resistance bands to the top of your power rack. You then hang the barbell on the bands before adding weights to the barbell. For safety, make sure you're using extra-thick bands and that they're safely secured to the rack and barbell in such a way that they will not slip off.

_"By strapping bands to the top of a power rack and essentially hanging the barbell off the bands before adding plates to the bar, we utilize the accommodating resistance strength curve in a different way. Now, instead of the using a lower standard load for a movement, we are able to use a heavier load as the band helps you more as you reach the bottom of a range of motion,"_ Rusin explains_._

_"Let's take the bench press for example. Say you could bench 200 pounds for_ \[five\] _reps. If you add pro-mini resistance bands to each side of the bar in a traditional banded setup, you may have to drop that standard load down to 185 pounds plus the bands to get_ \[five\] _reps in. But for the reverse band setup, you may be able to use 225 pounds plus reverse bands and get again the same_ \[five\] _reps. This allows us to go heavy, but protect the weakest link that is usually the bottom of a range of motion."_

​

# Thread: Nathan's Journey to Optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 125
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Nathan — 2013-08-15T06:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-92599>

Hello fellow travelers! So I got some feedback that it would be a good idea to start a journal and after reading a few others' (I'm half way through Martin's) I think it will be really cool to track my progress and look back to see how far I've made it.

**A little background**
I've been lucky to have good health for most of my life. That was until 8 or so years ago. That's when I started a gradual downhill decline. Very slow at first but it was more and more apparent as the years drove on. Occasional back pain turned into daily back pain, occasional restless nights became the norm, and then fatigue, brain fog, depression, confusion....

I would go for my daily checkups and my Dr. whom I had been going to for years would test my blood for the standard markers and always give me a clean bill of health. Looking back I think I just went along with what he said for the most part because I didn't want to admit to myself that something might be wrong. As things got worse I started to question more and my Dr. just chalked it up to me getting older. I'm 42 now so this was in my mid to late 30s and I wasn't buying into that.

I really didn't think I should be having the issues I was so started doing more and more research and eventually ended up seeing an integrative specialist who diagnosed me with Candida, Lyme, and heavy metal poisoning (mercury).

I started treatment in July 2012 so it's been just over a year. Things definitely got a lot worse before they started getting better. As first I didn't know what I was in for. I never really knew much about Lyme other than you get it from ticks. I figured I would take some meds and be better in a few months. Now I know better.

I made pretty good progress until late November 2012. That's when I gave myself permission to indulge and enjoy Thanksgiving. I had been alcohol and sugar free and the only carbs I had came from whole grains. But a few drinks and desserts over Thanksgiving weekend became more and more during the month of December with holiday parties in full swing. Then NYE and I had a vacation planned for the first week of February so I put off going back to my Lyme diet until after that. A full week of indulgence did me in and I paid for it for the next month. I was completely run down and feeling worse than I had in many months.

After a visit to my doctor she made me promise to go on the straight and narrow and I did. I was still eating whole grains though and thought I was doing the right thing.

**My transformation**
I started getting better again after a month or so but I still couldn't gain any weight (went from 170 to 145 at one point) and my sleep was still terrible. While searching for ways to fix my sleep I came across the Leptin Rx and Cold Thermogenesis. I had heard of a paleo diet and never bought into it but after reading JK I decided to give it a try. This was a major leap for me.

You see I was raised my entire life being told brown rice is the end all be all for good health. My father is a macrobiotic counselor so that was my belief. But grains were just going through me and I couldn't gain any weight and I did notice I felt better on weekends when I would allow myself to indulge in a nice steak so I figured what the heck.

Now 3 weeks into the Leptin Rx and I'm feeling much better. I can't believe I am finally getting full and staying content. Before I would have a huge plate of grains and veg for dinner and be hungry 2 hours later. Now I can actually make it through the whole day without eating lunch. And I started gaining weight. That has now reversed now that I'm doing CT baths so I need to work on that.

Sleep has improved a little and I look forward to it getting better.

I had a visit with my Lyme doc today and had her give me scripts for a bunch of new labs (hormones, HS-CRP, alpha MSH, and some others). She had retested my for heavy metals and my mercury is down to a normal range so that was very good to hear.

Everyone has been great on this site and I appreciate all the great advice I have received so far.

I'm just reading away trying to suck up as much knowledge as I can. Currently up to EMF 4, am halfway through Epi-Paleo Rx and halfway through Martin's journey. Lots more reading to do.

Going to the beach this weekend and looking forward to soaking up some electrons.

### caroline — 2013-08-15T06:45:07+0300

I am so glad you decided to start a journal Nathan.......I look forward to following your progress on your journey to optimal!

Have a great time at the beach ...soak up the sun and swim and build a sand castle ..... just because you can

maybe eat some oysters too!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-15T14:17:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-92628>

nice.............We all want results. If we work hard but not smart, the bottom line will suffer. It is your job to make sure our efforts and results are in alignment. The costs of your efforts should never exceed your results on a consistent basis. If they do, something is drastically amiss. Whatever we hold in our mind will tend to occur in our lives. If we continue to believe as we have always believed, we will continue to act as we have always acted. If we continue to act as we have always acted, we will continue to get what we have always gotten. The definition of insanity is allowing the same trend to occur month after month and expecting a different result. If we want different results in our lives from our work, all we have to do is change our attitude and our mind about our perspective and results. **Don't mistake thinking for action & don't mistake action for results**......it might cost you a fortune.

### Nathan — 2013-08-20T05:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-93195>

Back from the beach and I had a wonderful time. I did a lot of grounding, soaked up a lot of sun, and hung out in the ocean for a good bit. I also had a ton of shrimp, oysters, and fish so got my share of electrons. Was actually hoping for colder water. I googled the temp and it was around 70. Will have to go back in the fall to do some CT in the ocean. I actually slept pretty well both nights I was there. Back home last night and sleep was so so. Definitely improving although slower than I would like.

I did CT last night and it had been a few days and I did it again tonight. I seem to be loosing my tolerance for the cold. I was almost up to 30 minutes before and struggled to make it to 20 minutes tonight. Perhaps because I missed a few days? Well, back at it for now and hopefully my tolerance goes up fast.

The Leptin Rx has been going well and I've gained a few more pounds, which is a very good thing because I've been really underweight. I had to stop wearing my wedding band sometime ago because I got so skinny it would slide right off my finger.

Anyway, moving right along...

Yes Dr. Kruse, the beach is where it's at...no doubt. Can't wait to go back!

Thanks Martin. Read your story and glad to see all the progress you have made. How have you been feeling lately?

I've been feeling good and slightly frustrated. Feeling good about my Leptin Rx and CT and somewhat helpless and frustrated reading about EMFs. Just finished EMF 5 and dealing with the reality that I am in an EMF hot zone here in the DC metro area. My office building has wifi throughout so finally understand why I need to drink water constantly and am always thirsty. Will be looking into an electrician to install a kill switch in my upstairs. Have also been turning off my wifi when we don't need it but I still pick up wifi from a few neighbors. So I'll continue to research on further ways to mitigate EMFs and do the best I can.

I came to a realization the other day. A few years back before I knew I had Lyme and was just trying different things to feel better I took up meditation. I tried it for a year or so and was never good at it. I had a hard time turning off my mind and it just never clicked for me. Now I know why. I had meditated in my office...next to my pc that was always on and my wifi...so right at emf ground zero in my home. Mayhaps it's time to try again but grounded this time and away from wifi.

A question. I have had nights sweats twice within a week after doing CT. I used to get night sweats on and off and sometimes quite frequently however it's been some time since I've had them. Could CT have triggered these from releasing toxins into my body and that was my body's way of getting rid of them?

Anyway...continuing with CT and made it 20 minutes in the tub tonight. Hoping to break 30 minutes soon.

### Nathan — 2013-08-24T20:43:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-93627>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=93471)
>
> Have you read the iodine thread Nathan? and please listen to Jack's latest podcast....He goes into great depth about EMF's....all the stuff he has talked about repeatedly and written in his EMF series and the bootcamp....it is easier to wrap your head around when he is speaking about it....

Caroline, not yet but will add to the list. Currently on EMF 7. Just fascinating reading...but scary stuff these emfs...a part of me is like wtf...bury your head back in the sand...I don't want to know anymore! But I read on and except the reality of our situation. Really changes the way I look at things. A few years ago my sister went to nursing school and did really well and then after completing school decided she didn't want to pursue a career in nursing. I could never understand why but now I see the wisdom in her choice whether she realized it or not. She too has Lyme disease and I can only imagine what being around all of those additional emfs would have done to her health.

I've listened to some of JK's podcasts on emfs and have made some steps in the right direction. I now have grounds for my wife's and my laptops, got her a grounding strap for when she walks our dog. I go barefoot for now and am working through some blisters getting my bare feet used to it. I turn off our wifi when not in use, have unplugged numerous devices around the house. I have some electronic frequency devices to scare off rodents that I unplugged. I'm labeling my circuit breaker this weekend so I know which ones to kill at night.

Reading these blogs gives me the feeling I have a front row seat to scientific discovery on the level of Einstein and Darwin. Did Einstein and Darwin's contemporaries realize what they were on to at the time or did it take some years for mainstream acceptance? I never knew more than the basics around either but my interest has certainly piqued. This stuff is starting to sink in...although slower than I would like. As soon as I finish reading it all I'm going to reread because I know I'm missing a ton. I am having more and more ah-hah moments and my brain hasn't had to work this hard in a while.

Made it 30 minutes in the tub today doing CT. I chilled my chest first with my ice block and that seemed to make it a little easier. I started shivering pretty hard about 20 minutes in. I was reading emf 7 and my hand started shaking too much to read so I did deep breathing and focused on how my body felt and it helped and I was able to stop shivering. It's a cool sensation. My body itself doesn't feel that cold when I focus on my core. I also took my arms and feet out and that helped as well. Will have to work up to keeping my arms and feet in.

My hands and feet have always been cold, especially in the winter. My hands get cold super easy and when they get cold in the winter they ache. Has anyone who has experienced this had it go away once you cold adapt?

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-08-24T21:39:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-93635>

Nathan, try soaking your hands in ice water. I just started again after a long hiatus. The water filled w/ cubes was 35 degrees. The first several times I soaked a hand, I only lasted 15 seconds. I took several breaks of a minute or so, and then tried again. After 3 or 4 times, it was much easier. The next day I easily reached my goal of 4 minutes on the 1st try.

Try the same thing w/ your feet. Last winter I soaked both feet in 36 degree water for 60 minutes. 1st minute or so was tough, but the rest was pretty easy.

Bottom line ... try conditioning your hands and feet .... once you can handle ice soaking them, CT w/ hands and feet in the water should be much easier.

Got a few labs back. My MSH is still low but it increased to 14 from 3.2 so it's heading in the right direction.  I asked my doctor to do a HS-CRP and is doesn't look like it was done. I think because my doctor asked why I wanted it and I said to check my Leptin so I think she decided to order a Leptin Serum test instead and it came back at <.5 ng/ml. **Is it possible to have low Leptin and be Leptin resistant?**

She did an HLA and the results indicate I'm at risk for mold illness so recommends an ERMI and MARCoNS. I don't think I have mold in my house but better to be safe. Was quoted a cost of ~$290. **Can anyone recommend a quality mold test that is cheaper?**

I took a VCS test and failed. Will ask my doctor for CSM and Actos and will get more consistent taking my activated charcoal and chlorella to help with my detox.

I had night sweats again last night. I think CT is causing me to detox and then I'm sweating it out at night. Sleep has been worse lately

I did a hormone saliva test over the weekend so interested to see those results.

Sticking with the Rx and carrying on.

### freesia — 2013-08-27T05:53:24+0300

Well done on the a-MSH increase Nathan.
Thats a big deal!
I feel sure you're on the right track with these protocols.

> My hands and feet have always been cold, especially in the winter. My hands get cold super easy and when they get cold in the winter they ache. Has anyone who has experienced this had it go away once you cold adapt?

I started CT in April last year (Fall here) and my hands were running warm by mid-June or so.
I always, always had cold hands before. I hated Winter!

I've never put my feet, hands or forearms in the ice water, initially because I wasn't brave enough! In time I realised those bits were adapting (feeling warm at cold exposure) along with the rest of me anyway. It's winter here now and my feet warm in response to walking on very cold wet grass or tiles.
My husband puts his hands in the bathwater for 15 mins after I've finished - yes strange practices go on in this house and I hardly notice anymore! - and he's adapted them gradually to 44 F or so with no issues.

Hope you'll continue to see great progress.

### Nathan — 2013-10-08T05:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-97247>

I've been slacking on my journal. After the forum was down for a few weeks I just got out of sync. Time for some updates. Let's see what is new.

After reading the past few Brain Gut blog posts and learning of the extensive damage that antibiotics do to your gut I decided to stop taking antibiotics. It's been over a year that I've been on antibiotics and after reading that a week of antibiotics can give you a leaky gut for up to 9 months I can't imagine what a year has done to my gut. My doctor was pulsing me and after my last visit she said after my next cycle to go two weeks without antibiotics. I didn't notice any big changes from being off of antibiotics for two weeks so I decided that my body needs a break. Instead, I'm taking 5 drops of some very strong oregano oil twice a day to keep any additional bacteria at bay.

To help fix my gut I'm going heavy on natural probiotics and eating lots of sauerkraut, miso soup, and kimchi, and even some occasional natto. It's been about a month since I've been off antibiotics and I'm feeling better overall. In fact my sleep has shown some improvement.

Sleep has continued to be my most stubborn issue. I decided to get a Magnetico mattress to help with this and I've had it for a little over 3 weeks now. The first two nights I slept pretty well and I thought wow, I can finally sleep...but then I had a week of less than optimal sleep. The past week and a half has been pretty good. I still wake up once or twice during the night but I have been waking up feeling better rested than I have in a very long time. I've been dreaming a lot more too. Changes are happening.

I got some new tests back and my testosterone was very low at 216 ng/dl. I need to find my old lab for T but this is a substantial drop from before. Definitely not what any man wants to hear that his testosterone is in the dumper. This was another reason I decided to stop taking antibiotics. Not sure why my testosterone would drop so much when it was in a more normal range before. My doctor put my on Androgel and I've been applying that for the past few days. Even before the test I knew my T was low. Certain parts not working the way they should and that has not really been an issue in the past. I've also has long periods of zero motivation, which is unusual for me.

Cold Thermogenesis seems to boost it temporarily in the middle of the night. Nothing like waking up aroused and having to urinate really bad. Seems that's the only time it has worked lately. I suppose it my body's way of coming back into balance but we'll see.

I have continued to gain a few more pounds and can finally wear my wedding band again without fear of loosing it. People are starting to comment that I'm looking better. My face is slowly filling out and my youth is slowly coming back. I need a new passport but I've been putting it off because I've hated pictures of myself lately and I don't want to be stuck with a picture of me looking terrible for the next 10 years. I think I'll be ready for that picture again soon.

My wife is not doing CT but has been doing most of the Leptin Rx and the weight has been melting off of her. She's been working really hard and lost a bunch of weight over the past several months and then plateaued for a while and then she lost 12 pounds in 6 weeks on the Leptin Rx. She's sold. Still trying to convince her to do CT.

I have been doing CT pretty regularly but progress has been slow. The longest I have gone has been 36 minutes in 55 degrees. It had been a few days and I finally did it tonight but barely made 30. Not sure what was up. It's working though and I'm sticking with it. I used to work out a lot and was really ripped. I haven't worked out in many months but a buddy of mine saw me without a shirt on the other day and asked what I was doing for my workouts. I told him ice baths and he laughed and said seriously, what am I doing? I told him ice baths again and it's clear he doesn't believe me. I wouldn't have either a few months ago...

I just sent my parents Dr. Kruse's book and my mother is really excited about trying out the Leptin Rx. She thinks she may be able to get my stepfather on board too. Very exciting. I just gave my in-laws a copy and am hoping they get on board. I saw some family friends over the weekend and the husband of the woman I saw had cancer and is in remission. I asked how he was doing and she said good and was telling me just got into a vegan diet. I told her to NOOOOO and to get Dr. Kruse's book to him immediately before he does too much damage to himself and I think I convinced her. She noted how much better I looked than the last time she saw me and I'm sure she picked up my zeal for my new way of life.

That's all for now. Hope everyone else is doing well out there in Kruseland!

### freesia — 2013-10-10T11:30:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nathans-journey-to-optimal.7907/post-97421>

Hi Nathan,

Re the two weeks - it took me way longer. I started with low body temperature and I have long term ME/CFS, so these issues may account for extra hurdles in the cold hardening department? Plenty of people take longer so don't get discouraged!
Anyway, just to give you an alternative to a 2 week adaptation  it was about 5 weeks of icebaths every 2 days until I was getting regular signs I was adapting - i.e. I switched to non shivering thermogenesis and got the inner furnace burning reliably. I had to use heat on a few occasions when I hadn't warmed up after too many hours shivering.

I didn't feel adapted until maybe 3 or 4 months in. At that point I could comfortable tolerate daily baths in the low - mid 40s, had a normal body temperatureirst time all day (first time since pre adolescence!) and I stopped getting temperature fluctuations at night. In due course I became more heat tolerant too....nice bonus.

Best

I got an email for the sleep webinar so I will definitely check that out and I look forward to it. I'll be upgrading to gold soon for the sleep webinar and will check out the others as well. I have listened to the EMF, CT, and Autism webinars. All great information.

That is so great that you are close to the ocean. Nice to always have a good source for grounding. I walk barefoot when I can but I'm not sure how much good it does close to my house with power lines and pavement everywhere. Can't wait to see the ocean again.

Thank you Freesia! So what other benefits have you gained from your cold adaption? Do you still have symptoms from your ME/CFS? That is some really cold water! The lowest I can get my tub is 53. I'll be getting a water cooling system soon though so I can get my water colder.

I saw some dude running in the rain yesterday and it was about 58F out. I wanted to stop and ask him if he was trying to cold adapt. I guess that's another way to do it. I took my dog for a stroll today after my CT session and it was about 63 out and felt great. By the time I got done I was wishing it was down in the 50s. It will be soon enough.

All the best!
