### MeghanK — 2013-08-29T03:22:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-around-the-mid-section-from-ct.7978/post-94126>

**Practical Implications**,.... **I know many are not a fan of Stephan Guynet.. but thoughts?**

Understanding the underlying biology can often help us understand how best to apply techniques that are based on biological processes. Here are a few points of informed speculation that I'll offer to people who are interested in cold training for fat loss or performance.

1\. You don't have to lower your core temperature to activate brown fat. The brain activates brown fat in response to perceived threats to future core temperature, including a rapid drop in skin temperature. This is why cold showers probably activate brown fat strongly, even when the exposure time isn't sufficient to lower core temperature significantly.

2\. Simply revving up your brown fat for brief periods may not make much of a difference in calorie balance or fat loss. Like muscle tissue, what burns calories is using brown fat, not just having the capacity to use it. Resting metabolic rate for muscle and brown fat tissue are both low, despite both tissues having the capacity to use a huge amount of fuel when activated. You can have a highly trained non-shivering thermogenesis system, but if you don't use it for more than a few minutes a week, it may not make much difference in your body weight. Still, having a highly trained system does make you more resistant to cold stress, which may be advantageous, and may also have metabolic benefits. Also, it remains possible that brown fat activation has neuroendocrine effects on body fatness and metabolism that go beyond calorie use.

3\. The sensors that detect temperature for the purposes of activating brown fat are not, to my knowledge, located in brown fat itself (3). Some people have used ice packs for cold training. There's probably no advantage to putting ice on your upper back, where brown fat is located, relative to any other patch of skin on your torso. In fact, I might actually avoid putting ice on my upper back because the more you cool your brown fat, the slower uncoupling will proceed. Ice may also be a weaker stimulus than cold water due to the smaller surface area of skin it targets. Water doesn't actually have to be that cold to activate non-shivering thermogenesis, due to its high conductance.

4\. If you don't feel uncomfortable at all, it probably isn't doing anything. When the brain perceives a thermal threat, it initiates a coordinated physiological and behavioral program designed to oppose core temperature change. Part of that program includes activating brown fat, and part of it involves making you feel uncomfortable so you'll hurry up and put on a sweater or stop standing in a cold shower and wasting energy.

To illustrate that principle with an example, if you're overheated and you get into a cold shower, it feels good. Cold water doesn't activate your brown fat in that scenario, because it's helping you achieve thermal homeostasis, not posing a threat to it. Likewise, some people have found ways to feel more comfortable during cold training, such as keeping extremities warm. Since feeling uncomfortable is part of the program that the brain turns on when it perceives a cold threat, I wonder if these strategies make cold training less effective by reducing the degree of brown fat activation. You may not need to feel very uncomfortable to activate brown fat, but if you don't feel uncomfortable at all then I question whether you're having the desired effect.

I look forward to research on these questions so we can have more definitive answers. Any additional thoughts on cold training are welcome in the comments.

### MeghanK — 2013-08-29T03:23:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-around-the-mid-section-from-ct.7978/post-94127>

**Practical Implications**,.... **I know many are not a fan of Stephan Guynet.. but thoughts?**

Understanding the underlying biology can often help us understand how best to apply techniques that are based on biological processes. Here are a few points of informed speculation that I'll offer to people who are interested in cold training for fat loss or performance.

1\. You don't have to lower your core temperature to activate brown fat. The brain activates brown fat in response to perceived threats to future core temperature, including a rapid drop in skin temperature. This is why cold showers probably activate brown fat strongly, even when the exposure time isn't sufficient to lower core temperature significantly.

2\. Simply revving up your brown fat for brief periods may not make much of a difference in calorie balance or fat loss. Like muscle tissue, what burns calories is using brown fat, not just having the capacity to use it. Resting metabolic rate for muscle and brown fat tissue are both low, despite both tissues having the capacity to use a huge amount of fuel when activated. You can have a highly trained non-shivering thermogenesis system, but if you don't use it for more than a few minutes a week, it may not make much difference in your body weight. Still, having a highly trained system does make you more resistant to cold stress, which may be advantageous, and may also have metabolic benefits. Also, it remains possible that brown fat activation has neuroendocrine effects on body fatness and metabolism that go beyond calorie use.

3\. The sensors that detect temperature for the purposes of activating brown fat are not, to my knowledge, located in brown fat itself (3). Some people have used ice packs for cold training. There's probably no advantage to putting ice on your upper back, where brown fat is located, relative to any other patch of skin on your torso. In fact, I might actually avoid putting ice on my upper back because the more you cool your brown fat, the slower uncoupling will proceed. Ice may also be a weaker stimulus than cold water due to the smaller surface area of skin it targets. Water doesn't actually have to be that cold to activate non-shivering thermogenesis, due to its high conductance.

4\. If you don't feel uncomfortable at all, it probably isn't doing anything. When the brain perceives a thermal threat, it initiates a coordinated physiological and behavioral program designed to oppose core temperature change. Part of that program includes activating brown fat, and part of it involves making you feel uncomfortable so you'll hurry up and put on a sweater or stop standing in a cold shower and wasting energy.

To illustrate that principle with an example, if you're overheated and you get into a cold shower, it feels good. Cold water doesn't activate your brown fat in that scenario, because it's helping you achieve thermal homeostasis, not posing a threat to it. Likewise, some people have found ways to feel more comfortable during cold training, such as keeping extremities warm. Since feeling uncomfortable is part of the program that the brain turns on when it perceives a cold threat, I wonder if these strategies make cold training less effective by reducing the degree of brown fat activation. You may not need to feel very uncomfortable to activate brown fat, but if you don't feel uncomfortable at all then I question whether you're having the desired effect.

I look forward to research on these questions so we can have more definitive answers. Any additional thoughts on cold training are welcome in the comments.

### Hemming — 2013-08-29T20:52:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-around-the-mid-section-from-ct.7978/post-94230>

@freesia, good to hear your story too.

I have to say that my previous slightly negative response to CT has been reversed lately and my apparent fat was more likely some bloating that lasted for some days. I think my stomach looks better now.

# Thread: Be careful of what you wish for, you just might get it
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/be-careful-of-what-you-wish-for-you-just-might-get-it.7979/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2013-08-22T21:18:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/be-careful-of-what-you-wish-for-you-just-might-get-it.7979/post-93422>

I've been seriously thinking about moving out of San Francisco since the EMF series came out. As Dr. K refers to cities like SF as "EMF soup" or an "EMF tsunami," I've been motivated to find a new home. We've done a lot of looking at places outside of the city but, I have to admit, I've been a little reluctant as it's been hard for me to imagine moving away from my close proximity to the ocean. Right now I live only 4 minutes away from the most spectacular little beach where I swim and earth and watch dolphins and seals play to the backdrop of the Golden Gate Bridge and the Marin Headlands. Recently I discovered Alpine Lake and Mt Tam (a 20 minute drive) which have hiking trails with streams and water falls. Truly spectacular. For a former City Girl like me, these discoveries have been mind altering. See, I've lived in the SF Bay Area since 1986 and never took off my high heels long enough to discover the lusciousness of my surroundings -- that is, until now.

Well, be careful of what you ask for. Last week my boyfriend accepted an offer for a position in a city just outside of Portland, Oregon. I'm going to keep my place in SF for a while, but do plan on spending large chunks of time up there in the trees. Although they call this part of Portland the "Silicon Forest" I'm sure living in a house with a yard where I can earth all day long will be a big improvement over living in an apartment in SF. I'm super curious to see how much better I'm going to feel in a less altered field.

Besides generally feeling better and watching the weight around my tummy disappear, I'm wondering if anyone has any suggestions for experimentation?

# Thread: Trinity's Journey to Optimal Health
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Trinity — 2013-08-22T22:08:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93425>

Trinity's Story: Part 1

My story begins with my conception because I've come to understand that some of the conditions that predispose us to a life-long battle with obesity are already in place before we are ever conceived. My mother was severely leptin resistent, clinically hypothyroid, and morbidly obese when I was born. I don't blame her for my problems. She was as much a victim of her environment as I am of mine. I do, however, aknowledge that her health issues probably affected my metabolic development.

I was a chubby child... Not hugely obese by any means, but I was bigger than most of the other girls in my classes. I remember becoming aware that I was "fat" in the first grade. As it so happens, this was also the age that I attempted my first "diet". My early efforts at dieting weren't particularly successful and it almost goes without saying that I went on to become an obese teenager. Being an obese teen was tough and my self confidence was quite low during this time in my life. I tried lots of diets, but they never really worked... I would yo-yo up and down 10 pounds or so, never loosing enough to achieve a healthy weight. I didn't understood what was wrong with me. My diet didn't appear to be any worse than my skinny friends. In fact, many of my our meals were home cooked and regularly included vegetables.

I remained obese until I began college at 18. Around this time, I remember my mom telling me about a new diet that my uncle was trying. It was the Atkins diet. I thought it sounded really interesting because it allowed you to eat most of the foods (red meat, cheese, ect.) that I had always been told to avoid. I decided to give it a try. It worked marvelously. I was finally loosing weight without feeling like I was starving to death. Within a year and a half, I had lost somewhere around 70 pounds and achieved a normal, healthy body weight of 135 pounds on my 5'5" frame. In many ways, this success probably marked the beginning of my decline in health.

As Dr. Kruse has stated, low carb diets are great for taking off fat... But they are not so great at keeping it off long term. You see, when you become obese, not only do you increase the size of your fat cells... You also increase the number of fat cells in your body. Once you have too many fat cells, you are in big trouble if you ever hope to loose a significant amount of fat and keep it off. The problem arises from the interaction between fat cell volume and leptin signaling. Larger fat cells produce disproportionately more leptin than small fat cells. When you loose weight, your fat cells become smaller but they don't disappear. Most importantly, smaller fat cells produce less leptin.

It is well established in the research literature that dieting causes a drop in circulating leptin levels. Furthermore, this drop in leptin causes a "starvation response" that results in physiological stress, increased hunger, and an overall metabolic slowing. According to researchers,

"studies strongly suggest that the weight reduced state may be regarded as a condition of relative leptin insufficiency and that prevention of weight regain might be achievable by reversing this leptin insufficient state".

Indeed, this is the biological mechanism that underpins weight regain amoung successful dieters. Furthermore, it also appears that this diminished leptin response is mostly irreversible. Essentially the successful dieter's body fat stores become spread out over a large number of starving fat cells, none of which produce sufficient leptin to maintain normal weight and metabolic function. As a result, the research unfortunately suggests that a formly obese person will never regain the metabolic function of a non obese person.

As a formerly chubby kid, I suspect that I was probably starting out with more fat cells than the average person. The problem got worse as I grew up and gained more weight. The low carb diet was successful in reducing the size of those fat cells, but not in reducing their number. The ensuing leptin signaling issues likely made my determination to permanently maintain my 70 pound weight loss a recipe for disaster.

It quickly became clear that I was going to have to exercise to keep the weight off. For the first time in my life I felt good about my body and I was determined that I was NEVER EVER going to go back to being a fat person. I was willing to do anything not to gain the weight back so I took up exercise with a vengance, running and lifting heavy weights 1-2 hours per day, 6x per week. This worked for a while. A low carb diet in combination with heavy exercise actually kept most of the weight off for another 5-7 years... But little did I know. I was wearing my body out fast!

### Trinity — 2013-08-22T22:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93426>

Trinity's Story: Part 2

Having enjoyed resonably OK health most of my life, I didn't understand what was happening when I started to experience a host of strange symptoms including extreme fatigue, insomnia, unstable blood sugar, irregular cycles, hair loss, freezing cold extremities, mood swings, and depression. I could tell that the mood swings and depression were strongly correlated to my cycle. It got to the point that I wasn't sure who I was anymore. I seemed to be two different people depending on what time of the month it was... One was "normal me" and the other was some mean, angry, depressed monster that seemed to come out of nowhere. I was in grad school at the time and the fatigue eventually became so bad I started curling up on the floor under the desk in my office. It was the fatigue that finally drove me to see a doctor.

Of course, the findings were largely unremarkable according to the doctor. That is, if you consider an enlarged thyroid, thyroid hormones at the bottom of the reference range, sky high cortisol, and menopausal levels estrogen and progesterone "normal" for a 26 year old. His advice was "watchful waiting" on the thyroid, a prescription for synthetic "birth control pills" to regulate my cycle, and a suggestion that I consider an anti-depressant and counsellor for the mood and fatigue issues. I said no thanks to the anti-depressant and shrink. Although I felt depressed, there was absolutely nothing going on in my life to explain the feelings. They were clearly tied to my cycle and I was pretty sure that the menopausal levels of sex hormones were to blame.

This doctor visit ushered in an era of ever worsening mental and physical health. I saw several doctors during this time and I came to fear the humiliation of being told over and over that there was nothing wrong with me except for what was "in my head". Eventually, I stopped seeing doctors.

It was the insidious and inexorable weight re-gain despite increasingly aggressive efforts to control it through diet and exercise that finally convinced me to try again. I was convinced that I had a thyroid problem. I had a family history (mom is hypothyroid), all of the clinical symptoms, an ultrasound showing an enlarged thyroid gland, and labwork going back 5 years showing low thyroid hormone levels. After much research, I settled on a doctor that was more holistically oriented and known for using natural dessicated thyroid supplementation. Being a bit more open-minded, it was not hard to convince him that I had a thyroid issue. I walked away with a prescription for dessicated thyroid.

For the first month I felt much better. The fatigue lifted. My mood was better. This must be the answer! Unfortunately, I soon ran into a period of stress surrounding a move to a new house... The stress of the move combined with thyroid hormones and marginal adrenal status (unknown to me) was the straw that "broke the camel's back" and resulted in my first "adrenal crash". I had no idea what was happening. My entire autonomic nervous system destabilized with wild swings in heart rate and blood pressure that led to panic attacks. My circadean rhythm was destroyed as I started waking like clockwork every morning at 3AM with adrenaline rushes burning through my abdomen. My muscles became chronically tight and painful now matter how much I tried to stretch them out. My stress tolerance evaporated. Even a ringing phone would startle me to the point I would start shaking and feel like I was going to collapse. I discontinued the thyroid medication, but the symptoms never went away.

It took me some time and research to understand what had happened. My cortisol, thyroid, and sex hormones were tanked. My circadean rhythm was destroyed. After this turning point, I literally went a whole year unable to sleep more than 3-4 hours per night. I could barely function in my life or work and I had next to no support from my family who seemed convinced that I was a neurotic hypochondriac. I was, however, extremely fortunate to have a partner that stood by me and believed in my the whole way. Because he lived with me, I didn't have to convince him that I had a physical problem. It was him that finally encouraged me to find a good doctor and get help.

### Trinity — 2013-08-22T22:20:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93427>

Trinity's Story: Part 3

Approximately 5 years from the point this nightmare began, I found a good endocrinologist. I was pre-diabetic, my progesterone was non existant, my thyroid was in bad shape with low free t3 and reverse t3 elevated more than double the reference range, and my adrenals were shot with low cortisol and an upside down rhythm. I began my slow climb to health with prescriptions for bioidentical progesterone, supplemental cortef, and cytomel. I also found out that I was gluten intolerant with low iron and a Vitamin D level a mere step above "rickets".

By this point, I had given up on weight loss or exercise. I had to conserve what little energy I had just to function minimally at my job... I ate a reaonably healthy, low glycemic diet (could no longer tolerate extremely low carb), but the weight and fat just piled on. I finally accepted that I was going to be fat and there was nothing I could do about it. Being able to have enough energy to function at my job took precedence over what clothing size I wore.

Over a period of about two years, I recovered some of my former health. Though still no where near optimal, I got my iron, vitamin-d, and progesterone up, improved my thyroid function, and stabilized my nervous system through a combination of cortef and daily neurofeedback / meditation. I felt mostly "OK", but still had little sense of well being or productive energy. Despite better thyroid function, my metabolism was still shot. Eventually, I tried to loose weight again by eating less and getting back in the gym only to have it backfire on me. According to before and after DEXA scans, I actually lost muscle and gained fat from eating less and working out! To my extreme horror, I was now 51% bodyfat. I wasn't "obese". I wasn't even "morbidly obese". I was "super obese"; that special category for those unfortunate individuals with a bodyfat percentage over 50%. And somehow I had gotten there on a reasonably low glycemic, albeit SAD diet, that was mostly free of sugar and junk food!

Beyond my severe obesity problem, the other thing that never significantly improved was my sleep. Even with the Cortef, my ASI continued to show an upside down rhythm. Being quite analytical and research minded, I started doing regular adrenal stress tests and testing various doses and timings of Cortef supplementation in effort to fix this problem once and for all. No matter what I did, I could not eliminate the upward trend at night that was causing my chronic insomnia. After a number of these tests, I started to suspect that Circadean rhythm might be independent of the daytime cortisol concentration.

It was while researching Circadean rhythm that I first ran into Dr. Kruse's website. He presented an entirely unique perspective and I began to finally understand how obesity, leptin, dopamine, and circadean rhythm are all inter-related. I finally understood that some of the conditions that predispose us to obesity are in place before we are born. I finally understood how some of the physiological factors related to fat cell volume and leptin signaling cause dieters to develop hormonal problems, become depressed, and regain lost weight. I was not a personal failure because I had these problems! I had made an honest efort to do the best I could with the knowledge I had at the time, but the cards had been stacked against me, maybe even from birth!

All of a sudden, my life made a lot more sense  The knowledge I gained from Dr. K's amazing blogs and this forum actually released me from a burden of guilt and sense of personal failure. Best of all, it has offered me hope and the possiblity of meaningfully improving my health, permanently conquering my obesity, and generally living a more optimal life.

In PART 4, I will describe my new, more optimal plan to recover my health and some of the positive results I've seen so far.

### Trinity — 2013-08-22T22:42:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93432>

Trinity's Story: Part 4

I began my journey to Optimal health in January of this year. While I still have a ways to go, I have made progress accross the board.

C/T has been a huge focus of mine. I will have to post pics of my CT setup sometime cause it beats the crap out of hauling ice around

In fact, I just posted elsewhere on this forum about my massive increase in IGF-1 (growth hormone) from regular CT. Although I do not have a baseline from the very beginning, over the past 4-5 months of cycloset + regular CT, my IGF-1 levels have increased by more than 100 points. In fact, I am now above the reference range at 373 ng/mL (range 53-331). From what I've researched, this is quite high for someone my age (34 years old). My endocrinologist said that he does not see levels that high very often in his patients and was quite interested in my CT protocol. Apparently Dr. K got the same results (i.e. a 100+ increase in IGF-1 levels) from his CT experiment.

I am in this CT game for the long haul. As a morbidly obese person, I fully understand the absolute necessity of CT and the need to induce fat cell apoptosis. Without permanently destroying a significant number of my fat cells, long term fat loss will be physiologically impossible to sustain. I know this from first had experience because it was the substaantial weight loss earlier in my life that severely damaged my endocrine system. I kept the weight off for several years through sheer force of will combined with unhealthy dieting and aggressive exercise... But in the end my self discipline backfired on me and hormonal havoc ensued. Despite my best efforts, I gained all of the weight back and then some along with a host of new problems including adrenal dysfunction, low DHEA, massively elevated reverse t3, trashed progesterone, pre-diabetes, and a destroyed circadian rhythm. I have spent what were supposed to be the best and most productive years of my life trying to recover my health with little assistance and a great deal of bitterness towards the conventional medical establishment.

In addition to the massive increase in IGF-1 I mentioned, I have also seen other improvements from CT that support everything Dr K discusses in CT-6:

1) My t3 levels have improved over the past few months \*without\* increasing my cytomel dose

2) I have managed to get off of Cortef after almost 2 years on it. My cortisol is still not optimal, but its getting better. Sleep still isn't perfect either but my chronic insomnia has noticeably improved in response to CT, rigorously minding the light cycles, and now a magnetico pad.

3) I am noticing that I have become quite resistant to weight gain for the first time ever! In fact, I just bought pants 1 size smaller because my old pants were starting to look a little sloppy. I have only lost 4-5 pounds so I am guessing that there must be some muscle gain occurring in addition to fat loss. The truly amazing thing is that this has happened without any calorie restriction or increase in exercise!

Conventional wisdom's admonishment to restrict calories and exercise myself to death is simply not part of my plan to conquer my obesity anymore... That road led me away from health rather than towards it... And as they say, stupidity is defined as doing the same thing over and over expecting different results...

My new strategy is three-fold:

1) Use CT to work towards long term fat cell apoptosis: I actually invested in a commercial grade water chiller that I have hooked up to a submersible pump sitting in my bath tub. It is on a timer and starts chilling my water about an hour and a half before I get home from work. So far it has worked really great because it has allowed me to control and very gradually reduce the temperature as I have acclimated. It will cool down to 37 degrees and that is where I'm heading over the next few months (currently at 42 degrees).

2) Reduce Inflammation and Improve Mitochondrial Function: Although CT helps with this, I am also supplementing with Tumeric (Meriva), poly-resveratrol, CoQ10, Magnesium, D-Ribose, and am working on improving my dietary omega-3 ratio. I also recently purchased a Magnetico core pad. I have become aware that morbid obesity is a highly inflammatory state and CT-6 really confirmed my need to reduce inflammation when it talked about the inflammatory factors blocking leptin from binding to the receptor.

3) Work on improving circadian rhythm: To this end, I make a point to consistently get light exposure first thing in the AM and wear my blue blockers after 7pm every night. I am also consistently in bed by 10PM and sleep in complete darkness thanks to heavy duty light blocking curtains and a sleep mask. On the weekends, I make an effort to spend a few hours outside in the sun during the morning or early afternoon.

After reading CT-6 at Dr. K's suggestion, it appears that my results might be even better if I adopt a ketogenic diet. I am currently eating paleo and plan to gradually move in the ketogenic direction as the fall and winter approach. I have taken the carb reduction a bit slow because past efforts to go fully ketogenic have really backfired in terms of the effect upon my mood and energy. I now understand that this is because I have not been able to burn fat very efficiently. With my broken metabolism I have no idea how long converting to an efficient fat burner might take. I am currently planning to use D-ribose to temporarily fill in the gap as I attempt to make the adjustment. It is interesting that I have still gotten some of the benefits from the cold pathway without being fully ketogenic. The benefits I've seen so far are definitely motivating me to keep moving more towards optimal!

Trinity

### Trinity — 2013-08-23T17:36:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93509>

In case anyone is interested, here is my CT setup. It beats the crap out of hauling ice and was cheaper than the commerical ice makers that I priced out. It consists of:

HP EcoPlus commercial grade water chiller (1/2 HP)
EcoPlus Eco 1056 Submersible Pump 1083GPH
10-20 ft of 1 inch tubing

I have it on a surge protector and timer set to come on about an hour and a half before I get home from work. Because I got the bigger 1/2 HP model, it takes less than 2 hours to cool my bathtub down to ~42 degrees. Most of the time I really look forward to my CT sessions. Strange as it sounds to most people (this forum excluded  ), I find CT quite relaxing. It is also an opportunity for me to take some quiet time and catch up on reading on my kindle (housed in a waterproof case, of course  ). It has taken me 7 months to work my way down to 42 degree baths, but it has been a completely painless process. Because I can precisely control the temperature, I have simply adjusted the thermostat down a degree or two every week. Likely due to an extremely slow acclimation, I haven't shivered at any point during the process. There tends to be a mild adjustment period of 5 minutes or so when I first get in before I am 100% comfortable, but no shivering. I have actually observed that my body temperature seems to rise and maintain between 99-100 degrees throughout the bath. Even 30-40 minutes into the bath, I am often still holding around 99 degrees. I also meaure my skin temp and have found that it is currently running about 52 degrees in the 42 degree water. When I get out there is a warm up period of about an hour, but I am rarely uncomfortably cold. The only exception is if I lay down and stop moving around. I find that I need to do my CT at least 2 hours before bed to avoid going to bed uncomfortably cold. Warming up has gotten progressively easier though. I actually remember being much colder after completing a 70 degree bath several months ago than I am today after a 42 degree bath. I guess I can finally consider myself cold adapted

### Trinity — 2013-08-24T02:27:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93568>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=93550)
>
> Oh Trinity, your story sound just like mine except you are a little farther in the healing process then I am.
>
> This line really hit home for me "I was not a personal failure because I had these problems! I had made an honest efort to do the best I could with the knowledge I had at the time, but the cards had been stacked against me, maybe even from birth!" That is a hard one to get over, seeing yourself as being a failure.

Hi Nicole,

Yeah, that one has taken me many years to start moving past. I think in our society we are conditioned to think we are a failure if we are obese. There is a perceived stigma that we lack self control. After all, if you are obese it MUST be because you sit on your ass all day watching TV and eating Twinkies, right? If you can't loose weight on a diet, it MUST be because you are lying to yourself about how much you eat, right? Or maybe you just sabotage yourself because you're an "emotional eater"?

Realizing that I honestly HAVE done the best with the knowledge I had and the cards that I was dealt has been liberating. In my late teens, I knew next to nothing about metabolism or hormones. I didn't yet have the background or the motivation to dig through clinical research studies. Given my circumstances, I can't blame myself because there is nothing I could have reasonably done to cause things to turn out differently. And as much as I've struggled with my health over the past few years and in spite of the resulting not so infrequent "self pity parties", I have to admit that the experience has also forced me to "grow up" in some ways that I might not have otherwise. I've become more compassionate and less judgmental of other people. I've also realized that I'm not a quitter and that I'm actually a hell of a lot stronger than I ever realized or thought when I was younger.

I do, however, still struggle with a lot of feelings of bitterness and resentment towards the medical profession and, to some extent, toward my family for their lack of support towards me. When these feelings come up I keep trying to remind myself that they didn't (and mostly still don't) know any better. They were (and mostly still are) just as much a prisoner to "conventional wisdom" as I was earlier in my life... But completely letting go of that anger and bitterness is something that I still struggle with...

As Dr. K is fond of saying, "now that I know better, I will do better"  I told my mom recently that my goal was to be able to say "I feel great!" (and truly mean it) when someone asks me how I'm doing!

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=93550)
>
> I too read my kindle in the bath. I just made a board to go across my tub so I can set my kindle on it with a stand and it work great. It also allow my new kittens to cross the water too and sit and watch me...hahaha.
>
> Wishing you lots of success with this and know that we are on this journey together.
> Nicole

Haha! I have cats too, but they're not kittens anymore. My 20lb maine coon is quite fond of water too

### nicld — 2013-08-24T03:52:14+0300

That is exactly how I feel. I have tried everything I knew how to do but still could not "succeed". I ate right and exercised myself to a disorder. I am a little pissed at some of the medical professional I saw. I had one tell me no woman should eat more than 1400 calories no matter how much they workout and another who told me that concentration camp survivors came out of there fairly healthy, WTF!!!!

I just got 2 new kittens a couple of week ago (Sully and Mr. C Matthews, brothers) after we had to put one down due to old age and my cat got killed by something when she went outside. My kittens are intrigued with the water but don't want to come in. Now my nieces cat that I get her for Christmas TAKES a bath with her. Gets soaking wet at Jade dumps water on her.

I am telling you ladies...we are 3 peas in a pod! I swear I could have written these words...lol! Chubby kid, I remember in the 80's my mom had these diet caramels called Ayds..I loved them, size 12-14 in high school until senior year...was put on prozac and birth control and ballooned to over 200. A little phen fen got me down to 170's, a college waitressing job and tequila diet got me down to 145. Add wellbutrin and trazadone and bi polar diagnosis put me over 250 at age 24, got married at 25 at 165....had a baby, and another and another....havent seen the 100's since 2004.

I got very ill and recovered from things people often dont. I have stopped dogging myself about my weight....I have moved medical mountains and no longer blame myself for being fat...it is the end result of a big ass mess and i am working on it. I still have to work out my hormone issues and some gut issues....but it is happening....slowly

I want to punch people in the throat when they tell me I should try weight watchers or nutrisystem. And I loathe that they look at me like I must sit on the couch with a box of donuts every day....but I will get beyond this and amaze them all.

I am learning so much from you both....and I have begged my husband to buy me that water cooler....great idea!

Cant wait to hear more

### Jack Kruse — 2013-08-24T15:18:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93598>

> [Trinity said:](/goto/post?id=93425)
>
> Trinity's Story: Part 1
>
> My story begins with my conception because I've come to understand that some of the conditions that predispose us to a life-long battle with obesity are already in place before we are ever conceived. My mother was severely leptin resistent, clinically hypothyroid, and morbidly obese when I was born. I don't blame her for my problems. She was as much a victim of her environment as I am of mine. I do, however, aknowledge that her health issues probably affected my metabolic development.
>
> I was a chubby child... Not hugely obese by any means, but I was bigger than most of the other girls in my classes. I remember becoming aware that I was "fat" in the first grade. As it so happens, this was also the age that I attempted my first "diet". My early efforts at dieting weren't particularly successful and it almost goes without saying that I went on to become an obese teenager. Being an obese teen was tough and my self confidence was quite low during this time in my life. I tried lots of diets, but they never really worked... I would yo-yo up and down 10 pounds or so, never loosing enough to achieve a healthy weight. I didn't understood what was wrong with me. My diet didn't appear to be any worse than my skinny friends. In fact, many of my our meals were home cooked and regularly included vegetables.
>
> I remained obese until I began college at 18. Around this time, I remember my mom telling me about a new diet that my uncle was trying. It was the Atkins diet. I thought it sounded really interesting because it allowed you to eat most of the foods (red meat, cheese, ect.) that I had always been told to avoid. I decided to give it a try. It worked marvelously. I was finally loosing weight without feeling like I was starving to death. Within a year and a half, I had lost somewhere around 70 pounds and achieved a normal, healthy body weight of 135 pounds on my 5'5" frame. In many ways, this success probably marked the beginning of my decline in health.
>
> As Dr. Kruse has stated, low carb diets are great for taking off fat... But they are not so great at keeping it off long term. You see, when you become obese, not only do you increase the size of your fat cells... You also increase the number of fat cells in your body. Once you have too many fat cells, you are in big trouble if you ever hope to loose a significant amount of fat and keep it off. The problem arises from the interaction between fat cell volume and leptin signaling. Larger fat cells produce disproportionately more leptin than small fat cells. When you loose weight, your fat cells become smaller but they don't disappear. Most importantly, smaller fat cells produce less leptin.
>
> It is well established in the research literature that dieting causes a drop in circulating leptin levels. Furthermore, this drop in leptin causes a "starvation response" that results in physiological stress, increased hunger, and an overall metabolic slowing. According to researchers,
>
> "studies strongly suggest that the weight reduced state may be regarded as a condition of relative leptin insufficiency and that prevention of weight regain might be achievable by reversing this leptin insufficient state".
>
> Indeed, this is the biological mechanism that underpins weight regain amoung successful dieters. Furthermore, it also appears that this diminished leptin response is mostly irreversible. Essentially the successful dieter's body fat stores become spread out over a large number of starving fat cells, none of which produce sufficient leptin to maintain normal weight and metabolic function. As a result, the research unfortunately suggests that a formly obese person will never regain the metabolic function of a non obese person.
>
> As a formerly chubby kid, I suspect that I was probably starting out with more fat cells than the average person. The problem got worse as I grew up and gained more weight. The low carb diet was successful in reducing the size of those fat cells, but not in reducing their number. The ensuing leptin signaling issues likely made my determination to permanently maintain my 70 pound weight loss a recipe for disaster.
>
> It quickly became clear that I was going to have to exercise to keep the weight off. For the first time in my life I felt good about my body and I was determined that I was NEVER EVER going to go back to being a fat person. I was willing to do anything not to gain the weight back so I took up exercise with a vengance, running and lifting heavy weights 1-2 hours per day, 6x per week. This worked for a while. A low carb diet in combination with heavy exercise actually kept most of the weight off for another 5-7 years... But little did I know. I was wearing my body out fast!

This is precisely why the obese need CT........CT is the only way to make adipocytes undergo apoptosis permanently

### Trinity — 2013-08-25T16:58:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trinitys-journey-to-optimal-health.7980/post-93702>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=93598)
>
> This is precisely why the obese need CT........CT is the only way to make adipocytes undergo apoptosis permanently

Yup, that is why CT has been and will continue to be a huge focus in my journey back to health.

For anyone else that is obese and interested in why they might have found fat loss virtually impossible to maintain through diet and exercise, this link explains it pretty well. The problem is biology, NOT a lack of willpower!

[http://kindkehealthnotes.blogspot.com/2012/10/too-many-fat-cells.html](http://kindkehealthnotes.blogspot.com/2012/10/too-many-fat-cells.html)

I feel like this is an extremely important message that needs to be heard and understood if you are obese. The conventional wisdom approach of eat less and exercise more won't do much more than trash your metabolism and hormone panel over the long term if you don't address the adipocyte hyperplasia problem. The only way to address this problem permanently appears to be cold or liposuction (and lipo probably isn't a viable option if you carry fat all over your body as I do).

Trinity

Because I can precisely control the temperature, I have simply adjusted the thermostat down a degree or two every week. I also meaure my skin temp and have found that it is currently running about 52 degrees in the 42 degree water. When I get out there is a warm up period of about an hour, but I am rarely uncomfortably cold. The only exception is if I lay down and stop moving around. I find that I need to do my CT at least 2 hours before bed to avoid going to bed uncomfortably cold. Warming up has gotten progressively easier though. ### carys — 2013-08-23T15:48:07+0300

Hi y'all - could be a very stupid thread ahead but thought I'd ask

A) Thanks Dr. K for providing this forum as a means to communicate

B) Being very obese and having been tested for LR - it is confirmed to be so. I have tried a number of attempts to get below the a certain weight range and I bang my head on the wall - I simply CANNOT! Being born a 12 lb .10 Oz baby I must have come out LR. However - there is no doubt as I aged I ate terribly. I take responsibility for my physical state.

I workout weekly - 3 times a week weight lifting at 45 mins and bike 3 times a week of 8 miles each time.

I moved to a vegetarian diet 1 year ago in hopes of losing some weight - but mainly to feel better.

I do not have Type 2 Diabetes, I am not Hypo, I do not have HBP, I do not have inflammation physically in my legs/ arms/ fingers etc.

I just cannot get this weight to come off. Now then - being vegetarian I have moved to a very low calorie diet - in turn I may screwed up my metabolism. I lost 20lbs then the honey moon was over.

I had to work out about 3 hrs a day to get 20ls off.

sorry I digress -

I know this approach is paleo - why would being vegetarian not work?

Theres my stupid question

### caroline — 2013-08-24T01:07:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/obese-but-vegetarian.7997/post-93556>

Welcome Carys. Dr. K's book will give you a great overview of all this in an easy way to understand. Please consider starting a journal so we can all follow your progress - it really does help everyone! Commit to this journey 100% and you will see amazing changes in your life ....without question. It is not a short trip ...it is for life.... and you will reap huge rewards.

Please listen to Dr. K's podcasts - He is very easy to listen too and we are able to connect the dots a little faster. I definitely have a non scientific brain \[understatement here!\] but I do get the overall picture and I love trying to figure it all out  His last podcast would be a great place for you to start.

Please start the leptin- reset ....kind of put your brain in neutral for a bit - and trust the process.
Keep reading and asking questions .... believe me - we have all been there.

Have a read of Trinity's journal - she just started it and follow along - she has amazing insight into all of this and is doing amazing with C/T.

Make no mistake tho - the weight isn't going to drop off by next week ....we would all love that

for me ....I went up a few pounds when I started the reset \[this often happens with women - sad to say\] but in a couple of months I was down 2 sizes ....and a year later I am down 4 sizes .and about fifteen pounds - but the very most important thing is how healthy I am ....I feel great .... and I wish that for you

As Dr. Kruse said to you ...controlling the EMF's in your life is critical - that is job #1

# Thread: Another DHEA question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-dhea-question.8006/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2013-08-23T23:20:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-dhea-question.8006/post-93551>

I started taking DHEA because in the readings that I was doing and the symptoms that I was having it seemed like I was low and supplementing would help. I had a blood serum DHEA that showed I was low on DHEA and I was on about 25-50mg a day so I upped the dose to 50 mg 2X a day. I just had a saliva test done and had stopped the DHEA 2 days before I had the test (from some stuff I read it should have been longer but the instructions did not say that). I got the saliva results back and it said that I was high on DHEA and that my testosterone was near the top. Still struggling to decide which one I should believe, but that is another post.

Since I started taking the DHEA I have steadily gained weight and the biggest thing is starting getting painful acne on my face. Since I do not know which test to believe and I have stopped the DHEA gradually. As soon as I reduced/stopped, the acne stopped, coincidence? I am just about to ovulate so we will see what the luteal phase brings. I see a doc on Monday and hope that he can make sense of what is going on and if I really need the DHEA.

Should note that my progesterone is tanked and the thyroid is tanked. I think that the low progesterone is dragging everything else down. I do not have PCOS either and my cortisol levels appear to be fine.

Until I get the progesterone fixed is trying to fix the others, specifically the DHEA just a waste of time?

Am I on the wrong kind of DHEA?

I am still doing the leptin reset and the CT but have been gaining weight and not sleeping great the whole time I was doing them.

Anybody have any insights to this?

Thanks

### fitness@home — 2013-08-24T00:29:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-dhea-question.8006/post-93554>

I think Shijin can answer your question best...

I am post menopausal and use Twist DHEA cream. My DHEA and Testosterone are both on the high end. Acne is not an issue...

### Trinity — 2013-08-24T02:34:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-dhea-question.8006/post-93569>

Hi Nicole,

You are lucky that your cortisol is still OK. That will make fixing your thyroid a lot easier! I would start with progesterone if I were you. Fixing the progesterone is key and is likely the cause of your poor sleep. Is your sleep worse the week or so before your period? My sleep tanks like clockwork on day 18 of my cycle (right when progesterone should be at its highest). With bioidentical progesteone its much better, but still not optimal. It has only been within the past couple of months that I've finally got my E ratio up around 200 (optimal is somewhere around 300). Everyone is a little different, but it has taken 300mg of progesterone just to bring it up to where its at now.

Trinity

### nicld — 2013-08-24T03:30:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-dhea-question.8006/post-93573>

Hi Trinity,

I was really surprised when I got the cortisol test back and it was pretty normal. 7.2 in the morning then drops off during the day like it should. I know about 7-8 years ago I fried the adrenals so at least that is one thing I don't have to worry about.

I see the doc on Monday, ovulation day so I am hoping that I get a script right away and the second half of the cycle will not be as bad. Right now, my ratio is 38 around my high day. No wonder I feel like crap. From what I have read, estrogen dominance wreaks havoc with the thyroid. Tanking the T3 and making the Rt3 to T3 ratio bad too (mine is 10.95 when 20 and above is better).

Thanks fitness@home. I just cannot handle the acne, it sucks.

# Thread: Magnesium calcium and shark fin supplement
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-calcium-and-shark-fin-supplement.8008/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2013-08-24T15:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-calcium-and-shark-fin-supplement.8008/post-93600>

As money has been tight recently I have not bought any new magnesium. Ideally I would like to put that off for another 2 weeks or so. I do still have a mag,calcium and shark fin mix from a time when I thought that was a good idea.
How bad would it be to take that before bedtime ? I think the lack of additional magnesium is affecting my sleep .
Alternatively I could do foot baths with Epsom salts, but I wonder if the absorption rate is enough.
(Magnesium oxide 470mg ,calcium carbonate 255mg)
Thank you.

### caroline — 2013-08-25T02:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-calcium-and-shark-fin-supplement.8008/post-93661>

I don't feel that mag helps me sleep better.....I have been taking threonate in the morning and malate in the afternoon. Now it takes me forever to fall asleep - once I do - I sleep soundly. I think it is because the seasons are changing but I don't know what to do about it....

Well actually .... Brandy works - but that is such a bad idea....

### Lahelada — 2013-08-25T15:33:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnesium-calcium-and-shark-fin-supplement.8008/post-93694>

I do sleep better with mag. I did not take the mag at night after all but in the morning though. I slept well again last night 9 hours.
This explains pretty well what I was worried about with calcium acting as a stimulant. [http://www.metabolictyping.info/\_insom1.html](http://www.metabolictyping.info/_insom1.html) Well,next Friday I will try taking it at night as I don't want to disturb my pattern during the week .

# Thread: How do you feel about your body?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 44
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-08-25T10:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93680>

Do you think that women worry too much about how their body looks......

I suppose, on one hand we do, but when we are overweight - it just feels so bad, and so wrong.
We want to look hot and sexy for the guys in our life - that goes without saying .... but in my opinion - that is only a very small part.

We need to feel good about ourselves ... for ourselves. It feels so awful when your clothes are too tight - you can't get your favorite pants done up and your shirt is gaping...... I think it is kind of humiliating.

Men can just let their belt out a notch and they are good to go. I don't think that men feel as uncomfortable in their skin as women do when they are obese.

I had a friend that was a very bright, articulate, witty, lovely woman .... she was very successful with her own company.....but....she was obese. It was such a disappointment to her. She always said - she could control so much in her life - she was so successful .....but her body defied her at every turn and she felt like such a failure in that area of her life.

Does a guy feel this way too? Does a guy care if he can't wear skinny jeans! BTW - guys look so hot in skinny jeans!

I know men get that their loved one gets really distressed and angry and unhappy over the shape/size of their body.........but honestly .......and in my opinion....it isn't vanity.....it is so much more.

We really want to be the woman on the outside that reflects how we feel about ourself on the inside.
We want to be happy and healthy and actively engaged in life. We want and deserve to feel great about ourself .... and, of course, a man deserves this too.

Does anyone else feel like this? How do the guys feel?

What are your observations? Do women expend too much energy/time worried about their shape that could be far better put to more productive use?

Having said all that ..... I think that it is amazing when a woman can embrace all her curves and just go for it. Is it because she has a wonderful, caring man that makes her feel like a princess and loves her unconditionally?

and having said that ....I had all of that and more and it wasn't enough ....I needed to love myself ......I needed to feel good about myself. It had nothing to do with someone else's "perfect". It had to do with reaching my own potential and giving myself the gift of confidence and vitality and loving myself....

Sorry .....I think I am really rambling here ......

I hope that some of the guys will give us their thoughts.........

### fitness@home — 2013-08-25T14:28:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93687>

I can only speak for myself here. I have suffered from a strange affliction I like to call "Mirror Distortia" since my early teens. Over the years it didn't matter if I was underweight, normal weight, or overweight... My body's reflection never appeared to be good enough in my eyes.

Women are definitely too hard on themselves. I felt the worst about my body after the birth of each child...when that is a completely normal to time for a woman's body to have gained weight and change shape!

Another symptom of "Mirror Distotia" is when you look at old pictures of yourself and suddenly realize you actually were NOT as large as you thought. In fact, you appear to be very normal-sized...

We waste a lot of time being too hard on ourselves.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-08-25T14:31:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93688>

When a person, regardless of their sex, relies on another person's opinion, it is a very slippery slope.

When a person asks the opposite sex for an opinion about body image, that is usually a no win situation for the person being asked. (aka - see point above)

Caroline ...... you provided the answer in your post ..... "I had all of that and more and it wasn't enough ....I needed to love myself ......I needed to feel good about myself. It had nothing to do with someone else's "perfect". It had to do with reaching my own potential and giving myself the gift of confidence and vitality and loving myself" .............. That is a profound and accurate view.

### Hemming — 2013-08-25T18:07:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93707>

Yes, we definitely care about it, just look at all the guys working out in the gyms to impress the ladies with their guns. I completely agree with fitness@home, I have the same problem now of worrying too much about the mirror and my weight. When you truly love yourself you don't care about exactly how your body looks. I'm sadly not at that point right now but I'm trying to get there.

### Hope — 2013-08-25T20:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93720>

This is a beautiful thread. I used to literally hate my body. Going through and dumping a lot of stuff to declutter and create a better space in our home, I came across an old calendar from 2000, where I'd write how I feel, what exercise I did etc. I literally wrote in there how I hate my body.....etc etc. I also always felt not good enough and then a guy I dated fed into the dysfunction....it's taken a lot of internal work and mental healing (largely just getting my brain healthier period, detoxified and just working better in general), and though I have a loving DH, I could never really hear his compliments, or even those of other people. I always rejected it. It wasn't until I really started to love and accept myself at a deep level - and this is still an ongoing process - that I could really hear it, believe it and tell myself. In fact just this week I had to chuckle when my dentist called me 'thin' (which at 150 and 5'8'' I am not really yet), but I could accept it. AND I can now look into the mirror and love my body. In fact I just did that this morning trying on clothes and seeing what I wanna keep. Surely an actual tan helps too. lol.

I now believe it doesn't matter what others say - it's all about what you believe about yourself. I've studied Taoism and Chinese Medicine some, which fascinates me.....one of the tenets of it is the Earth Element which correlates to the spleen, which makes blood, gives energy, key for immunity etc in Chinese Medicine. When you are not right with yourself, and specifically if you don't stand up for yourself and know deep down your own value, and beauty, you constantly look for it in the wrong places, or you can't accept it from other people....it has to come from inside. And when that doesn't happen, you waste a lot of energy and ultimately can feel miserable physically...all of which I did and suffered with. It's so so fascinating. And I see all kinds of truly beautiful people do it too - they have a beauty that nature gave them, but they can't see it, for whatever reason....I often think of Princess Diana with this. But anyway.

I know now that I don't need to look like a movie star to feel healthy, and beautiful and good enough.....and when I do get compliments, it's like icing on the cake, a reflection of what I already know. Like at Whole Foods I was picking out veggies and one of the helpers said the other day, "you look awesome today". And I was a bit shocked, because I was wearing an old skirt, a plain old white shirt. Nothing fancy at all. But I enjoyed it.

It's definitely a new thing for me, and I am sure without the various JK protocols, the magnetico sleep pad, iodine detox and almost nightly meditation, I might not have ever gotten 'here'. It sure took me long enough, considering I just turned 40. I used to be jealous of girls that had all the goods AND confidence at far younger ages.....but now I'm OK with it. I am on my own journey......and that's what it's all about

### Inger — 2013-08-25T22:11:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93734>

Very beautiful said, Hope. Me too have suffered a lot with this. I always find the other more beautiful.. but when I am out in the wild I always feel so good. No issues with body image. And when I get enough rest and eat well and all this stuff. Then I feel so ok, even if I see I am far from perfect. It just does not matter..lol Right now I feel not so well, I miss my own privacy so much. I am far too close to others energies, that are not really building me up. This is hard for me but I need to find a way to cope. I just ned to breath once in a while, to find some lonely time. I do not even have time to read the posts here now. It will get easier when I get my first payment, now I live on others mercy and I need to be with them much as they wish so. Taking care of kids and stuff. Sure they are so lovely but still there is so much noise, kids that get fed crap is not alwasy so easy to handle for sure.. but it is not their fault. Poor kids. It hurts me to see.

Shortly said... when I have less stress and more peace in my life I feel so much better - and I look better too  I bet because I am a woman and we are so sensible beeings. IDK but all I crave is just... peace. Too much noise in the world today. Tomorrow I am going to take my bike and hide at the beach!

### nicld — 2013-08-25T22:20:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93735>

Yes, yes, yes and yes. I am hoping someday to over come body image issues. I have got better since I saw Robin Woodall but each day is somewhat of a struggle. Even though I have gained a bunch of weight this summer I am not beating myself up about it (hormone issue) and not trying to exercise the weight off or starve myself the way I have in the past.

I need to start seeing myself as the sexy beautiful woman that my husband sees me as.

### Inger — 2013-08-25T22:41:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93741>

You are very lucky to have a hubby that sees you as sexy and beautiful. My ex did not really, I fear. except when I weighed 47 kg.. way too little maybe. I except of then always felt too fat. Yeah I am not the thinnest but I am not very fat either. I really feel happy for you having such a loving husband

### caroline — 2013-08-26T02:16:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93772>

Thankyou all for sharing your thoughts and feelings.

There is something I haven't articulated yet .... I can't really work it out. Like fitness - I would look in a mirror and see an overweight person - no matter what size........

But - it is the Leptin reset that is the game changer here. Our brains are much healthier .... we are much healthier.
I now weigh less than I ever have .... but that totally is not the issue. It is the body composition that is so much better.
So - even tho I weigh less - I look so much healthier and my shape is so much better, and I am so much happier

The most important thing for me is - my outside matches how I feel on the inside. I can look at myself naked and think - hey - I look just fine.

So - our brain is now in the game ..... Dr. K. is probably thinking to himself......."it took you long enough"

Give us enough rope and we will eventually climb into the light ...........

### nicld — 2013-08-26T03:31:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93781>

Thanks Inger, I am very very lucky. He always tried to even when I was 260 pounds. We have been together since I was 15 so over 24 years. I just want to be the self confident happy wife that I deserve to be. That would make him happy fir me to love myself as much as he loves me. I have to say the past 6 months I have made great strides in self acceptance.

### caroline — 2013-08-26T03:42:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93783>

Nicole - I love what you just said. I think it is so hard on our partners when we are so unhappy about ourselves. They deserve to have a happy, confident lover....It must be so baffling to them......

### caroline — 2013-08-26T03:57:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93784>

Hope - you speak of arriving in this great place and you just turned 40 ......I think you are so lucky to have gotten here. How many never make it?
I am way older than you and I just got here!! ....... I took the slow train .....but I am here now

and it turns out it was our brain that was holding us back.

### caroline — 2013-08-26T04:06:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93785>

### caroline — 2013-08-26T04:20:50+0300

### caroline — 2013-08-26T04:38:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93790>

Light bulbs keep going off for me here......I hope that is happening for others to....

So - now that we are re wiring our brain ..... we are much happier and healthier and able to see much more objectively.
I can look at myself naked \[BTW that is a first!\] and say .... hey - you still have a lot of pudge in your middle - but that's okay.
I don't feel disgusted anymore....I seem to be really seeing myself accurately ....not someone else's idea of what is good and beautiful and not expending all the energy that it takes.

In the long run - Dr. K. is right .....I somewhat hate that he is always right

so now - imperfection is just fine ....we can move on to solving world problems...

I can't believe that I got here in just one page! The sex thread took me about 60+ pages....

### nicld — 2013-08-26T04:54:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93791>

Trying to embrace my perfect imperfections

### caroline — 2013-08-26T04:56:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93792>

When I started this thread it was because of something that Jack had said in my journal....
I didn't mean for this to be about me so much....

I really want to know how an obese man feels .....is it anywhere near the same as a woman? or is it totally different?

When an obese man, or a thin man, wants to go to bed with a woman ....does he even think about his shape/size/weight?
Does it impact him at least a little ....or are they good to go?

I guess, as JK said in the sex webinar, a woman needs a symphony........ and I don't think a man does....

sorry if I am off track ..... but it all is about how we feel about ourselves - especially when our brain isn't optimal.

### prAna303 — 2013-08-26T20:30:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93828>

Now we start to talk... Brains that share things other than just food.

Yes we do look at others with other eyes than before and i think that we have lost the feeling for being a complete human. On one side we have the ones only living for the body and the way they look and on the other side the ones that seek so called mindfulness. Today most care how they look but not in how they attract others, it is a constant catwalk but without the glamour. When did it go wrong?

If i look at myself i always hated to be present on pictures, there is not a single photo from my childhood and that makes me feel great. Today i know why i hated this so much, when we start to capture frames of our lifes we only get some small parts of what we are. I always thought i looked like a idiot because i am very physical in my prescence, a still frame is just boring and you get to look at meaningless details. I think we are not made up with a sense for frozen pictures, it is scaring because still means dead. Today everyone is snapping frames of themself eating dinner, taking a zip of java and documenting their exercise. Everything is about frames of the perfect life, this makes us unsure.

The mirrors are a man made utility, the only time we could see ourself before that was in the lake when it is still, did we get scared? Yes...

I always listen to the voice of my body, when i feel that i can not do my adventures in life something must change. The image in the mirror is not the reality. I helped one of my friends with her image of her body. She is an athlete at the highest level and she felt like shit about her body. A simple picture, photoshoped and she felt that she was amazing. The best thing was when i showed her a movie when she moves, then it clicked for her big time... She looks like a wild animal and that is beauty for me AND her now.

When we move with a purpose in life we always look wonderful.

Caroline, when you sit on the beach and look around. The things around you is so back to basic... If you look at men surfing it is the same as looking at to wild creatures fighting for the female attention. It is deep inside us.

So i think that if we try to get the both sides, body and mind we shine. When a body works with the mind in sync it is perfection, a single frame does not matter anymore, that is so boring.

### caroline — 2013-08-27T02:48:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93858>

Peter .... as always ....you nailed it. Is that what Dr. Kruse has been saying all along? When body and mind work together we can shine.....that is perfection .....how perfect to be imperfect and love ourselves all the more.

There has been a huge revelation to me lately - just the last couple of days. I had coffee with a friend at the ocean - and after I left her I realised how excited I always am around her. We talk about the craziest, off the chart stuff - it is as if I come to life around her and I now realise I am really bored around a lot of my other friends. They are smart, lovely people but I am on a different wave length, a different path that they have no concept of and have no interest in....

### cantweight — 2013-08-27T05:09:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93875>

This was an interesting read along the lines of body/mind connection and body image....

[http://www.psychologicalscience.org...tbeat-can-trigger-out-of-body-experience.html](http://www.psychologicalscience.org/index.php/news/releases/visualized-heartbeat-can-trigger-out-of-body-experience.html)

### cantweight — 2013-08-27T05:27:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93878>

I've always felt kind of weird with my body perception. I constantly forget that I am fat. When I dream I am always fit and healthy looking.... On multiple occasions I have written my weight on papers I had to fill out for Drs. as 1 something instead of 2 something. I am always shocked by pictures of myself. I look like someone swallowed me. Its kind of eerie the way I honestly forget the reality of my physical appearance.

I try not be hard on myself as I have learned too much to chastise myself for the state of my bod. I punished it enough when i was young....that really didn't serve us well. It is still in the process of cleaning up a huge mess, its has done right by me so far....I still have faith in it.

There are times when obesity smacks me upside the head. Amusement parks....Im so afraid of being over the weight limit, getting dressed for parties is hard when I hate the fat clothes I have to wear, and when other people look at me with that look....that look that says I know you sit on your couch all day, watch Oprah, and eat donuts. I hate that shit.....but I get over it....and eventually I am back to forgetting I am fat. Weird right?

### caroline — 2013-08-27T05:34:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-93880>

I totally get that ... I never felt fat on the inside - not until I looked in a mirror .... and then I couldn't understand how all that fat had attached itself to me!

You always hear stories of people who have lost weight and then regain it ....and people \[supposedly in the know\] would say it is because they don't see themselves as a thin person. What a stupid statement really.

I always thought of myself as a thin person - regardless of what my exterior looked like ....

### AnnieW — 2013-08-29T16:23:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-94157>

I like this thread but it makes me sad because of my self image. I am just gross. I'm 6' tall and almost 350 lbs. My shape is not feminine at all. I have a HUGE stomach and almost no butt or boobs. I am covered in stretch marks and have tons of body hair thanks to PCOS. I am sad to think that my skin is going to suck after I lose this weight. Since doing the leptin reset I feel pretty good inside with the exception of some GI issues which are related to me changing my diet. I am hopeful that I won't look that bad when I'm done. I'm also losing hair on my head. I hope the reset helps fix a lot of these hormonal issues.

### cantweight — 2013-08-29T16:36:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-94160>

Hang in there Annie...that good feeling you have inside will grow exponentially! You will notice things bit by bit that make you feel good....a change in skin tone, bright eyes, a healthier mouth, little things that will add up to something incredible. I am still very overweight...very very overweight. But the good feeling has spread, I am grateful to my body for healing from so much, my PCOS symptoms are minimal...the acne is less, even the hair is slower growing and seems lighter on my face...easier to keep it in its place so I dont feel like a goat anymore..lol.

My hair went from falling out to fantastic. My hairdresser couldnt believe it at my last cut. The grey is less visible for some odd reason and it is shiny and full. There is so much good to how I feel that the fat is just the last piece I am waiting for my body to figure out how to deal with.

The stretch marks will fade and Dr K has not had major issues with excess skin and I am hoping for the same. Put good thoughts in your head. Even if you dont believe them at first. Visualize your healthy, whole body and then do your best to see it through

### nonchalant — 2013-08-29T16:51:34+0300

### AnnieW — 2013-08-29T18:01:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-feel-about-your-body.8018/post-94187>

Thanks guys for the encouragement. I've read all this stuff on other forums about how you're basically screwed with loose skin so I'm trying not to get my cortisol up about it.

I thought about a nice gift to myself if and when I can reach my goal weight. I wanted to get a Tria home laser hair removal system. I'm usually very skeptical of things like that, but I read a review on PCOS diva's website and apparently it's been working for women with PCOS. They are expensive but I figured I could start that at home and then work up to getting it done professionally so I wouldn't have to spend so much money.

Thanks again for the encouragement!
